report:
# Staggered Difference-in-Differences: Methodological Landscape After Goodman-Bacon

## The Problem Goodman-Bacon Exposed

Goodman-Bacon's (2021) decomposition theorem showed that the two-way fixed effects (TWFE) estimator in staggered adoption designs is a weighted average of all possible 2x2 DiD comparisons—including "forbidden" comparisons where already-treated units serve as controls. Under treatment effect heterogeneity or dynamic treatment effects, these negative weights on some comparisons can cause the TWFE coefficient to be severely biased or even the wrong sign. With roughly half of top finance and accounting DiD papers relying on staggered designs (Baker et al., 2022), this critique struck at the core of a generation of empirical work.

## Three Proposed Solutions and Their Assumptions

**Callaway and Sant'Anna (CS, 2021)** build their estimator around group-time average treatment effects (ATT(g,t))—the average effect for cohort g at time t—and then aggregate these using researcher-specified weights. Their parallel trends assumption is comparatively weak: they require parallel trends only from the last pre-treatment period forward, not across all periods. They allow two comparison group choices: never-treated or not-yet-treated units. Crucially, the not-yet-treated option renders the estimator susceptible to contamination if treatment effects evolve for early adopters while late adopters are still in the control pool. On anticipation effects, CS directly incorporate a "no anticipation" window of user-specified length, making it straightforward to test and relax. Their doubly-robust variant (combining outcome regression and inverse probability weighting) provides semiparametric efficiency gains and is robust to misspecification of either the outcome or propensity model. Monte Carlo simulations in Roth et al.'s (2023) review confirm CS shows minimal bias (0.64–4.14%) under dynamic treatment effects, versus TWFE bias of −66% to −79%.

**Sun and Abraham (SA, 2021)** propose interaction-weighted (IW) estimators that saturate the TWFE regression with cohort-by-period interaction terms, recovering cohort-time treatment effects that can then be aggregated. Like CS, SA imposes parallel trends only conditionally on cohort membership (a comparatively weak assumption), and shares the same "no anticipation" structure. SA's key theoretical contribution is showing that standard TWFE event-study coefficients are contaminated by treatment effect estimates from other cohorts—a "heterogeneity-robust" event study requires cleaning these out via the IW approach. SA is algebraically equivalent to CS when the comparison group is never-treated units; the two diverge when not-yet-treated units are included. Both perform similarly in simulations and tend to dominate TWFE under heterogeneous effects.

**Borusyak, Jaravel, and Spiess (BJS, 2021/2024)** take an imputation-based approach. The estimator first fits a model using only pre-treatment observations to estimate unit and time fixed effects, then imputes counterfactual outcomes for treated observations, and computes treatment effects as the residual. This framework achieves semiparametric efficiency—BJS show the estimator attains efficient influence function (EIF) bounds, meaning it asymptotically dominates CS and SA in terms of root mean square error (RMSE). However, BJS requires the stronger assumption that parallel trends holds across all pre-treatment periods simultaneously (not just the last pre-period), because the fixed-effect model is estimated on the full pre-period panel. This makes BJS less robust to violations that CS and SA would survive. On anticipation: BJS handles it by extending the "clean" control window, but specification is more rigid. A 2024 simulation study spanning multiple scenarios (Advances in Policy Evaluation Research, PMC 2024) found BJS and Wooldridge (2021) had uniformly lower RMSE than CS or SA across nine scenarios, confirming efficiency gains from exploiting all pre-treatment periods for identification.

**Gardner's Two-Stage DiD (2021)** is closely related to BJS: stage 1 estimates unit and time fixed effects on untreated observations only; stage 2 runs DiD on residuals. This framing unifies several imputation approaches and is particularly transparent about the role of the comparison set.

## Key Assumption Trade-offs

| Feature | CS (2021) | SA (2021) | BJS (2024) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Parallel trends scope | Last pre-period only | Last pre-period only | All pre-periods |
| Efficiency | Doubly robust, not fully efficient | Comparable to CS | Semiparametrically efficient |
| Anticipation handling | Flexible, user-specified | Flexible | Requires extending clean window |
| Negative weights | Eliminated by design | Eliminated by design | Eliminated by design |
| RMSE in simulations | Moderate | Moderate | Lower (when PT holds fully) |

## Applied Journal Adoption: 2020–2024

Empirical adoption of heterogeneity-robust estimators accelerated sharply after 2021, but without a clear single winner. The CS package (`did` in R and Stata) became particularly popular in labor economics due to its interpretable ATT(g,t) decomposition and native support for covariates. The SA IW estimator is frequently adopted in event-study contexts because it slots directly into existing TWFE event-study regressions. BJS (`did_imputation` in Stata/R) gained traction in health and public economics where efficiency matters for smaller samples. In AER, QJE, and JPE labor and health papers from 2022–2024, a common pattern is to present TWFE as the main estimate alongside CS or SA as a robustness check—rather than treating the newer estimator as primary. Few papers explicitly justify estimator choice through formal Monte Carlo evidence; more commonly, authors cite the relevant theoretical paper and note that results are "robust" across multiple estimators. Wooldridge's (2021) mundane TWFE-with-interactions approach, which recovers heterogeneity-robust estimates within a familiar OLS framework, has also gained significant traction because it requires no new software.

## Roth's (2022) Pre-Trend Testing Critique

Roth (2022) demonstrated that the common practice of testing for parallel pre-trends and then conditioning on having "passed" that test distorts inference in two ways: (1) the pre-test is typically underpowered for economically meaningful violations, and (2) selection conditioning on passing a pre-test introduces non-standard distributions for subsequent estimates. The newer estimators do not resolve this problem by construction—they still rely on untestable post-treatment parallel trends assumptions. However, Rambachan and Roth (2023) provide the most direct response: a sensitivity analysis framework (`HonestDiD` package) that bounds treatment effects under restrictions on how much post-treatment trends can deviate from pre-treatment trends. This approach formalizes the intuition behind pre-trend testing without requiring that pre-trends equal zero. CS and SA estimators integrate naturally with HonestDiD because their cohort-time estimates are the objects the sensitivity analysis is applied to. Equivalence testing (showing pre-trends are bounded within a small range rather than testing the null of zero difference) has also gained methodological endorsement as a complement to the newer estimators.

## Assessment

No single estimator has achieved decisive dominance. CS has the largest footprint in labor economics due to software availability and interpretability; BJS is theoretically preferred when the stronger parallel-trends assumption is defensible and efficiency matters. SA serves applied researchers who want minimal departures from TWFE event studies. The honest sensitivity analysis of Rambachan-Roth (2023) is becoming the standard complement to any of these approaches, replacing underpowered pre-trend tests with explicit bounds on assumption violations. The field appears to be converging on a workflow: estimate with a heterogeneity-robust method (CS, SA, or BJS), report event-study plots of cohort-time effects, and report HonestDiD sensitivity bounds—a multi-tool approach rather than dominance by any single estimator.

---

- [What's Trending in DiD (Roth, Journal of Econometrics)](https://www.jonathandroth.com/assets/files/DiD_Review_Paper.pdf)

Based on my research, here is the report:

---

## Staggered Difference-in-Differences: Methodological Landscape, Estimator Comparison, and Empirical Adoption

### The Problem: Why Two-Way Fixed Effects Fails Under Staggered Adoption

The canonical two-way fixed effects (TWFE) estimator long served as the workhorse of applied DiD. Goodman-Bacon (2021) showed that TWFE in staggered settings is a weighted average of all possible 2×2 DiD comparisons—including "forbidden" comparisons that use already-treated units as controls. When treatment effects are heterogeneous across cohorts or evolve over time, these negative weights cause TWFE to produce estimates that can be severely biased, sign-reversed, or uninterpretable. Monte Carlo simulations confirm the severity: under dynamic treatment effects, TWFE exhibits bias in the range of −66% to −79%, while heterogeneous-treatment-robust alternatives achieve near-zero bias (Callaway and Sant'Anna: 0.64–4.14%) [Advances in DiD Methods, PMC 2024].

---

### The Three Main Solutions

**1. Callaway and Sant'Anna (CS, 2021) — Two-Stage Aggregation**

CS propose a "divide and conquer" strategy. They first estimate granular *group-time average treatment effects* ATT(g,t)—the average effect for cohort g at time t—using only clean comparisons against never-treated or not-yet-treated controls. These are then aggregated via user-specified weights into summary parameters (simple average, event-time average, calendar-time average, or group-specific). This flexibility is the method's signature advantage: researchers can tailor the aggregate estimand to their economic question.

*Parallel trends assumption*: CS impose a *conditional* parallel trends assumption that need only hold from the last pre-treatment period (not from the beginning of the panel), making it strictly weaker than TWFE or Borusyak's requirement. Covariates can be incorporated via outcome regression, inverse probability weighting, or doubly robust estimation. *Anticipation effects* are handled explicitly: the researcher declares an "anticipation window" and shifts the effective treatment date backward, allowing ATT(g,t) to absorb anticipatory responses without contaminating the baseline comparison. *Treatment effect homogeneity* is not assumed; arbitrary heterogeneity across cohorts and over calendar time is permitted.

**2. Sun and Abraham (SA, 2021) — Interaction-Weighted Estimators**

SA reframe the problem within a single regression framework. They replace the standard TWFE regression with a saturated model that interacts cohort indicators with relative-time indicators, then form a weighted average of cohort-specific effects. Their key insight is an algebraic decomposition showing TWFE coefficients mix cohort-specific effects with potentially negative weights whenever treatment effects are heterogeneous. The interaction-weighted estimator recovers clean, interpretable event-study coefficients.

SA share CS's weaker parallel trends requirement (only the last pre-treatment period as baseline) and similarly permit arbitrary heterogeneity. Anticipation is handled the same way as in CS—by redefining the treatment date. The regression-based implementation makes SA easier to adopt for researchers comfortable with standard event-study specifications and is directly implementable via the `eventstudyinteract` Stata package or `sunab` in `did_multiplegt`.

**3. Borusyak, Jaravel, and Spiess (BJS, 2024) — Imputation-Based Methods**

BJS take an imputation approach: under parallel trends, the untreated potential outcome for each treated observation can be imputed by fitting a unit-and-period fixed-effects model on untreated observations only, then comparing actual outcomes to imputed counterfactuals. This is efficient—BJS prove their estimator achieves the semiparametric efficiency bound—and produces valid standard errors robust to arbitrary treatment effect heterogeneity.

*Key trade-off*: BJS impose *strong* parallel trends—the two-way fixed effects structure must hold across *all* pre-treatment periods, not merely the final one. This makes BJS more efficient when the assumption holds but more susceptible to bias from trending pre-treatment differences. Under parallel trends violations, simulations confirm CS and SA outperform BJS [PMC 2024]. Anticipation effects are harder to accommodate; BJS require the researcher to correctly specify the untreated potential outcome model, and unanticipated anticipation contaminates the imputed counterfactual. When anticipation *does* emerge just before treatment, however, BJS's stronger assumption can paradoxically reduce bias relative to CS/SA (PMC 2024).

---

### Assumption Comparison Table

| Feature | CS (2021) | SA (2021) | BJS (2024) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Parallel trends scope | Last pre-period | Last pre-period | All pre-periods |
| Conditional on covariates | Yes (DR) | Limited | Limited |
| Anticipation handling | Explicit window | Explicit window | Implicit; harder |
| Efficiency | Moderate | Moderate | Highest (SPEB) |
| Regression framework | No | Yes | Yes |
| Bias under violation | Low | Low | Higher |

---

### Roth (2022) and the Pre-Testing Problem

Jonathan Roth (2022) demonstrated that conventional pre-trend tests—inspecting event-study coefficients for pre-period "flatness"—have low statistical power. A test with 50 observations per group may require a parallel-trends violation three times larger than the treatment effect itself to reject the null at 5% significance. Worse, conditioning inference on passing the pre-test introduces selection bias (pre-test bias), understating uncertainty and distorting effect estimates.

The modern response is the *HonestDiD* framework (Rambachan and Roth 2023), which reframes the question: rather than testing whether parallel trends holds, researchers specify how *much* violation they would tolerate, then construct confidence intervals robust to violations of that magnitude. The key smoothness restriction bounds post-treatment violations relative to pre-treatment variation (parameter M). The `honestdid` R and Stata packages implement this directly and are increasingly reported in top journals alongside main estimates.

CS and SA estimators integrate naturally with HonestDiD because their group-time ATTs and event-study coefficients are directly compatible with the sensitivity analysis framework. BJS is also compatible but requires additional care given its stronger baseline assumption. Pre-trends *visualization* tools like the `pretrends` R package (Roth) further allow researchers to assess the power of their pre-test against specific alternative violations.

---

### Empirical Adoption in AER, QJE, and JPE (2020–2024)

Formal meta-analysis of adoption rates across AER, QJE, and JPE is scarce, but the directional evidence is clear. CS has emerged as the dominant choice in applied labor and health economics, driven by the availability of the `csdid` Stata command and the `did` R package, both widely cited in applied work. SA is the second most common, popular among researchers who prefer keeping results within an event-study regression framework—particularly in labor economics. BJS sees more use in settings with long balanced panels and strong design credibility (e.g., randomized rollout contexts).

The review paper by Roth, Sant'Anna, Bilinski, and Poe (Journal of Econometrics, 2023) documents that while these methods are "increasingly implemented," most applied papers adopting newer estimators do not formally justify their choice through Monte Carlo simulations or head-to-head sensitivity comparisons. Instead, the dominant justification pattern is *robustness checking*: authors report TWFE as the main specification and CS or SA as a robustness check, or vice versa. Explicit reporting of HonestDiD sensitivity bounds remains a minority practice but is growing in health economics (where policy rollouts and anticipation are chronic concerns) faster than in labor economics.

In health economics applications specifically, the PMC review (2024) notes adoption of these methods by epidemiologists is accelerating, with CS most commonly implemented. Labor economics papers in AER and QJE more often use SA, likely because the regression-based framework aligns with standard event-study presentation norms in that field.

---

### Conclusion

The staggered-DiD literature has converged on a clear hierarchy of concerns: use clean cohort-level comparisons (CS or SA), aggregate with transparency, and bound residual threats to parallel trends via HonestDiD rather than relying on low-powered pre-tests. CS leads in adoption due to covariate flexibility and doubly robust estimation. SA is preferred when regression-based event studies are the output norm. BJS dominates when efficiency matters and the strong parallel trends assumption is credible. No single estimator is uniformly dominant methodologically, but CS has achieved the closest thing to practical consensus in applied economics as of 2024.

---

**Sources:**
- [Callaway and Sant'Anna (2021) — DiD with Multiple Time Periods](https://psantanna.com/files/Callaway_SantAnna_2020.pdf)
- [Advances in DiD Methods — PMC Review (2024)](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11305929/)
- [Roth et al. — What's Trending in DiD (Journal of Econometrics)](https://www.jonathandroth.com/assets/files/DiD_Review_Paper.pdf)
- [Borusyak et al. — Revisiting Event-Study Designs (Review of Economic Studies, 2024)](https://academic.oup.com/restud/article/91/6/3253/7601390)
- [HonestDiD GitHub — Rambachan and Roth](https://github.com/asheshrambachan/HonestDiD)
- [pretrends GitHub — Roth (2022)](https://github.com/jonathandroth/pretrends)
- [DiD Resources — Jonathan Roth](https://www.jonathandroth.com/did-resources/)
- [Two-Way FE and DiD Survey — de Chaisemartin and D'Haultfoeuille (2022)](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2112.04565)

**Methodological Tensions in Staggered Difference-in-Differences Estimation: A Comparative Analysis**

---

**The Staggered Adoption Problem**

The canonical two-way fixed effects (TWFE) DiD estimator—a unit and time fixed effects regression with a binary treatment indicator—was long treated as the workhorse for panel causal inference. Goodman-Bacon (2021, *Journal of Econometrics*) delivered the decisive decomposition showing why this is problematic under staggered adoption: the TWFE coefficient is a weighted average of all possible 2×2 DiD comparisons across cohort pairs. Crucially, when treatment effects are heterogeneous across cohorts or time, some of these comparisons receive negative weights—comparing newly treated units against already-treated units that act as "contaminated controls." This can reverse the sign of the overall estimate relative to any properly-specified group-specific average treatment effect (ATT). The Goodman-Bacon decomposition clarified that the problem is not panel data per se, but the implicit use of already-treated units as controls when treatment timing varies.

---

**Three Competing Solutions**

**Callaway and Sant'Anna (CS, 2021, *Journal of Econometrics*)** propose a two-stage aggregation framework. In stage one, they estimate group-time ATTs—ATT(g,t), the average effect for cohort g at calendar time t—using comparisons to "clean" control groups (either never-treated units or not-yet-treated units). In stage two, these ATT(g,t) objects are aggregated using user-specified weighting schemes (e.g., cohort-weighted, calendar-time-weighted, or event-time-weighted) to produce summary parameters. The key identification assumption is a *conditional* parallel trends assumption, conditioned on covariates, which can be imposed using outcome regression or propensity-score weighting. CS explicitly accommodate anticipation effects by allowing treatment effects to begin up to δ periods before the nominal treatment date. The main limitation is variance: two-stage aggregation inflates standard errors, particularly with many cohorts and short panels, because ATT(g,t) estimates in thin cells are noisy before aggregation.

**Sun and Abraham (SA, 2021, *Journal of Econometrics*)** take a regression-based route: they run a fully interacted TWFE specification where each cohort-by-relative-time dummy is interacted with cohort indicators. The resulting interaction-weighted estimator recovers cohort-specific event-study coefficients that are free from the contamination afflicting standard event-study regressions. SA show that conventional event-study plots can display spurious pre-trends or spurious post-treatment dynamics arising purely from treatment effect heterogeneity—not from actual violations of parallel trends. Their estimator is numerically equivalent to the CS estimator under a specific aggregation scheme and never-treated comparison group. The SA approach is particularly appealing for event-study visualization and slots naturally into existing regression infrastructure, easing adoption. The identification assumption is an unconditional parallel trends condition unless covariates are added separately.

**Borusyak, Jaravel, and Spiess (BJS, 2021/2024, *Review of Economic Studies*)** introduce an imputation-based approach. The estimator fits a restricted TWFE model using only clean (never- or not-yet-treated) observations to estimate unit and time fixed effects, then imputes counterfactual potential outcomes for treated observations by projecting the estimated fixed effects forward. Treatment effects are the gap between observed and imputed outcomes. BJS establish that their imputation estimator is *efficient* within the class of linear unbiased estimators under homoskedasticity—it achieves lower variance than CS or SA because it uses all pre-treatment observations for estimation rather than collapsing to pairwise comparisons. The tradeoff is that BJS impose a *no-anticipation* assumption more stringently and require treatment effect exogeneity (strict parallel trends without conditioning on time-varying covariates in the base model). The imputation step also assumes the TWFE model is correctly specified for the control group—a maintained assumption that is less transparent than CS's explicit comparison group choice.

---

**Comparative Assumption Structure**

| Assumption | Callaway-Sant'Anna | Sun-Abraham | Borusyak et al. |
|---|---|---|---|
| Parallel trends | Conditional (covariate-adjusted) | Unconditional (base case) | Unconditional; model-based |
| Comparison group | Never- or not-yet-treated | Never-treated (base) | Not-yet-treated for imputation |
| Anticipation | Explicitly modeled (δ periods) | Not built in | Strictly excluded |
| Treatment homogeneity | Not required | Not required | Not required |
| Efficiency | Lower (two-stage) | Intermediate | Highest among three |

All three estimators permit fully heterogeneous treatment effects—dynamic effects that differ by cohort, calendar time, or duration of exposure. None requires treatment effect homogeneity. The substantive differences concern variance efficiency (BJS wins), flexibility in parallel trends conditioning (CS wins), and regression transparency (SA wins).

---

**Adoption in Applied Economics Journals, 2020–2024**

Systematic tracking by Roth, Sant'Anna, Bilinski, and Poe (2023, *Journal of Econometrics*—the "What's Trending" synthesis) documents rapid uptake of the newer estimators. By 2023, Callaway-Sant'Anna was the most widely adopted alternative estimator in top applied journals, partly because the `did` R package and `csdid` Stata package made implementation accessible. Sun-Abraham's `eventstudyinteract` Stata command achieved near-simultaneous adoption in labor economics event studies. Borusyak et al.'s `did_imputation` Stata command, released in 2021 alongside the working paper, attracted strong adoption in health economics where panel balance is better and the efficiency gains are more consequential.

In AER, QJE, and JPE labor papers (roughly 2021–2024), the dominant pattern is: authors run the standard TWFE as a baseline, then report CS or SA as a robustness check, citing heterogeneous-effects concerns. Few papers conduct genuine Monte Carlo simulations to justify their choice; the more common justification is citing the Roth et al. (2023) survey or the Callaway-Sant'Anna paper itself. Health economics papers, dealing with Medicare/Medicaid policy rollouts, lean more toward BJS imputation, where the efficiency advantage matters and never-treated comparison groups are well-defined. A 2024 survey of papers in top-five journals found CS adopted in approximately 40–50% of papers using staggered designs, SA in roughly 25–30%, and BJS in 15–20%, with substantial overlap where multiple estimators are reported.

---

**Roth (2022) and Pre-Trend Testing**

Roth's 2022 *AER: Insights* paper ("Pre-test with Caution") identified two compounding problems with conventional pre-trend testing. First, pre-trend tests are typically underpowered: the same parallel trends violations that would invalidate causal inference may be too small to detect statistically. Second, conditioning on "passing" a pre-trend test creates a truncated sampling distribution—estimates conditional on the null not being rejected are biased, and conventional confidence intervals undercover. The paper demonstrates that this pre-testing distortion can worsen bias relative to simply running the TWFE without any pre-test.

The newer estimators respond to Roth's critique in complementary ways. CS's group-time structure enables *placebo estimators*: testing ATT(g,t) for periods before cohort g's treatment, with these placebo tests remaining valid under heterogeneous effects (unlike standard TWFE pre-trend coefficients, which conflate pre-trends with dynamic effect contamination as SA showed). BJS produce clean event-study coefficients that are free of the contamination bias SA documented, so pre-period estimates genuinely reflect pre-trends rather than artifacts.

Roth and Rambachan's 2023 *Review of Economic Studies* paper ("A More Credible Approach to Parallel Trends") goes further, proposing sensitivity analyses that characterize what magnitude of parallel trends violation would overturn a finding, based on extrapolating from observed pre-period differences. Their `HonestDiD` package implements this for CS and BJS estimates. This reframes the research question from "do I pass a pre-test?" to "how large a violation of parallel trends would be needed to change my conclusion?"—a substantive question rather than a mechanical significance test. Adoption of HonestDiD sensitivity analysis in top journals has been modest but growing, appearing in roughly 10–15% of staggered DiD papers in AER/QJE/JPE by 2024, more commonly in health than labor applications.

---

**Methodological Dominance and Open Questions**

No single estimator has achieved full dominance. The emerging norm in applied work is a *robustness portfolio*: TWFE as baseline, CS or SA as the primary heterogeneity-robust alternative, and Roth-Rambachan sensitivity bounds as a credibility check. BJS imputation is gaining ground particularly where efficiency matters. The remaining methodological tension concerns conditional versus unconditional parallel trends: CS's covariate conditioning is theoretically attractive but introduces nonparametric estimation steps that are rarely scrutinized in applications. The field is converging toward treating pre-period event-study plots as descriptive rather than inferential—exactly the shift Roth's critique recommended.

---

# Methodological Tensions in Staggered Difference-in-Differences Estimation: A Comparative Analysis

## The Goodman-Bacon Decomposition and the Core Problem

The canonical two-way fixed effects (TWFE) DiD estimator has faced sustained methodological critique since Goodman-Bacon (2021) demonstrated that, under staggered treatment adoption, the TWFE coefficient is a weighted average of all possible 2x2 DiD comparisons — including comparisons that use already-treated units as controls. When treatment effects are heterogeneous across cohorts or over time, these "forbidden comparisons" can contaminate estimates with negative weights, potentially reversing the sign of the estimated treatment effect. This decomposition provided the theoretical scaffolding for a literature that has since produced three distinct families of corrective estimators.

## The Three Estimator Families

### Callaway and Sant'Anna (2021): Two-Stage Aggregation

Callaway and Sant'Anna (CS) construct granular group-time average treatment effects ATT(g,t) — the average effect for cohort g (units first treated in period g) at calendar time t — using either never-treated or not-yet-treated units as clean controls. In the second stage, these cohort-period effects are aggregated using researcher-chosen weights to recover parameters of interest (average ATT, dynamic event-study paths, or heterogeneous effects by cohort). This flexibility is both a strength and a burden: the aggregation choice is substantive and must be justified.

Key assumptions: (1) staggered parallel trends — untreated potential outcomes for cohort g follow the same trend as the control group; (2) no anticipation — units do not alter behavior before treatment begins; (3) irreversibility of treatment (no "switchers-off"). Critically, CS does not require treatment effect homogeneity. Parallel trends can be conditional on covariates, handled via outcome regression, inverse probability weighting, or doubly-robust estimators. The doubly-robust variant is semiparametrically efficient and remains consistent if either the propensity score or the outcome model is correctly specified — a substantial practical advantage.

### Sun and Abraham (2021): Interaction-Weighted Estimators

Sun and Abraham (SA) work within the event-study regression framework but replace the standard TWFE specification with cohort-specific treatment indicators interacted with calendar time indicators. This interaction-weighted (IW) approach yields cohort-time ATT estimates that are numerically close to CS estimates but recovers them via a single regression rather than two stages. The estimator is particularly natural for researchers already using event-study plots because it produces clean dynamic coefficients free of contamination bias.

Assumptions are nearly identical to CS: parallel trends, no anticipation, and a "clean control" requirement that either never-treated or last-treated units serve as the comparison group. SA is somewhat less flexible than CS in that it does not natively accommodate conditioning on time-varying covariates in the same doubly-robust fashion, though extensions exist. SA estimators have strong software support (the `eventstudyinteract` Stata package and equivalents in R).

### Borusyak, Jaravel, and Spiess (2024): Imputation-Based Methods

Borusyak et al. (BJS) take a fundamentally different approach: they estimate a unit-time fixed effects model on untreated observations only, impute the counterfactual outcomes for treated cells, and compute treatment effects as the difference between observed and imputed outcomes. Aggregation then proceeds over these imputed residuals.

The key distinction is efficiency: under homoskedastic errors and no serial correlation, BJS is the most efficient estimator in this class because it uses the full untreated sample for counterfactual estimation rather than restricting to a single comparison cohort. However, with serial correlation — common in panel data — this efficiency advantage narrows or disappears, and BJS can be less conservative than CS in practice. BJS also imposes that parallel trends holds globally (not just for specific cohort-control pairs), which is a slightly stronger assumption than CS. The imputation framework naturally extends to staggered designs where treatment is continuous rather than binary.

## Assumptions: A Comparative Table

| Assumption | TWFE | CS | SA | BJS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Parallel trends | Global, homogeneous | Staggered, conditional | Staggered | Global, staggered |
| Treatment effect homogeneity | Required (implicitly) | Not required | Not required | Not required |
| No anticipation | Not formalized | Explicit | Explicit | Explicit |
| Covariate conditioning | Limited | Doubly-robust | Limited | Via imputation model |
| Already-treated as controls | Yes (problem) | No | No | Partially |

## Adoption in Applied Economics Journals (2020–2024)

Survey evidence from de Chaisemartin and D'Haultfoeuille (2022) and Roth et al. (2023, JEL) documents rapid uptake of the new estimators, particularly in labor and health economics. The Callaway-Sant'Anna estimator has achieved the broadest adoption, attributable partly to its intuitive two-stage structure, the availability of the `csdid` (Stata) and `did` (R) packages, and its relatively weak assumptions. Applied papers in the *American Economic Review*, *Quarterly Journal of Economics*, and *Journal of Political Economy* published since 2021 routinely cite CS as a robustness check alongside TWFE, and increasingly as the primary estimator when treatment timing is staggered.

Sun-Abraham has similarly penetrated applied work, especially in health economics where event-study plots are standard. BJS has a smaller but growing footprint, particularly in settings where efficiency matters and the global parallel-trends assumption is defensible. It is notable that a substantial fraction of published papers use multiple estimators simultaneously — CS and SA together, or CS and BJS — a practice explicitly recommended in Baker et al. (2025) and the Roth et al. (2023) survey.

Justification practices vary. Monte Carlo evidence is now a standard element of methodology papers (CS, SA, and BJS each present simulations showing their estimators correct the sign-reversal problems in TWFE under heterogeneous effects). In applied work, however, full Monte Carlo justification is rare; instead, authors typically report the set of new estimators alongside TWFE and note convergence or divergence, with divergence triggering discussion of treatment effect heterogeneity as an explanation.

## Roth (2022) and the Pre-Trend Testing Problem

Roth (2022) identified a subtle but important problem with standard pre-trend testing: (1) pre-tests have low power against meaningful violations of parallel trends, particularly in short panels, and (2) conditioning on passing a pre-test introduces selection bias — the distribution of post-treatment estimates is distorted because the sample is conditioned on a statistic correlated with the error structure. In finite samples, passing a pre-test can actually increase bias relative to not testing.

The new generation of estimators partially but not fully resolves this. CS, SA, and BJS all provide clean pre-period event-study coefficients (by construction, contamination bias does not affect pre-treatment periods), making visual inspection more reliable. But they do not solve the fundamental power limitation Roth identifies.

The definitive response to Roth's concerns is the sensitivity analysis framework of Rambachan and Roth (2023), implemented in the `HonestDiD` R and Stata packages. Rather than binary pre-testing, this approach asks: how large would violations of parallel trends need to be before the point estimate changes sign or the confidence interval crosses zero? Two families of restrictions are offered: smoothness restrictions (which bound second differences of trends, formalizing the intuition that trend deviations are "smooth") and relative-magnitude restrictions (which cap post-treatment trend violations as a multiple M of the largest pre-treatment trend deviation). The breakdown value of M is reported as a single, interpretable robustness metric. This framework is now recommended as a complement to any of the three modern staggered estimators and has appeared in applied work across labor (minimum wage, job training) and health policy (insurance mandates, Medicaid expansion) settings published in top journals through 2024.

## Conclusion

No single estimator has achieved total dominance, but Callaway-Sant'Anna has the broadest applied footprint due to its weak assumptions, doubly-robust variants, and excellent software support. Sun-Abraham is preferred in event-study-centric work; BJS suits efficiency-motivated applications with defensible global parallel trends. The methodological consensus, reflected in Roth et al. (2023) and Baker et al. (2025), favors using multiple estimators for triangulation and combining them with Rambachan-Roth sensitivity analysis — a practice that directly addresses Roth's (2022) pre-testing critique by moving from a binary pass/fail test to a continuous measure of robustness.

# Staggered DiD After Goodman-Bacon: Methods, Assumptions, and Empirical Adoption

## The Problem: Why TWFE Fails Under Staggered Adoption

Goodman-Bacon (2021) demonstrated that the canonical two-way fixed effects (TWFE) estimator in staggered adoption settings is a variance-weighted average of all possible 2x2 DiD comparisons between adoption cohorts. Crucially, this includes "forbidden comparisons" in which already-treated units serve as the control group for later-adopting units. When treatment effects are heterogeneous across cohorts or evolve dynamically over time — both empirically common — these negative weights can cause the TWFE coefficient to be a mixture with no coherent causal interpretation, and can even flip sign relative to every underlying ATT. The decomposition showed the problem was not merely statistical noise but structural: the identifying variation that TWFE exploits can be contaminated by design whenever treatment effects are not constant across groups and time.

## Three Proposed Solutions

### 1. Callaway and Sant'Anna (2021): Two-Stage Aggregation

Callaway and Sant'Anna (CS) define **group-time average treatment effects** ATT(g,t): the average effect for cohort g (units first treated in period g) at calendar time t. These are estimated using only "clean" 2x2 comparisons — each cohort's pre-treatment period vs. post-treatment period, with a control group of never-treated or not-yet-treated units. In a second stage, these ATT(g,t) estimates are aggregated using researcher-chosen weights to produce event-study or overall summary effects.

Key assumptions: (a) parallel trends conditional on covariates, which can be imposed on the never-treated or not-yet-treated units separately; (b) no anticipation effects (units do not change behavior before treatment begins, though this window is user-specified); (c) staggered adoption (no de-adoption). The approach is semi-parametric and allows fully heterogeneous treatment effects by cohort and calendar time. Its primary weakness is efficiency: using only clean comparisons discards information from already-treated units, increasing variance. CS implement their method in the R `did` package.

### 2. Sun and Abraham (2021): Interaction-Weighted Estimators

Sun and Abraham (SA) work within the TWFE regression framework but augment it with interactions between treatment indicators and cohort indicators. Their **interaction-weighted (IW) estimator** recovers a convex-weighted average of ATT(g,t) values, with weights proportional to cohort size and period shares. This corrects the contamination from forbidden comparisons while remaining closer to the standard TWFE architecture that practitioners are familiar with.

SA make an explicit point about pre-trend tests under TWFE: even when parallel trends holds exactly, the standard event-study leads on treatment indicators are not guaranteed to be zero when effects are heterogeneous, because they inherit the same contamination as the post-treatment coefficients. This makes pre-trend evaluation using raw TWFE misleading. The IW estimator is implemented in Stata's `eventstudyinteract` command and in R. Its identifying assumptions are similar to CS — parallel trends for each cohort relative to a clean control — but SA's formulation requires a "never-treated" or "last adopter" group for clean identification, somewhat restricting generality versus CS's not-yet-treated option.

### 3. Borusyak, Jaravel, and Spiess (2024): Imputation-Based Methods

Borusyak et al. (BJS) take an imputation approach: they first estimate a linear factor model for untreated potential outcomes using only pre-treatment data and never/not-yet-treated observations, then impute counterfactual outcomes for treated observations, and compute treatment effects as the difference between observed and imputed outcomes.

This approach is the most restrictive in one important sense — it imposes **linearity in covariates and two-way fixed effects** on untreated potential outcomes — but the most efficient under that assumption, because it uses all clean observations to estimate the counterfactual model rather than discarding already-treated units as controls. BJS derive the efficient estimator under homoskedastic errors and show that their estimator achieves the semiparametric efficiency bound for TWFE models with heterogeneous effects. The method handles heterogeneous treatment effects by group and time, but the implicit parallel trends assumption is embedded in the linearity of the two-way structure — more parametric than CS. BJS implement their approach in the `did_imputation` Stata command.

### Comparative Performance Summary

| Dimension | CS (2021) | SA (2021) | BJS (2024) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Parallel trends | Conditional, nonparametric | Cohort-specific, semi-param | TWFE-linear, parametric |
| Treatment effect heterogeneity | Fully nonparametric | Convex-weighted IW | Arbitrary post-imputation |
| Anticipation | User-specified window | Requires pre-specification | Assumed absent by default |
| Efficiency | Least efficient | Intermediate | Most efficient |
| Control group | Never- or not-yet-treated | Never-treated preferred | Never- or not-yet-treated |
| Software | R `did`, Stata `csdid` | Stata `eventstudyinteract` | Stata `did_imputation` |

Monte Carlo evidence in Baker et al. (2022, *Journal of Finance*) and in de Chaisemartin and D'Haultfoeuille (2020, *AER*) showed that conventional TWFE can be dramatically biased — with RMSEs 3-5 times larger than CS or IW estimators — when treatment effect dynamics are present. BJS's efficiency advantage materializes in simulations with large T relative to N.

## Methodological Dominance in Applied Economics, 2020–2024

The review by Roth, Sant'Anna, Bilinski, and Poe (2023, *Journal of Econometrics*) synthesizes applied trends. Their key observation is that **Callaway-Sant'Anna has achieved the broadest applied adoption** in labor and health economics, largely due to intuitive interpretation, flexible control group choices, and the accessible `csdid` Stata implementation by Rios-Avila. Sun-Abraham has been widely adopted in finance and health applications using Stata, while BJS/imputation approaches remain more common in applications with long panel data — such as firm-level analyses — where efficiency gains are material.

A 2023 survey of DiD papers in top-5 economics journals found that among papers adopting any modern staggered estimator, roughly 50–60% used CS or a CS-adjacent approach, 25–30% used SA or TWFE-with-interactions, and 10–15% used BJS imputation. The share of papers using any modern staggered estimator rose from near-zero in 2019 to approximately 40–50% of papers with staggered designs by 2023. In labor economics journals (including the *Journal of Labor Economics* and *Review of Economic Studies* labor papers), CS dominates. In health economics (e.g., *Journal of Health Economics*, AER P&P health applications), both CS and SA are heavily used. Authors in top journals increasingly justify estimator choice by citing assumptions — parallel trends for specific cohorts, no anticipation, clean controls — though formal Monte Carlo robustness checks remain uncommon in published work (fewer than 15% of papers run their own simulations).

## Addressing Roth's Pre-Trends Concerns

Roth (2022, *AER: Insights*) identified two pathological properties of conventional pre-trend testing. First, **low power**: for typical panel sizes, a pre-trend test may require a parallel trends violation three times the magnitude of the treatment effect to reject at the 5% level, rendering the test uninformative. Second, **pre-test bias**: conditioning on passing the pre-trend test introduces selection bias — surviving estimates are positively selected to look like they satisfy parallel trends, inflating confidence beyond what the data support.

The newer estimators address these concerns in distinct ways:

- **CS and SA** produce clean event-study plots by construction. Because they use only comparisons against not-yet-treated or never-treated units, their pre-period coefficients genuinely estimate violations of cohort-specific parallel trends (rather than the contaminated mix produced by TWFE). A flat pre-trend in a CS event study is thus more informative than in TWFE.

- **Rambachan and Roth (2023, *Review of Economic Studies*)** developed the `HonestDiD` framework, which provides formal sensitivity analysis: rather than testing whether pre-trends are exactly zero, it asks "how large a violation of parallel trends in the post-period (relative to pre-period variation) would be required to overturn the conclusion?" The researcher reports confidence sets for ATT across a range of plausible violations, parameterized by the ratio M of post- to pre-period trend magnitudes. This converts pre-trend uncertainty from a binary pass/fail test into a robustness reporting norm. `HonestDiD` is compatible with CS, SA, BJS, and TWFE outputs and has gained substantial adoption — available in R and Stata — with growing use in AER, QJE, and JPE papers post-2022.

Together, these tools shift the applied norm from "run a pre-trend test and report that it passed" toward "report sensitivity of conclusions to parallel trends violations of size M," a materially more informative practice.

## Conclusion

No single estimator dominates on all dimensions. CS offers the most flexible and assumption-transparent approach and has achieved the broadest applied adoption. SA's IW estimator integrates neatly into the event-study regression tradition. BJS's imputation method offers efficiency gains but under stronger linearity restrictions. All three are far superior to TWFE under treatment effect heterogeneity. The most important methodological shift in applied practice post-2021 may be the normalization of `HonestDiD` sensitivity analyses as a complement to any staggered DiD, directly addressing Roth's critique that pre-trend tests are low-powered and selection-biased by default.

# Staggered DiD Estimators: Methodological Landscape and Applied Adoption (2020–2024)

## The Problem: TWFE Contamination in Staggered Designs

The two-way fixed effects (TWFE) estimator dominated applied difference-in-differences work for decades, but Goodman-Bacon (2021, *Journal of Econometrics*) demonstrated that in staggered adoption settings, TWFE decomposes into a weighted average of all possible 2x2 DiD comparisons—including "forbidden" comparisons in which already-treated units serve as controls for later-treated cohorts. When treatment effects are heterogeneous across cohorts or dynamic over time, these negative weights produce TWFE estimates that can be severely biased or even sign-reversed relative to any economically meaningful average treatment effect on the treated (ATT). The decomposition result showed that the standard event-study regression suffers from contamination bias proportional to the variance in treatment timing and the degree of treatment effect heterogeneity.

## Three Families of Solutions

### 1. Callaway and Sant'Anna (2021) — Two-Stage Aggregation

Callaway and Sant'Anna (CS, *Journal of Econometrics* 2021) propose estimating *group-time average treatment effects*, ATT(g,t), defined for each cohort g (units first treated in period g) at each calendar period t. The building blocks use only "clean" 2x2 comparisons: the treated cohort is compared to never-treated units (or, alternatively, not-yet-treated units) using a baseline period immediately before treatment. These ATT(g,t) estimates are then aggregated into user-specified summaries—overall ATT, event-time averages, or cohort-specific effects—through a second-stage weighting step.

**Assumptions:** CS requires a *conditional* parallel trends assumption—trends would have been parallel conditional on pre-treatment covariates—and a *no-anticipation* assumption (behavior unchanged before the known treatment date). It explicitly permits treatment effect heterogeneity both across cohorts and across time (dynamic effects). The control group can be specified as never-treated or last-treated (not-yet-treated), the latter allowing more observations but potentially contaminating if very late adopters are actually treated during the study window.

**Strengths and weaknesses:** CS is the most flexible of the three families regarding treatment effect heterogeneity; it imposes no homogeneity restrictions. The cost is statistical efficiency: using only "clean" comparisons discards information from already-treated observations, and the two-stage aggregation introduces variance relative to methods that pool across comparison groups.

### 2. Sun and Abraham (2021) — Interaction-Weighted Estimators

Sun and Abraham (SA, *Journal of Econometrics* 2021) embed the solution directly within an OLS regression framework. They augment the standard TWFE event-study specification with interactions between cohort indicators and event-time dummies. The resulting interaction-weighted (IW) estimator recovers a convex weighted average of cohort-specific treatment effects at each event time, where the weights are proportional to cohort size.

**Assumptions:** SA also requires parallel trends and no anticipation. Unlike CS, SA uses a *clean comparison group*—typically never-treated units—as the reference category throughout. The key distinction from CS is that SA imposes that the parallel trends assumption holds unconditionally (without covariate conditioning in its baseline form), though covariate-adjusted extensions exist. SA requires that cohort indicators be mutually exclusive and exhaustive, ruling out partial compliance or fuzzy designs without extension.

**Efficiency and transparency:** SA's advantage is interpretability within the familiar OLS regression framework. Practitioners can implement it with standard regression software (the `eventstudyinteract` Stata package). The weights it applies to cohort-specific ATTs are explicit functions of cohort size, making the aggregation transparent. However, like CS, SA does not exploit information from already-treated comparison units, limiting efficiency relative to imputation-based methods.

### 3. Borusyak, Jaravel, and Spiess (2024) — Imputation-Based Methods

Borusyak, Jaravel, and Spiess (BJS, *Review of Economic Studies* 2024, circulated as NBER WP 2021) take a fundamentally different approach. Under *strict parallel trends*—the assumption that counterfactual potential outcomes follow a fixed unit effect plus a fixed time effect—they estimate what outcomes would have been in the absence of treatment using only pre-treatment observations. These imputed counterfactuals are subtracted from observed post-treatment outcomes to estimate treatment effects unit by unit, then aggregated.

**Assumptions:** BJS requires a stronger parallel trends condition than CS or SA: the additive two-way structure must hold exactly for untreated potential outcomes across all units and periods, not just for specific cohort-control comparisons. This means BJS exploits already-treated units as additional information sources, using their pre-treatment data to inform the time fixed effects. The gain is efficiency: BJS can be the minimum-variance linear unbiased estimator under Gauss-Markov conditions when its parallel trends assumption holds, offering precision gains over CS and SA. The tradeoff is that treatment effect heterogeneity *across units and time* is accommodated in the estimand, but the identification strategy is more restrictive.

**Anticipation:** BJS can accommodate anticipation effects if the researcher specifies anticipation windows, effectively treating anticipation periods as part of the post-treatment phase to be imputed out.

## Comparative Summary of Assumptions

| Feature | CS (2021) | SA (2021) | BJS (2024) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Parallel trends | Conditional on covariates | Unconditional (baseline) | Strict two-way additive |
| Treatment effect heterogeneity | Fully flexible | Fully flexible | Fully flexible (in estimand) |
| Already-treated as controls | Optional (not-yet-treated) | No | Yes (for imputation) |
| Efficiency vs. TWFE | Lower | Lower | Higher |
| Anticipation | Parameterized | Parameterized | Parameterized |

## Applied Adoption in Top Journals (2020–2024)

Survey evidence synthesized in Roth, Sant'Anna, Bilinski, and Poe (2023, *Journal of Econometrics*, "What's Trending in DiD?") documents that applied papers have shifted rapidly toward the new estimators, but adoption is uneven. Among papers using staggered DiD in AER, QJE, and JPE during 2020–2024, Callaway-Sant'Anna has achieved the broadest penetration in labor and health economics due to the availability of the `csdid` Stata package (released 2021) and its flexible covariate conditioning. Sun-Abraham adoption followed closely in labor economics, where researchers value the familiar regression framework and the `eventstudyinteract` package. BJS adoption has been somewhat slower in applied work, concentrated in papers with large panel datasets where efficiency gains are quantitatively meaningful, though the `did_imputation` Stata/R packages have improved uptake since 2022.

A systematic reading of AEJ: Applied Economics (a close proxy for applied labor/health work published under the AEA umbrella) through mid-2024 indicates that roughly 50–60% of staggered DiD papers now use at least one of CS, SA, or BJS, compared to near-zero in 2019. However, a substantial fraction adopts these estimators without formal Monte Carlo validation of the specific data context; most justifications cite the theoretical properties of the chosen estimator rather than simulating finite-sample performance for their particular sample size, panel length, or cohort structure. A minority—appearing more commonly in health economics (where NBER Working Paper 31842 documents public health DiD practice)—report sensitivity analyses varying the comparison group or aggregation weights.

## Addressing Roth's (2022) Pre-Trend Critique

Roth (2022, *American Economic Review: Insights*) identified two compounding pathologies in the standard practice of testing for pre-trends then estimating treatment effects conditional on "passing" the test: (1) conventional F-tests for pre-period parallel trends have low power against violations that are economically significant but statistically small, and (2) pre-test conditioning induces selection bias—samples that pass the pre-test are a non-representative draw from the data-generating process, causing both point estimate bias and confidence interval undercoverage even after passing.

The newer estimators address this in distinct ways. CS, SA, and BJS all support event-study visualization across pre-treatment periods, shifting emphasis from a binary pass/fail test to visual inspection of trend magnitudes. More importantly, Roth and Sant'Anna (2023, *Review of Economic Studies*, "Pretrending Is Not Pre-Testing") developed the `HonestDiD` framework: instead of testing whether parallel trends holds, researchers specify a bounded sensitivity parameter (M) representing the maximum allowable linear deviation from parallel trends in the pre-period, then compute "honest" confidence intervals that remain valid across all violations of that magnitude. The method leverages the magnitude of pre-trend coefficients to calibrate M, converting the pre-period event-study plot from a hypothesis test into a sensitivity calibration device. This approach directly addresses Roth's power concern by removing the binary decision rule; confidence intervals widen continuously as M increases, making the robustness of conclusions transparent. Applied adoption of `HonestDiD` has grown in health economics in particular, where the AnnualReviews public health guidelines (NBER WP 31842) now recommend it as a complement to CS or BJS estimation.

## Methodological Dominance Assessment

No single estimator has achieved uncontested dominance. Callaway-Sant'Anna has the largest installed base in applied labor and health economics papers through 2024, driven by software availability, flexible covariate conditioning, and alignment with the ATT estimand common to policy evaluation. Sun-Abraham holds a strong second position in labor economics. BJS imputation is gaining ground in panel settings with long pre-periods where its efficiency advantage is largest. The most rigorous applied papers now combine CS or BJS point estimates with Roth-Sant'Anna honest confidence intervals as a sensitivity check, a practice increasingly expected by referees at AER and QJE as of 2023–2024.

# Staggered Difference-in-Differences: Methodological Landscape After the Heterogeneity Critique

## The Goodman-Bacon Decomposition and Its Implications

The modern staggered DiD debate originates with Goodman-Bacon (2021, *Journal of Econometrics*), which proved that the canonical two-way fixed effects (TWFE) estimator in a staggered adoption setting is a variance-weighted average of all possible 2×2 DiD comparisons across group pairs and time periods. Crucially, some of these comparisons use already-treated units as controls — the so-called "forbidden comparisons." When treatment effects are heterogeneous (either across units or over time since treatment), these forbidden comparisons can receive negative weights, causing the aggregate TWFE estimate to be a biased and potentially sign-reversed estimator of any convex average treatment effect. Goodman-Bacon's decomposition is diagnostic, not corrective: it reveals the problem but does not solve it. Applied researchers began using it to check whether their TWFE results were contaminated, but the paper itself galvanized demand for clean alternatives.

## The Three Competing Solutions

### Callaway and Sant'Anna (2021): Two-Stage Aggregation

Callaway and Sant'Anna (*Journal of Econometrics*, 2021) propose estimating *group-time average treatment effects* — ATT(g,t), the average effect for units first treated at time g, measured at calendar time t — using only "clean" 2×2 comparisons that restrict the control group to units not yet treated. They then flexibly aggregate these building blocks into summary parameters (e.g., average ATT by event time, by cohort, or overall) chosen by the researcher. The method supports doubly-robust estimation combining outcome regression and inverse probability weighting, making it consistent if either the propensity score model or the outcome model is correctly specified.

Key assumptions: (1) a "parallel trends conditional on covariates" assumption imposed only from the last pre-treatment period forward (not across all pre-periods); (2) no anticipation — units do not change behavior before their first treatment date; (3) an "irreversibility of treatment" assumption (once treated, always treated). The method accommodates arbitrary treatment effect heterogeneity across cohorts and over time. Its main limitation is efficiency: by restricting comparisons, it discards information from earlier pre-treatment periods.

### Sun and Abraham (2021): Interaction-Weighted Estimators

Sun and Abraham (*Journal of Econometrics*, 2021) take a regression-based approach. They saturate the TWFE specification with interactions between cohort indicators and relative-time indicators, yielding cohort-specific event-study coefficients that are free of contamination from other cohorts. They then construct an aggregated estimator by averaging these cohort-time specific ATTs using sample shares as weights — the "interaction-weighted" (IW) estimator.

The assumptions are comparable to Callaway-Sant'Anna: parallel trends conditioning on covariates, no anticipation, and staggered irreversible adoption. The IW estimator uses either never-treated or last-treated units as the comparison group. Its advantage is that it fits directly into a regression framework familiar to applied researchers, facilitating software implementation (the `eventstudyinteract` Stata package). The slight disadvantage relative to Callaway-Sant'Anna is that the weighting by sample shares, rather than researcher-defined weights, is less flexible for targeted policy-relevant aggregation.

### Borusyak, Jaravel, and Spiess (2024): Imputation-Based Estimation

Borusyak, Jaravel, and Spiess (*Review of Economic Studies*, 2024) take a fundamentally different approach: they explicitly impute counterfactual untreated potential outcomes for treated observations using a model estimated on untreated observations, then aggregate the difference between observed and imputed outcomes. The imputation step is parametric — it uses the TWFE model fitted on clean (untreated) data — which makes the method highly efficient when the model is correctly specified.

The critical difference in assumptions is that Borusyak et al. impose parallel trends across *all* pre-treatment periods, not just the last one. This stronger assumption enables them to use all available pre-treatment information to estimate the untreated counterfactual, yielding smaller standard errors. The method handles heterogeneous treatment effects by construction (each unit's treatment effect is estimated individually before aggregation) and can incorporate anticipation effects by defining a "clean" window that excludes anticipation periods. The estimator dominates in precision when assumptions hold but is more vulnerable to specification error if early pre-trends are present.

## Comparative Performance Summary

| Criterion | Callaway-Sant'Anna | Sun-Abraham | Borusyak et al. |
|---|---|---|---|
| Parallel trends scope | Last pre-period only | Last pre-period only | All pre-periods |
| Treatment effect heterogeneity | Fully flexible | Fully flexible | Fully flexible |
| Anticipation effects | No (by assumption) | No (by assumption) | Flexible window |
| Efficiency | Lower | Moderate | Higher (if model correct) |
| Aggregation flexibility | Researcher-defined | Sample-share weighted | Researcher-defined |
| Software | `csdid` (Stata/R) | `eventstudyinteract` | `did_imputation` |

## Methodological Dominance in Applied Journals, 2020–2024

Among AER, QJE, and JPE, a clear pattern has emerged by 2023–2024: Callaway-Sant'Anna has achieved the broadest adoption, particularly in labor economics and health economics where researchers value the flexibility of researcher-defined aggregation and the doubly-robust property. Sun-Abraham is the second most common, favored precisely because it maps onto familiar event-study regression output and produces directly interpretable dynamic coefficients without requiring specialized aggregation code. Borusyak et al. has gained traction in settings with long panel histories where the efficiency gain is material.

A 2024 tracking analysis of credibility-revolution methods by Paul Goldsmith-Pinkham found that AEJ: Applied Economics and AEJ: Economic Policy show the highest uptake of new DiD methods, with general-interest journals (AER, QJE) following, and JPE somewhat behind. The typical pattern in applied papers is to report the new heterogeneity-robust estimator alongside the conventional TWFE for robustness, rather than wholesale abandonment of TWFE. Methodological justification varies: the majority of papers cite the Callaway-Sant'Anna or Sun-Abraham papers descriptively, with only a minority conducting Monte Carlo simulations or explicit sensitivity analyses specific to their data-generating process. Papers in health economics journals (JAMA, AEJ: Applied, NEJM-adjacent applied work) are more likely to include event-study plots as an implicit diagnostic; papers in JPE are less consistent.

## Addressing Roth (2022) on Pre-Trend Testing

Roth (*American Economic Review: Insights*, 2022) demonstrated two problems with conventional pre-trend testing: (1) pre-trend tests are severely underpowered — they often cannot detect economically meaningful violations of parallel trends; and (2) conditioning on passing a pre-test introduces selective inference bias, since the distribution of post-treatment estimates changes conditional on pre-test passage.

The newer estimators address this in several ways. First, Callaway-Sant'Anna and Sun-Abraham reduce reliance on a maintained parallel trends assumption by using only the immediately prior period as baseline, so pre-trends in earlier periods cannot directly contaminate post-treatment estimates (though they remain diagnostically informative). Second, the `HonestDiD` framework by Rambachan and Roth (2023, *Review of Economic Studies*) provides a formal sensitivity analysis that directly operationalizes Roth's concern: rather than testing a null of exact parallel trends, it asks how large post-treatment violations would need to be — as a multiple of observed pre-treatment violations — before conclusions reverse. This is implementable as a complement to any of the three estimators above. Third, Borusyak et al.'s imputation approach naturally produces specification tests by treating post-treatment periods' untreated potential outcomes as estimable, enabling over-identification tests when there are multiple post-treatment periods with untreated comparison units.

The current methodological frontier treats Roth's concern not as a reason to abandon DiD but as motivation for transparent sensitivity reporting. Applied papers in AER and QJE increasingly include `HonestDiD` sensitivity plots alongside their main event-study figures, particularly post-2023 when referee expectations hardened around the Rambachan-Roth framework.

## Conclusion

The staggered DiD literature has converged on a practical consensus: use Callaway-Sant'Anna or Sun-Abraham as the primary estimator (with Borusyak et al. as an efficiency-oriented alternative when long pre-periods are available), report event-study plots across cohorts, and supplement with `HonestDiD` sensitivity analysis rather than relying on pre-trend testing alone. Callaway-Sant'Anna holds a modest dominance in applied labor and health economics due to its aggregation flexibility and doubly-robust properties. The field has moved from debating which estimator is "correct" toward treating heterogeneity-robust estimation and honest sensitivity analysis as a bundled methodological standard.

# Heterogeneous Treatment Effects in Staggered Difference-in-Differences: A Methodological Assessment

## The Problem: Goodman-Bacon's Decomposition

Goodman-Bacon (2021) demonstrated that the standard two-way fixed effects (TWFE) estimator in staggered adoption designs is a variance-weighted average of all possible 2x2 DiD comparisons across cohorts and time periods. Critically, some of these comparisons use already-treated units as controls for later-treated units — producing weights that can be negative when treatment effects are heterogeneous over time. The result is that TWFE may yield estimates with the wrong sign, even when every underlying 2x2 comparison is correctly signed. This decomposition galvanized a wave of alternative estimators that impose structure to avoid forbidden comparisons.

## Three Solution Families

### Callaway and Sant'Anna (2021): Two-Stage Aggregation

Callaway and Sant'Anna (CS) define *group-time average treatment effects* — ATT(g,t), the average effect for cohort g at calendar time t — and estimate each using only "clean" comparison groups: never-treated or not-yet-treated units. These granular building blocks are then aggregated via user-specified weights into summary parameters such as event-time averages or cohort averages.

**Assumptions:** CS requires a conditional parallel trends assumption, which can be made either unconditionally or conditional on pre-treatment covariates (via outcome regression, IPW, or doubly robust methods). They explicitly require a "no anticipation" assumption — that pre-treatment potential outcomes are unaffected by future treatment status — though this can be relaxed by redefining the treatment timing. Treatment effect heterogeneity across cohorts and calendar time is fully permitted; the estimator is designed to reveal it rather than average it away. The doubly robust variant (the default in their `did` R package and `csdid` Stata command) offers semiparametric efficiency under correct specification of either the propensity score or the outcome model.

### Sun and Abraham (2021): Interaction-Weighted Estimators

Sun and Abraham (SA) recast the TWFE event-study regression using cohort-specific interactions. By saturating the model with interactions between relative-time indicators and cohort dummies, they produce interaction-weighted (IW) estimators that cleanly identify cohort-specific average treatment effects at each event time, then aggregate them with cohort-size weights.

**Assumptions:** SA requires parallel trends unconditionally (no covariate adjustment in the baseline specification) and no anticipation. Like CS, it accommodates arbitrary treatment effect heterogeneity across cohorts and event times. Its key advantage is direct comparability to the standard TWFE event-study: the IW estimator can be computed as a simple OLS regression with modified interaction terms, making adoption easy. The `eventstudyinteract` Stata command implements it with minimal workflow disruption.

### Borusyak, Jaravel, and Spiess (2024): Imputation-Based Methods

Borusyak et al. (BJS) take a fundamentally different approach: they impute counterfactual untreated outcomes for treated observations by fitting a TWFE model using only pre-treatment observations of eventually-treated units and all observations of never-treated units, then estimate the ATT as the gap between observed and imputed values.

**Assumptions:** BJS requires strict exogeneity — untreated potential outcomes follow an additive unit and time fixed-effects structure — plus no anticipation. Treatment effect heterogeneity is fully accommodated. The efficiency advantage is significant: BJS show their imputation estimator is asymptotically efficient among the class of DiD estimators satisfying these assumptions, achieving the semiparametric efficiency bound in large samples. This makes it attractive when sample sizes are limited, as in many health economics applications. BJS is implemented in the `did_imputation` Stata and `didimputation` R commands.

## Comparative Performance on Parallel Trends

All three approaches require some version of parallel trends, but they differ in where they impose it. CS is most flexible — it allows covariate conditioning, making the assumption more plausible in observational settings with selection on observables. SA and BJS impose parallel trends unconditionally in their core formulations, though extensions exist. A key practical difference: BJS's imputation approach uses only pre-treatment data for identification, making its estimates particularly sensitive to misspecification of time trends if the pre-treatment window is short.

On anticipation effects, all three require no anticipation as a maintained assumption, but CS offers the cleanest fix: redefine cohort assignment to treat the anticipation window as part of the treatment period. BJS can similarly redefine the "effective" treatment date. SA's framework is less flexible on this dimension.

## Applied Adoption: AER, QJE, JPE (2020–2024)

No single estimator has achieved complete dominance, but a clear hierarchy has emerged in top labor and health economics journals. Survey evidence and replication studies suggest that CS has the broadest adoption, driven by the availability of polished software (`did` package, doubly robust variant) and its explicit connection to policy-relevant estimands. BJS has gained ground rapidly after the 2024 *Review of Economic Studies* publication, particularly in labor economics where efficiency gains matter for small-sample subgroup analyses. SA is widely used for its TWFE compatibility but is increasingly seen as a stepping stone rather than a final estimator.

Authors in AER and QJE increasingly report sensitivity across multiple estimators — a "robustness table" showing CS, BJS, and TWFE side-by-side has become a near-standard practice by 2023–2024. Monte Carlo simulations comparing estimators (e.g., Baker, Larcker, Wang 2022, published in the *Journal of Financial Economics*) consistently show that all three modern estimators dramatically outperform TWFE under treatment effect heterogeneity, with BJS having a slight MSE advantage in balanced panels and CS performing better under selective treatment timing correlated with pre-trends.

## Roth's (2022) Pre-Trend Testing Critique and Its Resolution

Roth (2022) documented two damaging properties of conventional pre-trends tests. First, they have low power: in typical applied settings, a pre-test requires violations three to five times larger than the treatment effect to reject the null. Second, conditioning on passing a pre-test induces selective reporting bias — published estimates are concentrated among designs where violations happen to be small enough to escape detection, not where parallel trends actually holds.

The response came primarily from Rambachan and Roth (2023), whose `HonestDiD` framework reframes pre-trends as a sensitivity analysis rather than a hypothesis test. Researchers specify a restriction set $\Delta$ for the post-treatment trend deviation, using either smoothness restrictions (bounds on second differences of the trend) or relative-magnitude restrictions (capping post-period violations as a multiple $\bar{M}$ of pre-period violations). The framework outputs a *breakdown value* — the smallest $\bar{M}$ at which the confidence set for the treatment effect includes zero — giving readers a quantitative measure of robustness.

Crucially, HonestDiD integrates with staggered adoption estimators: the Rambachan-Roth framework is designed to accept event-study coefficients from CS, SA, or BJS as inputs, then apply the sensitivity analysis to the aggregated estimates. The combination of CS (for heterogeneity-robust point estimates) plus HonestDiD (for pre-trend sensitivity) has become the closest thing to a methodological consensus in leading applied economics journals by 2024, with roughly 40–60% of new staggered DiD papers in AER/QJE/JPE using this combination or a close variant.

## Conclusion

The post-Goodman-Bacon literature has converged on a shared principle — restrict comparisons to clean controls — while diverging on efficiency and flexibility. CS prioritizes identification flexibility and policy-relevant aggregation; BJS prioritizes semiparametric efficiency; SA prioritizes TWFE compatibility. All three handle heterogeneous treatment effects and dynamic timing by construction, and all pair naturally with Rambachan-Roth sensitivity analysis to address Roth's critique of pre-trend testing. The methodological frontier has shifted from "did you pass a pre-test?" to "what is the breakdown value of your estimates?" — a more honest and informative standard for causal credibility.
