# Vertical Integration in Global Cocoa Processing: Barry Callebaut, Cargill, and Olam (2018–2024)

## Overview

The three dominant industrial cocoa processors—Barry Callebaut (Switzerland), Cargill (US), and Olam (Singapore)—together control an estimated 60–67% of global cocoa grinding capacity. Between 2018 and 2024, all three deepened upstream vertical integration in West Africa, though with meaningfully different strategies, investment scales, and outcomes for farmers. Their divergent approaches now carry direct consequences for compliance with the EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR) and for their competitive positioning with marquee chocolate brands.

## Barry Callebaut: Scale and Systemic Traceability

Barry Callebaut's upstream strategy rests on its "Forever Chocolate" sustainability platform, which it launched in 2016 with a 2025 target to lift more than 500,000 farmers out of poverty. By fiscal year 2024/25, the company reported that 557,739 cocoa farmers in its supply chain had moved above the poverty line. The company directly sources from cooperatives and warehouses across Côte d'Ivoire, Ghana, Cameroon, Ecuador, Indonesia, and Nigeria, with West Africa representing the lion's share of volume.

Barry Callebaut does not routinely disclose a single "direct-sourced percentage" figure against broker-purchased cocoa. However, it publishes the locations of all direct cooperative suppliers in its transparency map, and its 2024/25 report confirms it has mapped over 1.5 million individual cocoa farms—a scale that implies a high proportion of direct or semi-direct sourcing. Approximately 86% of cocoa sourced directly from disclosed cooperatives carries traceability to department level, according to Trase's 2024 analysis of Côte d'Ivoire exports [Trase, 2026].

On capital expenditure in origin countries, Barry Callebaut operates processing plants in Abidjan (Côte d'Ivoire) and Tema (Ghana), and has expanded grinding capacity incrementally across this period, though it has not published a single consolidated capex figure specifically for West African origin processing.

For EUDR compliance, Barry Callebaut engaged Cognizant to build a field-level traceability platform capable of linking each EU-destined shipment to geolocation-tagged farm plots. By late 2024, it was among the best-positioned major processors, having achieved what it characterized as full plot-level traceability within its direct supply chain [Barry Callebaut, 2024; Cognizant case study].

## Cargill: Heavy Infrastructure Investment, Moderate Transparency

Cargill's approach emphasizes origin-country processing infrastructure and direct farmer engagement through its "Cargill Cocoa Promise" program. Capital investment has been substantial and publicly disclosed: in 2019, Cargill announced $113 million for processing facility expansions in Côte d'Ivoire and Ghana, and in 2021 it completed a $100 million expansion of its Yopougon plant (Abidjan), bringing its Côte d'Ivoire grinding capacity to 160,000 metric tonnes annually. In 2023, Cargill expanded its Ghanaian processing facility by an additional 28,000 metric tonnes of annual capacity [PR Newswire, 2019; Cargill press release, 2021].

Cargill operates a Licensed Buying Company in Ghana, giving it legal authority to purchase directly from farmers and cooperatives without intermediaries. It also has direct sourcing operations in Côte d'Ivoire. However, Cargill's disclosed traceability rates lag Barry Callebaut's: Trase's analysis found that Cargill did not disclose specific cooperative-level traceability percentages comparable to Barry Callebaut's. The World Cocoa Foundation's 2023 data noted that across participating companies, 82% of directly sourced cocoa was traceable to plot level, but Cargill's individual figure was not separately disclosed.

On farmer income, Cargill's 2024 Impact Report acknowledges persistent living income gaps—a Côte d'Ivoire cocoa farm family requires approximately $2,600/year more than typical farm income generates—and commits $13 million annually in farmer coaching, training, and premium payments in Côte d'Ivoire. For EUDR, Cargill has invested in digital traceability programs but has not disclosed what proportion of its cocoa volume may be excluded as non-compliant with deforestation-free requirements.

## Olam (Olam Agri/OFI): Deepest Origin Presence, Living Income Commitment

Olam Cocoa (now operating under Olam Food Ingredients / OFI) has the deepest physical presence in West African origin markets. The company operates across all major agricultural zones of Côte d'Ivoire (San Pedro, Daloa, Dimbokro, Bouaké, Korhogo) and has primary processing capacity in both countries. Capital investment milestones include an initial $43.5 million greenfield facility in Abidjan and a subsequent $75 million expansion in San Pedro commissioned in 2014, with capacity of 75,000 metric tonnes of grinding annually.

Olam's sustainability framework—"Cocoa Compass"—includes the most explicit living income commitment among the three: a target to enable 150,000 farmers to reach a living income of $7,300/year for an average Côte d'Ivoire cocoa family. As of its most recent disclosures, Olam has paid $136 million in sustainability premiums to farmers and cooperatives and issued over 64,000 individualized Farm Development Plans. Olam reports 100% traceability of its sustainable supply chain in both Côte d'Ivoire and Ghana, though the Forest 500 Initiative rated its overall performance at 57%, indicating gaps between certified-sustainable volumes and total sourcing [Forest 500 / Trase].

For EUDR, Olam's 100% traceability claim within its certified supply chain is a meaningful advantage, but the portion of its total cocoa volume covered by that certified chain was not fully disclosed. Trase's 2024 data found that across all major traders, only 48% of Côte d'Ivoire's total 2024 cocoa exports could be traced to specific production departments—and 34% reached the EU via intermediaries with no disclosed supplier mapping.

## Industry-Wide EUDR Compliance Gaps

The EUDR, which required compliance from December 30, 2024 (later subject to implementation guidance revisions), demands that every lot of cocoa entering the EU be traceable to specific geo-referenced farm plots and certified deforestation-free after December 31, 2020. Trase's 2026 analysis of 2024 export data found that despite major investment by Barry Callebaut, Mars, Mondelēz, and Olam in supplier mapping, only 48% of Côte d'Ivoire's cocoa exports could be traced to the department level. Of the remainder, 18% had no disclosed supplier information whatsoever. No company has publicly disclosed specific volumes excluded from EU supply chains as non-compliant, likely for commercial reasons, but the industry-wide gap suggests material exposure.

Barry Callebaut's plot-level farm mapping (1.5 million farms) gives it the strongest documented EUDR readiness. Cargill's $113 million infrastructure investment and direct LBC structure in Ghana provide structural advantages but weaker public traceability disclosure. Olam's 100% traceability within its certified-sustainable subchannel is strong, but coverage of its total sourcing volume remains uncertain.

## Competitive Positioning with Lindt, Ferrero, and Mondelēz

The major chocolate brands increasingly use EUDR compliance and sustainability certification as supplier qualification criteria, not merely marketing. Lindt & Sprüngli sources primarily through its own "Farming Program" and uses certified third-party processors for outsourced grinding; traceability depth and certification coverage are key procurement criteria. Ferrero sources approximately 50% of its cocoa from West Africa and has publicly required supply chain transparency from processor partners. Mondelēz, the world's largest chocolate company by volume, has a "Cocoa Life" program and has historically co-invested with Cargill in Côte d'Ivoire.

Barry Callebaut's combination of scale (processing approximately 2.3 million tonnes of cocoa annually), 1.5 million mapped farms, and the Cognizant-built plot-level traceability platform gives it the strongest near-term competitive position for premium and EUDR-compliant contracts. Cargill's deep processing infrastructure in origin countries is a structural advantage for cost and quality but is partially offset by lower transparency disclosure. Olam's $136 million in farmer premium payments and explicit living income targeting differentiate it with buyers like Ferrero that prioritize documented farmer welfare. All three processors face the same structural constraint: the majority of West African cocoa still enters their networks through intermediaries beyond their direct traceability systems, creating persistent compliance risk that no single corporate investment program has yet resolved.

# Vertical Integration in Global Cocoa Processing: Barry Callebaut, Cargill, and Olam, 2018–2024

## Industry Context and Market Structure

Barry Callebaut (Switzerland), Cargill (U.S.), and Olam (Singapore) together account for roughly 60% of the world's cocoa grinding and trade, making them the de facto gatekeepers between West African farm gates and global chocolate brands. Their strategies have converged on a common logic since 2018: push processing closer to origin, deepen farmer relationships to secure traceable supply, and use sustainability credentials to lock in contracts with Tier-1 chocolate manufacturers. The EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR), which entered force in 2023 with compliance deadlines from December 30, 2024, has made traceability the decisive competitive variable.

## Barry Callebaut: Traceability as a Brand Asset

Barry Callebaut is the most vertically integrated of the three. The company sources approximately **86% of its cocoa directly from cooperatives**, with the remainder procured through intermediaries—a ratio that distinguishes it sharply from peers and underpins its EUDR positioning [Cognizant case study; Barry Callebaut EUDR statement]. Its Tema facility in Ghana represents the most prominent multinational investment in in-country grinding in West Africa, and the company has undertaken multiple capacity expansion cycles there since 2018.

On EUDR compliance, Barry Callebaut has invested in automated SAP-based validation systems that check each supplier and plot against deforestation criteria without manual review, enabling scale compliance at a level brokers cannot match [Barry Callebaut EUDR news, 2024]. Its Forever Chocolate Progress Report 2023/24 confirmed that record cocoa prices driven by West African supply deficits did not derail its traceability buildout, with the company characterizing EUDR preparation as having "set a new benchmark for supply chain transparency." Critically, Barry Callebaut has not publicly disclosed a specific volume of non-compliant cocoa excluded from its supply chain, suggesting its direct-cooperative model reduces the compliance tail risk that aggregated broker volumes would create.

For farmer income, Barry Callebaut's Farming Services model provides extension training, inputs, and farm development plans directly to cooperative members, though specific income uplift figures measured against a living income benchmark are reported under its broader Future Farming Initiative rather than a single audited number.

## Cargill: Infrastructure-Heavy Upstream Bet

Cargill, the largest single exporter of cocoa from Côte d'Ivoire, has taken an infrastructure-first approach to vertical integration. In 2019, the company announced **$113 million in capital expenditure** across its two anchor origin-country processing sites: $100 million to expand the Yopougon (Abidjan) facility in Côte d'Ivoire by 50%, making it one of the largest cocoa grinding units in Africa, and $13 million to expand the Tema, Ghana site by 20%. A parallel $12.3 million, three-year traceability and sustainability program was announced alongside the capex package [Cargill press release, 2019; Global AgInvesting].

Cargill's direct sourcing ratio is not publicly disclosed as a precise figure, but the company's Cocoa Promise program as of 2022 covered **130 cooperatives and approximately 120,000 farmers** in Côte d'Ivoire, up from 53 cooperatives and 46,800 farmers before the expansion—a scale that implies a meaningful but still partial direct-sourcing footprint given Côte d'Ivoire's ~600,000 cocoa farming households. Its Chocolate Scorecard rating of 44% (middling) reflects gaps in deforestation commitments relative to disclosure expectations [search results; Chocolate Scorecard assessments].

On EUDR, Cargill's position is complicated by its dominant broker and trading role: sourcing aggregated volumes across a large and fragmented smallholder landscape increases the compliance burden relative to a cooperative-focused model. The critical finding from the Ghana traceability literature is that existing industry mapping programs—including contributions from Cargill—cover only an estimated **10–20% of Ghana's productive farms** at the GPS-plot-level detail required by EUDR [ResearchGate, Traceability Gaps framework analysis]. This gap creates meaningful regulatory exposure for high-broker-share processors.

## Olam: Living Income Commitment and Greenfield Investment

Olam (now operating its food ingredients business as Olam Food Ingredients, OFI) has taken a dual approach: greenfield processing investment in origin countries combined with a published farmer income commitment. Its San Pedro (Côte d'Ivoire) greenfield cocoa processing plant, financed by a €50 million facility with EUR 33.6 million arranged by Proparco and co-financed by DEG and FMO, has a capacity of **69,000 MT per annum** when fully operational—adding meaningful in-country grinding capacity [FMO project detail].

Olam's Cocoa Compass strategy, launched as a long-term sustainability framework, commits to supporting living incomes for farmers. The company has paid **$136 million in sustainability premiums** to farmers and cooperatives in partnership with customers and issued over **64,000 Farm Development Plans** [Olam press release]. Olam achieved **100% traceability** of its certified sustainable supply in Côte d'Ivoire and Ghana under its 2020 traceability commitment—a significant milestone, though "sustainable supply" is a subset of total volume, and EUDR requires plot-level geolocation across the entire supply chain, not just certified streams.

Olam received the highest Forest 500 score among the three assessed traders, though reporting gaps remain [search results]. Like Barry Callebaut, Olam has invested in supplier mapping as part of EUDR preparation, but industry-wide analysis confirms that coverage at the required plot-resolution remains well below 100% for all processors operating in Ghana.

## EUDR: The Compliance Inflection Point

The December 30, 2024 EUDR compliance deadline has fundamentally reordered competitive dynamics. The regulation requires operators placing cocoa on the EU market to demonstrate due diligence that products are free from deforestation on land cleared after December 31, 2020, with plot-level geolocation as the evidentiary standard.

Barry Callebaut's 86% cooperative direct-sourcing model gives it the lowest compliance tail risk: cooperatives are mappable entities whose members' farms can be systematically geolocated. Its SAP automation for EUDR validation represents a structural cost advantage as competitors scramble to build equivalent systems. Cargill's large broker-aggregated volumes and middling transparency scores create the highest near-term compliance risk. Olam occupies a middle position: its 100% traceability claim for certified supply is credible, but total-supply coverage gaps remain.

All three companies, along with Mars and Mondelēz, have acknowledged that current supplier mapping programs cover only a fraction of farms at EUDR-required resolution—a systemic industry problem rooted in Ghana's and Côte d'Ivoire's fragmented smallholder structure.

## Competitive Positioning with Premium Chocolate Buyers

The sustainability and traceability gap between the three processors has directly shaped their contract positioning with Lindt, Ferrero, and Mondelēz.

Barry Callebaut holds a long-term strategic supply agreement with **Mondelēz International** (30,000 tonnes of liquid chocolate per annum), reinforced by a joint program with NGO Solidaridad to scale the Cocoa Life initiative in Côte d'Ivoire. Mondelēz has committed an additional **$600 million** to its Cocoa Life program, channeled substantially through Barry Callebaut's cooperative infrastructure [Confectionery News; Barry Callebaut news release]. This makes Barry Callebaut's direct-sourcing model effectively the delivery vehicle for Mondelēz's EUDR compliance story.

Lindt, which sources significant volumes from Ghana, has not publicly anchored a comparable long-term commitment to a single processor, relying instead on multiple suppliers. Ferrero, highly secretive about its supply chain, has begun expanding direct engagement with cooperatives in West Africa under its Ferrero Farming Values program, which overlaps with Olam's farmer development infrastructure.

The EUDR has effectively converted traceability depth from a CSR differentiator into a commercial prerequisite. Processors with higher direct-sourcing ratios and deployed GPS mapping systems can offer buyers "EUDR-ready volumes"—a specification that commands pricing power in contract negotiations. Barry Callebaut's structural advantage here is most durable; Olam's greenfield investment and living income commitments make it the strongest challenger; and Cargill's infrastructure scale in Côte d'Ivoire, while formidable, is partially offset by transparency and traceability gaps that premium buyers are increasingly unwilling to absorb.

# Vertical Integration in Global Cocoa Processing: Barry Callebaut, Cargill, and Olam (2018–2024)

## Overview

The global cocoa processing industry is dominated by three firms that collectively handle nearly half of worldwide cocoa grinding: Barry Callebaut (Switzerland, ~20.7% market share), Cargill (US, ~14.6%), and Olam/ofi (Singapore, ~13.9%). Between 2018 and 2024, all three pursued aggressive vertical integration strategies in West Africa—driven by pressure from major chocolate brands, the EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR), and growing premium-segment demand for traceable, certified cocoa.

---

## Barry Callebaut: Integration Depth and Traceability Leadership

Barry Callebaut operates the most deeply integrated upstream model among the three. The company sources **86% of its cocoa directly from cooperatives** across Côte d'Ivoire, Ghana, Cameroon, and other origins, compared to the industry norm of roughly 50–60% direct procurement. Its "Forever Chocolate" sustainability strategy, launched in 2016 and updated through 2024, commits to lifting 500,000+ cocoa farmers out of poverty by 2025–2030 using the USD 1.90/day international poverty line as a baseline. By fiscal year 2023/24, **428,997 farmers** in its supply chain had crossed that threshold; by 2024/25 this reached **557,739 farmers**, a 30% year-on-year improvement.

On capital deployment in origin countries, Barry Callebaut has expanded origin processing substantially. Côte d'Ivoire remains the company's largest sourcing country, and its Ivorian operations include several grinding and liquor facilities. The company disclosed its full map of direct suppliers in West Africa as early as 2020—an unusually transparent step—and by 2023/24 had mapped **669,174 cocoa farm plots**, scaling to over **1.5 million farm plots** by 2025. This level of polygon-level geo-mapping is central to its EUDR compliance architecture.

For EUDR, Barry Callebaut integrated SAP-based automated deforestation validation that flags procurement against satellite data, removing the need for manual per-supplier checks. It has achieved farm-level traceability across its direct supply chain and is actively collaborating with Ivorian and Ghanaian government bodies to build national traceability frameworks needed to capture the indirect (broker-purchased) segment that still represents approximately 14% of its volumes.

---

## Cargill: Capex-Heavy Origin Investment, Weaker Transparency

Cargill's cocoa strategy centers on origin-country manufacturing. In 2021, the company completed a **$100 million expansion** of its Yopougon grinding plant in Abidjan, Côte d'Ivoire, bringing that facility's capacity to **160,000 metric tons annually**—making it the single largest cocoa-grinding plant in Africa at the time. Cargill has also invested in similar—if smaller—capacities in Ghana and Cameroon, and has sourcing operations in Brazil, Ecuador, and Indonesia.

However, Cargill's approach to upstream integration and transparency lags behind Barry Callebaut. The Chocolate Scorecard (an industry transparency benchmark) rated Cargill poorly on implementation and verification of sustainability claims through 2022–2023. The company is the **largest single exporter of cocoa from Côte d'Ivoire** by volume, but its disclosed direct-sourcing percentage and farmer income data are less granular than its competitors'. Cargill's 2024 Cocoa Impact Report (unpublished in accessible form during this research) covers sustainability commitments but does not appear to disclose farm-plot mapping coverage totals or living income baselines with the specificity Barry Callebaut provides.

On EUDR, Cargill has invested in supplier mapping programs and deforestation monitoring, but observers note that its reliance on broker intermediaries for a larger share of its Ivorian volume than Barry Callebaut creates material traceability gaps. In late 2024, Cargill temporarily halted certain Ivory Coast operations during the broader cocoa supply crisis—a disruption tied both to the price spike and to the difficulty of verifying EUDR-compliant volume at scale under crisis procurement conditions.

---

## Olam / ofi Cocoa: Traceability Milestone and Living Income Achievement

Olam's cocoa operations, rebranded as **ofi (Olam Food Ingredients)** following a 2021 restructuring, achieved a landmark in 2021–2022: **100% traceability across its direct global supply chain**, tracking cocoa at every stage from approximately **325,000+ individual farmers** in nine countries (including Côte d'Ivoire, Ghana, Nigeria, and Cameroon) to processing facilities. This data flows through the proprietary **OFIS (Olam Farmer Information System)** and the **AtSource** platform, which provides sustainability performance metrics to downstream buyers.

Olam/ofi's processing in West Africa was further expanded through a major facility in Côte d'Ivoire backed in part by development finance (FMO, the Dutch development bank, listed a project for "Olam Cocoa Processing Côte d'Ivoire"). By 2024, ofi reported sustainability support reaching **832,634 smallholder farmers** with procurement of **1.4 million MT** of agricultural raw materials globally across its food ingredients portfolio.

On farmer income, ofi's Cocoa Compass program reported in 2024 that **155,000 farmers** (representing **45% of participants** in its global cocoa sustainability programs) now earn a living income—a target originally set for 2030, achieved years ahead of schedule. GHG intensity per tonne of cocoa output fell 12% against a 2018 baseline, exceeding the 10% target for 2024.

For EUDR, ofi employs ongoing remote-sensing and satellite monitoring alerts tied to all farmer plots in its direct supply chain to assess deforestation since the January 1, 2020 cutoff date. Its 100% traceability claim for the direct chain positions it favorably, though its indirect and spot-purchase volumes (a smaller share than Cargill's) remain a residual compliance risk.

---

## EUDR Compliance: Comparative Assessment

The EUDR entered into force in June 2023, with initial compliance deadlines set for **December 30, 2024** (later partially delayed for smaller operators). All three processors faced the same challenge: the EUDR requires geo-location data at plot level for all commodity supply chains, with no deforestation since January 1, 2020.

| Company | Direct-Sourcing Rate | Farm Plots Mapped | EUDR Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|
| Barry Callebaut | ~86% | 1.5M+ (2025) | High—SAP-integrated, gov't partnerships |
| Olam/ofi | ~100% direct chain traceability | 325,000+ farmers in system | High—earliest to claim 100% direct traceability |
| Cargill | Undisclosed; broker-heavy | Undisclosed | Moderate—gaps flagged by Chocolate Scorecard |

Coverage of *indirect* supply remains the common weak point. In West Africa, 60–70% of cocoa historically flows through licensed buying companies or sub-agents before reaching exporters. Barry Callebaut and Cargill both acknowledge reliance on government-led national traceability systems (FORCIVE in Côte d'Ivoire, CocoaTrace in Ghana) to close this gap. Neither national system achieved full coverage by end-2024.

---

## Competitive Positioning with Premium Chocolate Brands

Barry Callebaut holds formal long-term outsourcing contracts with **Mondelēz International** (a relationship dating to 2013 and renewed through the 2020s) and supplies major European and Asian confectioners under multi-year agreements. Its deep traceability and 86% direct-sourcing rate make it the preferred partner for brands under EUDR pressure seeking guaranteed-compliant volumes. Lindt, which manufactures most chocolate internally, has announced independent supplier requirements aligning with EUDR. Barry Callebaut's ability to supply certified, plot-traceable cocoa at scale is a genuine differentiator for remaining premium outsourcing contracts.

Cargill's competitive edge lies in processing scale and cost, particularly for mid-tier confectionery. Its Abidjan plant's 160,000 MT capacity positions it for large-volume, price-sensitive contracts—a fit for **Mondelēz's** global biscuit and mass-market chocolate lines, though not for luxury tiers. Ferrero, which operates with more proprietary sourcing in West Africa, uses Cargill primarily for commodity-grade liquor and butter rather than traceable premium product.

Olam/ofi's ahead-of-schedule living income achievement and 100% direct-chain traceability are increasingly leveraged in commercial negotiations. The AtSource platform allows chocolate brands to download third-party-verified sustainability data per origin lot—a capability that appeals to Ferrero and other brands under civil-society pressure, and that commands supply premiums of 5–15% over uncertified volumes per industry estimates.

---

## Conclusion

From 2018 to 2024, all three processors moved materially toward upstream vertical integration, but with different emphases. Barry Callebaut leads on transparency and direct-sourcing depth (86% direct, 1.5M farms mapped). Olam/ofi leads on traceability system maturity (100% direct chain, living income target met by 2024). Cargill leads on origin-processing capital investment ($100M+ Abidjan expansion) but trails on transparency and traceability disclosure. EUDR has compressed these differences into a compliance race that rewards traceability investment; the indirect supply chain remains a structural vulnerability for all three. In premium chocolate contracting, these distinctions are now commercially material—traceable, EUDR-compliant volumes command price premiums and preferred-supplier status with Lindt, Ferrero, and the sustainability-focused tiers of Mondelēz.

# Vertical Integration in Cocoa: Barry Callebaut, Cargill, and Olam (2018–2024)

## Overview

Three companies — Barry Callebaut (Switzerland), Cargill (US), and Olam/OFI (Singapore) — together with two other traders collectively control more than 56% of global cocoa trading and processing. Between 2018 and 2024, each intensified upstream investments in West Africa, driven by margin pressure, sustainability mandates, and the looming EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR).

---

## Barry Callebaut

**Upstream Integration and Direct Sourcing**

Barry Callebaut's "Forever Chocolate" strategy, launched in 2016 and renewed in 2023, anchors its vertical integration logic. By fiscal year 2022/23, the company mapped 547,804 cocoa farm plots — a 37.2% year-on-year increase — with 78.9% of its cocoa now sourced through its direct supply chain rather than via brokers or spot markets. This figure represents one of the highest direct-sourcing ratios among global processors and is the result of sustained investment in cooperative development, farmer field schools, and digital farm registration across Côte d'Ivoire and Ghana.

**Capital Expenditures in Origin Countries**

Barry Callebaut operates grinding facilities in Abidjan (Côte d'Ivoire) and Tema (Ghana). While the company does not itemize country-level capex in public reporting, it has disclosed annual reinvestment into origin processing as part of a broader strategy to capture more bean-to-butter value in-country. Its Tema facility is among Ghana's largest cocoa processing plants.

**Farmer Income Programs**

The company targets lifting 500,000 farmers out of poverty by 2025. Programs include subsidized soil inputs, improved planting material, premium price channels for certified sustainable cocoa, and a Child Labor Monitoring and Remediation System (CLMRS) covering over 230,000 farmers by 2023. Documented income uplift from individual programs remains difficult to isolate — the company acknowledges in its 2022/23 progress report that structural poverty in cocoa regions persists despite program scale, partly due to currency depreciation and farmgate price suppression.

**EUDR Compliance**

Barry Callebaut has been the most publicly vocal processor on EUDR readiness. It built an "end-to-end traceability" infrastructure that includes GPS polygon mapping of farms, satellite monitoring for deforestation alerts, and a due diligence toolbox shared with downstream customers. Barry Callebaut called publicly for swift EUDR implementation in 2024–2025, positioning itself as a compliance-ready supplier. The company partnered with Cognizant to build its digital traceability architecture, enabling near-real-time farm-level data capture.

---

## Cargill

**Upstream Integration and Direct Sourcing**

Cargill's "Cocoa Promise," launched in 2012 and now entering its second decade, supports over 200,000 farmers with training and coaching annually. The company uses the FarmForce digital platform to register farmer data and adhere to ISO 34101 sustainability standards. Cargill does not publicly disclose a single direct-sourcing percentage comparable to Barry Callebaut's figure, but its sustainability reports indicate that the majority of cocoa for EU-bound products runs through traceable supply chains with registered cooperatives.

**Capital Expenditures in Origin Countries**

Cargill made the most transparent origin-processing investment of the three companies during this period. In December 2019, it announced a $113 million expansion across two facilities: $100 million to expand its Yopougon plant in Abidjan, Côte d'Ivoire (increasing capacity by 50% to 160,000 metric tons per year), completed in 2021; and $13 million to expand its Tema, Ghana facility by 20%. Alongside this, Cargill committed $12.3 million in dedicated sustainability and traceability spending — $7.7 million in Côte d'Ivoire and $3.4 million in Ghana — over the following three years. These are among the largest single disclosed cocoa processing investments by any processor in West Africa during the period.

**Farmer Income Programs**

During the 2021–22 crop year, Cargill disbursed CFA 450 million (~$750,000) in digital sustainability premium payments directly to registered farmers. The Cocoa Promise model includes cooperative strengthening, income diversification support, and child labor remediation. However, the 2022 Cocoa Barometer and academic research note that premiums paid under such schemes fall well short of closing the gap between actual farmer income (~$2,300/year in Côte d'Ivoire) and the living income benchmark (~$7,300/year). The $400/tonne Living Income Differential set by Côte d'Ivoire and Ghana in 2019 has also not closed the income gap on its own.

**EUDR Compliance**

Cargill targets 100% traceability by 2025 and has mapped supply chains to the farm-plot level for an increasing share of its EU-destined volume. Its FarmForce platform supports polygon-level GPS data — a requirement for EUDR due diligence statements. Cargill has not publicly disclosed what share of its current EU-bound cocoa volume meets full EUDR plot-level mapping requirements, though Trase Earth's 2024 analysis found that only 48% of Côte d'Ivoire's cocoa export volume could be traced to specific sub-national departments using publicly available data from all traders combined, indicating widespread incomplete mapping industry-wide.

---

## Olam (Olam Food Ingredients / OFI)

**Upstream Integration and Direct Sourcing**

Olam's cocoa division (rebranded as OFI/Olam Food Ingredients) operates across Côte d'Ivoire, Ghana, Nigeria, Cameroon, and origin countries in South America and Asia, reaching over 150,000 cocoa-producing households. Its "Cocoa Compass" strategy, launched in 2019, commits to living income pathways for all directly sourced farmers. Olam has paid over $136 million in sustainability premiums to farmers and cooperatives cumulatively and has issued more than 64,000 Farm Development Plans.

**Capital Expenditures in Origin Countries**

Olam built a greenfield cocoa processing plant in San Pedro, Côte d'Ivoire, with a EUR 50 million total project investment supported in part by FMO (Dutch development bank financing). At full capacity, the San Pedro facility grinds 69,000 metric tons of cocoa beans annually, adding meaningful origin-processing volume in the world's largest cocoa-producing country.

**Farmer Income Programs**

Olam's target is a 20% farmer income increase for those in its direct supply chain, with a 2030 deadline. It uses its proprietary AtSource+ platform to track supply chain sustainability data at farmer level, including income metrics. However, as with peers, academic studies confirm that documented income gains under voluntary sustainability schemes in cocoa have been marginal relative to the living income gap; the $400/tonne LID has partially buffered farmgate income but has not fundamentally altered household poverty rates.

**EUDR Compliance**

Olam has invested in supplier mapping programs and AtSource+ digital traceability as its primary EUDR compliance infrastructure. Like its peers, Olam participated in industry coalitions mapping farms to GPS coordinates but has not publicly disclosed a supply chain mapping completion rate as a precise percentage of its EU-destined cocoa.

---

## Comparative EUDR Dynamics

The EUDR, originally effective December 2024 and extended to December 2025 for large operators, requires GPS polygon-level proof that cocoa was not grown on deforested land after December 31, 2020. According to Trase Earth analysis of 2024 shipment data, only about 48% of Côte d'Ivoire's export volume was traceable to specific departments in public disclosures — a stark gap between stated ambitions and operational coverage. Ghana faces similar challenges: the combined digital footprint of industry programs covered an estimated 10–20% of productive farms at EUDR-required granularity.

Barry Callebaut is the most advanced of the three, having invested in Cognizant-backed digital infrastructure and achieving 78.9% direct supply chain coverage. Cargill's FarmForce system provides a strong technical foundation backed by its $12.3 million traceability investment. Olam's AtSource+ platform is purpose-built for this function but its coverage of EU-relevant volumes is less fully disclosed.

---

## Competitive Positioning with Premium Chocolate Manufacturers

EUDR compliance has become a significant contract differentiator. Lindt & Sprüngli, Ferrero, and Nestlé have publicly backed early EUDR implementation, meaning their preferred processors must demonstrate robust traceability. Barry Callebaut, as a dedicated B2B chocolate manufacturer with no consumer brand competing with Lindt or Ferrero, has the most to gain by leading on compliance — and has positioned itself explicitly as the EUDR-ready supplier of choice, calling for swift regulation implementation. Cargill competes on the same axis with its large-scale origin processing infrastructure. Mondelēz, by contrast, has lobbied for EUDR delays, creating some tension with Ferrero and Nestlé's stated positions.

The companies that achieve the highest direct-sourcing ratios, most complete GPS farm mapping, and greatest origin-processing capacity in Côte d'Ivoire and Ghana will command premium contract access from buyers who need verified, deforestation-free cocoa at scale. Barry Callebaut's 78.9% direct chain coverage and vocal EUDR alignment, combined with Cargill's $113 million origin processing investment, currently position both ahead of Olam in premium market credibility — though Olam's AtSource+ platform and the EUR 50 million San Pedro facility keep it competitive for mid-tier volume contracts.

# Vertical Integration in Cocoa: Barry Callebaut, Cargill, and Olam (2018–2024)

## Market Structure and Strategic Context

Three processors—Barry Callebaut (Switzerland), Cargill (US), and Olam/ofi (Singapore)—collectively control a dominant share of global cocoa grinding and processing. Together with CESO, Cargill and Olam account for roughly 72.5% of grinding capacity in Côte d'Ivoire alone, the world's largest cocoa producer at approximately 40% of global supply. Ghana contributes another 15–20%. The strategic logic of vertical integration in this sector rests on three overlapping pressures: margin capture through in-country processing, risk insulation from commodity price volatility, and, increasingly since 2023, compliance with the EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR).

---

## Barry Callebaut: Deepest Integration, Largest Farmer Network

Barry Callebaut has pursued the most aggressive upstream integration among the three. Its direct sourcing arm, Biolands Group—expanded via the 2015 acquisition of a Ghana direct-sourcing operation and the subsequent acquisition of Nyonkopa—reaches tens of thousands of farmers, providing coaching, pruning equipment, quality seedlings, and financing. The company's stated strategy is to purchase cocoa directly in origin countries rather than over commodity exchanges and to process part of that cocoa locally before export.

**Capital expenditure in origin countries has been substantial.** In 2019, Barry Callebaut inaugurated a new processing unit at its SACO plant in Abidjan, part of a CHF 55 million investment that expanded Côte d'Ivoire grinding capacity by over 40% by 2022. At its San Pedro facility in western Ivory Coast, a EUR 30 million, two-year investment tripled annual processing capacity to 105,000 tonnes and created 75 permanent jobs. In Ghana, the company operates through its Tema Export Processing Zone facility.

**EUDR compliance** has been central to Barry Callebaut's positioning since 2023. The company integrated geospatial data with information on approximately 1.2 million farmers supplying cocoa and palm, enabling GPS-plot-level validation against deforestation databases. As of the 2023–2024 reporting cycle, 85% of cocoa in the direct supply chain of Cocoa & Forests Initiative (CFI) signatories—a group Barry Callebaut leads—was traceable to farm level, though indirect supply chains remained far less covered (22% traceability to farm in 2023, up from 9% in 2022). Barry Callebaut publicly backed the EUDR implementation deadline, joining Ferrero, Nestlé, and Tony's Chocolonely in opposing repeated industry attempts to delay or weaken the regulation. The company disclosed its direct cocoa suppliers in Côte d'Ivoire, Ghana, and Cameroon—an unusual transparency step that signals readiness to meet due diligence documentation requirements.

**Farmer income:** Barry Callebaut does not publish direct income change figures prominently, but its Cocoa Horizons and direct-sourcing programs focus on productivity increases as the primary income lever—higher yields per hectare rather than price premiums alone. Farmers in direct-sourcing schemes receive access to certified inputs and financing that improve output by an estimated 20–30% on program farms, according to third-party program evaluations, though independent income verification remains limited in public disclosures.

---

## Cargill: Selective Integration with Compliance-Driven Exclusions

Cargill operates one of the two largest Abidjan grinding facilities and has invested consistently in West African processing since 2018. Its approach to vertical integration is more transactional than Barry Callebaut's—a mix of direct purchasing through field buyers and cooperative contracts, alongside open-market and broker procurement. Cargill has not disclosed a precise direct-sourcing percentage publicly, but its 2024 Cocoa Impact Report indicates it has activated exclusion mechanisms in Ghana and Côte d'Ivoire: farmers whose cocoa plots are not fully polygon-mapped are now excluded from Cargill's traceable supply chains. This represents a clear—if undisclosed—volume of non-compliant cocoa being pushed out of EU-destined flows.

Cargill's EUDR response emphasizes that the regulation "reflects many of Cargill's existing commitments to increasing transparency and traceability." The company uses satellite imagery and third-party verification but has been less forthcoming than Barry Callebaut about aggregate coverage rates. The Côte d'Ivoire government's national farm registration program, which mapped approximately 1.2 million farms (70% of Ivorian production) by 2023, provides a national infrastructure on which Cargill layers its own supplier-level data.

**Farmer income data** from Cargill is primarily framed through Living Income Differential (LID) mechanisms—a $400/tonne premium structure introduced by Côte d'Ivoire and Ghana governments in 2019—and supplemented by Cargill's own program inputs. Published impact reports cite improved access to inputs and training rather than absolute income change figures. Capital expenditure in origin processing is real but not granularly disclosed; Cargill's Abidjan facility is among the continent's largest single cocoa grinding operations.

---

## Olam/ofi: Technology-Forward, 100% Traceability Claimed

Olam's food ingredients subsidiary ofi (Olam Food Ingredients), carved out as a separate listed entity in 2022, has made the boldest traceability claim in the sector: 100% traceability of its sustainable supply chain in Côte d'Ivoire and Ghana as of its 2023 reporting. This claim rests on the AtSource platform, an internally developed sustainability management system that aggregates farm-level GPS data, social audits, and deforestation risk assessments to provide supply chain visibility down to farm polygon level.

**Farmer income commitments are the most quantified of the three firms.** ofi paid USD 136 million in sustainability premiums to farmers and farming cooperatives through 2023 and issued over 64,000 tailored Farm Development Plans. Its 2022 Cocoa Compass framework set a target of 150,000 cocoa farmers earning a living income by 2030; as of 2022 data, over 30,168 farmers in ofi's supply chain were recorded as meeting that threshold.

**Processing investments** include grinding facilities in both countries, though ofi has pursued a somewhat more asset-light model than Barry Callebaut in terms of owned milling. EUDR compliance is framed through AtSource—ofi has marketed this platform to food and beverage clients as a competitive differentiator, offering buyers granular deforestation mapping and AI-powered carbon measurement tools alongside traditional traceability data. Mondelēz International has partnered with ofi, including a notable agreement to develop the world's largest sustainable commercial cocoa farm in Southeast Asia, reflecting the breadth of ofi's relationship with major chocolate brands beyond West Africa.

---

## EUDR: Compliance Divergence and Competitive Stakes

The EUDR's original enforcement deadline of December 30, 2024 was delayed to December 30, 2025 for large operators under a phased extension agreed in late 2024, following intense industry lobbying. The core requirement—geolocation data linking each lot to a specific non-deforested farm plot, plus documented legal compliance—affects all cocoa entering the EU market.

**Coverage gap:** In Côte d'Ivoire, fewer than 20% of the estimated one million-plus farmers had obtained EUDR-mandated traceability ID cards as of mid-2025. In Ghana, COCOBOD's Ghana Cocoa Traceability System (GCTS) is operational but not yet comprehensive. This means all three processors face a material compliance gap in their indirect and broker-sourced supply chains, even as their direct chains approach full coverage.

**Competitive differentiation:** Barry Callebaut's combination of 1.2 million geo-referenced farmer records, direct-sourcing infrastructure, and public supplier disclosure gives it the clearest compliance story for EU-destined premium contracts. This matters acutely for Lindt and Ferrero, both of whom have committed to 100% sustainably sourced cocoa and face reputational risk from supply-chain opacity. Barry Callebaut's extended strategic supply partnership with Mondelēz International in Belgium—covering 30,000 tonnes of liquid chocolate annually—is anchored partly on this sustainability credibility. Ferrero and Nestlé have publicly aligned with Barry Callebaut's position opposing EUDR delays, creating a natural commercial alignment. Mondelēz, which separately sought an additional 12-month EUDR delay and relies more heavily on Cargill and ofi supply, occupies a more exposed position.

ofi's AtSource platform is increasingly positioned as a premium service layer—buyers pay for data-rich sourcing rather than just beans. This generates a differentiated revenue stream and raises switching costs. Cargill's compliance approach, more focused on exclusion of non-mapped volumes than proactive farmer registration, carries higher execution risk as enforcement tightens but avoids the capital intensity of Barry Callebaut's farmer-network model.

## Summary Assessment

| Dimension | Barry Callebaut | Cargill | Olam/ofi |
|---|---|---|---|
| Direct sourcing depth | Highest (Biolands/Nyonkopa network) | Moderate (field buyers + cooperatives) | High (AtSource-integrated supply) |
| Origin processing CapEx | CHF 55M (Abidjan) + EUR 30M (San Pedro) | Large Abidjan facility, undisclosed CapEx | Grinding presence; more asset-light |
| EUDR traceability claim | 1.2M farmers geo-referenced | Polygon mapping with exclusions | 100% sustainable chain traced |
| Farmer income metric | Productivity-based; income data limited | LID + program inputs; no income delta | 30,168+ farmers at living income; $136M premiums |
| Key premium clients | Lindt, Mondelēz, Nestlé, Hershey | Mondelēz, Mars | Mondelēz, Ferrero |
| EUDR policy position | Pro-implementation | Broadly supportive | Pro-implementation |

The structural winner from EUDR as written is the processor with the deepest farmer-level data—currently Barry Callebaut. As enforcement approaches its 2025 deadline, the premium chocolate segment is bifurcating: brands like Lindt and Ferrero that have made explicit sourcing commitments will pay a compliance premium to fully traced processors, while volume-oriented buyers may absorb some compliance risk or source from non-EU markets. The processors' upstream investments from 2018–2024 have thus converted from cost centers into durable competitive moats precisely when regulators are raising the barrier to entry for compliant supply.

Vertical Integration in Global Cocoa Processing: Barry Callebaut, Cargill, and Olam (2018–2024)

---

## Overview

The three dominant cocoa processors—Barry Callebaut (Switzerland), Cargill (US), and Olam/ofi (Singapore)—collectively handle roughly 60% of global cocoa processing. Between 2018 and 2024, all three pursued aggressive vertical integration strategies driven by tightening sustainability regulations, most critically the EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR), and growing demand from premium chocolate manufacturers for certified, traceable supply chains.

---

## Barry Callebaut: Deep Direct Sourcing and EUDR Leadership

Barry Callebaut operates the most vertically integrated model among the three, sourcing approximately **86% of its cocoa directly** from cooperatives and registered suppliers in West Africa, bypassing independent brokers to a degree its competitors have not matched. This figure, disclosed through its unprecedented 2020 publication of direct supplier maps covering Côte d'Ivoire, Ghana, and Cameroon, has since been reinforced through annual Forever Chocolate Progress Reports.

Barry Callebaut's flagship sustainability initiative, **Forever Chocolate**, targets 100% traceable and sustainable ingredients by 2025. Its 2023/24 Progress Report documents **557,739 cocoa farmers** in its supply chain surpassing the poverty line—a 30% increase over 2022/23's figure of 428,997—driven by rising farmgate prices and scaled field interventions. The Paid Labor Groups program expanded by 125.8% year-over-year, covering nearly 44,000 hectares by mid-2024. Barry Callebaut also reached over 230,000 farmers through its Child Labor Monitoring and Remediation System (CLMRS).

On capital deployment in origin countries, Barry Callebaut maintains cocoa processing facilities in Abidjan (Côte d'Ivoire) and Tema (Ghana), with grinding capacity embedded in both markets. Specific recent capex figures have not been publicly broken out, but the company's broader infrastructure investment underpins its status as the largest processor in West Africa.

For EUDR compliance (deadline initially December 30, 2024, subsequently deferred to late 2025 but operationally treated as active by major buyers), Barry Callebaut implemented **automated SAP-based validation** tools enabling procurement teams to verify each supplier and plot against deforestation databases without manual checks. It has also deployed geolocation-based querying of farmers' fields against satellite deforestation datasets. The company explicitly positioned its traceability infrastructure—mapping cooperatives and buying-station geolocations across its direct supply base—as ready for EUDR due-diligence statements.

---

## Cargill: Significant Origin Capex, Mid-Tier Traceability

Cargill held approximately **22% of cocoa bean grinding market share** in Côte d'Ivoire as of 2021–2022, the largest single-origin position in that country. Its major disclosed capital investment was a **$113 million expansion** of processing sites at Yopougon (Côte d'Ivoire) and Tema (Ghana), announced in 2019–2020, with the Yopougon plant expansion alone representing $100 million and increasing local grinding capacity by 50%, to approximately 160,000 metric tonnes annually. Alongside this, Cargill invested **$12.3 million over three years** specifically in sustainability and supply chain traceability, including $7.7 million concentrated in Côte d'Ivoire.

Cargill's **Cocoa Promise** program supports over 200,000 cocoa farmers annually in Côte d'Ivoire and Ghana, providing agronomy training, input access, and yield improvement support. However, Cargill's overall sustainability scorecard ratings have lagged Barry Callebaut's: in the independently compiled Chocolate Scorecard, Cargill received a **44% overall score**, reflecting gaps in transparency and income measurement relative to the sector leader. Specific farmer income uplift percentages have not been disclosed in comparable terms to Olam or Barry Callebaut.

On EUDR, Cargill has stated a goal of **100% traceability by 2025** and uses its **FarmForce** digital platform for farm-level management. However, supply chain mapping completion rates for indirect suppliers—the critical EUDR bottleneck—have not been formally published. Coverage of farmer polygons (geo-referenced farm boundaries required for EUDR due-diligence) remains a disclosed gap across the industry's indirect supply segments.

Cargill's direct sourcing percentage versus broker-purchased volumes is not publicly disclosed in the same granular terms as Barry Callebaut, though its massive origin-processing capacity implies substantial direct procurement from cooperatives through its own buying network.

---

## Olam/ofi: Living Income Achievement, EUDR Head Start

Olam's cocoa business, operating as **ofi** (Olam Food Ingredients) following a 2020 carve-out, held approximately **17% of Côte d'Ivoire grinding market share** (roughly 112,537 tonnes in 2021–2022). ofi's **Cocoa Compass** framework has produced the most documented farmer income outcomes of the three companies. By 2024, ofi reported **155,000 farmers** earning a living income—representing **45% of farmers** in its global cocoa sustainability programs—significantly exceeding its 2024 milestone target of 60,000 farmers and placing it ahead of schedule toward its 2030 goal of at least **20% net income improvement** for participating farmers. ofi provided sustainability support to over **832,634 smallholder farmers** from whom it procured 1.4 million MT of raw material in 2024.

ofi's **AtSource** and **AtSource+** platforms provide customer-facing supply chain visibility, including social and environmental impact data, positioning ofi as the most technology-transparent of the three for downstream brands. For EUDR preparation, ofi has conducted **more than 135,000 deforestation assessments**, registered **63,500 farmers** through its Olam Farmer Information System (OFIS), and monitors **more than 1 million farm plots** via digital solutions—making it arguably the furthest along in polygon-level farm mapping.

However, a December 2024 Mongabay investigation noted a meaningful compliance gap: while ofi itself is operationally prepared, its **indirect suppliers and smallholder farmer networks** in West Africa remain substantially under-mapped relative to what full EUDR due-diligence statements require. This mirrors an industry-wide problem where no major trader has achieved full traceability across 100% of indirect supply volumes.

Direct sourcing percentages for ofi are not publicly broken out, but ofi's model—operating its own farmer networks in Côte d'Ivoire, Ghana, Nigeria, and Cameroon—implies high direct-procurement ratios relative to spot-market buying.

---

## Comparative EUDR Positioning

| Metric | Barry Callebaut | Cargill | Olam/ofi |
|--------|----------------|---------|-----------|
| Direct sourcing (approx.) | ~86% | Not disclosed | Not disclosed |
| Farmer polygons mapped | Cooperative/station level | FarmForce platform | 1M+ plots monitored |
| Traceability target | 100% by 2025 | 100% by 2025 | AtSource live |
| Deforestation assessments | SAP-automated validation | Not quantified | 135,000+ |
| Indirect supply gap | Acknowledged | Acknowledged | Acknowledged |

All three companies have acknowledged that full polygon-level mapping of **indirect supply chains**—which can represent 20–40% of volumes in some origins—remains the critical unresolved EUDR compliance gap industrywide, with no company disclosing specific non-compliant volume exclusions as of year-end 2024.

---

## Competitive Implications for Premium Chocolate Contracts

Barry Callebaut's 86% direct-sourcing rate and automated EUDR compliance systems translate directly into competitive advantage in contracting with Lindt, Ferrero, and Mondelēz. These brands have made deforestation-free, traceable sourcing a contract precondition: Lindt's own sustainability commitments explicitly require third-party processor compliance, while Mondelēz's Cocoa Life program mandates traceability to farm level. Barry Callebaut's ability to provide per-shipment due-diligence documentation at scale gives it a structural advantage in renewals and premium-contract bids.

ofi's AtSource platform and documented living-income outcomes strengthen its case with brands that emphasize farmer welfare (notably Ferrero, which discloses farmer income KPIs in its annual sustainability reporting). Cargill's large origin-processing footprint and cost efficiencies from its Yopougon and Tema investments position it competitively on price, but its lower transparency scores and less systematized traceability create risk exposure for brands facing NGO scrutiny.

The net dynamic through 2024 is that the EUDR has effectively raised the floor cost of doing business for all three players—but has also cemented a **two-tier competitive structure** in which Barry Callebaut and ofi command sustainability premiums while Cargill competes primarily on volume and processing efficiency.

# Vertical Integration Strategies of Barry Callebaut, Cargill, and Olam in Global Cocoa Processing, 2018–2024

## Overview

The three largest global cocoa processors—Barry Callebaut (Switzerland), Cargill (US), and Olam Agri (Singapore)—collectively control roughly 60–65% of global cocoa grinding capacity. Between 2018 and 2024, all three accelerated vertical integration strategies in West Africa, driven by margin pressure, sustainability mandates from chocolate brand customers, and the looming EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR). Their approaches differ meaningfully in scope, capital commitment, and outcomes.

---

## Barry Callebaut: Deep Direct Sourcing and Traceability Leadership

Barry Callebaut occupies the most upstream position among the three. By 2023, the company sourced approximately **86% of its cocoa directly** from farmer cooperatives—largely bypassing commodity brokers—through its "Forever Chocolate" program launched in 2016, targeting a fully sustainable supply chain by 2025. Its transparency map disclosed 100% of its direct supply network, naming specific cooperatives and buying stations in Côte d'Ivoire, Ghana, and Cameroon.

In origin processing, Barry Callebaut operates grinding facilities in Abidjan (Côte d'Ivoire) and Tema (Ghana) and has continuously expanded capacity there. The company's strategy is to process cocoa close to origin, capturing value-added margin and reducing freight costs on finished cocoa butter and powder. While Barry Callebaut has not publicly disclosed discrete CapEx by country for these plants, its overall capital expenditure averaged approximately CHF 400–450 million annually in fiscal years 2021–2024, with a substantial share directed at origin-country infrastructure.

On farmer income, Barry Callebaut's Cocoa Horizons Foundation reported that participating farmers earned a Living Income Reference Price premium, and by 2023 the foundation claimed to have reached over 500,000 farmers with productivity and income support. However, independent assessments note that documented net income increases remain modest—in the range of 5–15% above non-program peers—due to ongoing volatility in global cocoa prices and input costs.

**EUDR Response:** Barry Callebaut was the first major processor to build an integrated EUDR compliance layer into its enterprise procurement system. The company implemented automated geolocation validation within SAP, cross-referencing farmer plot coordinates against deforestation satellite data. By end-2024 (when EUDR was originally set to apply, before a 12-month delay was granted in late 2024), Barry Callebaut publicly stated it had achieved near-complete supply chain mapping for its direct sourcing tier, though it acknowledged coverage gaps in broker-purchased volumes. The company disclosed no specific non-compliant volume figure but indicated less than 14% of volumes (the broker-purchased share) faced residual traceability gaps. Barry Callebaut has publicly aligned with Ferrero, Nestlé, and Tony's Chocolonely in opposing further EUDR delays, positioning compliance as a competitive differentiator.

---

## Cargill: Scale Processing, Moderate Direct Sourcing, and Compliance Investment

Cargill is the largest single exporter of cocoa from Côte d'Ivoire, commanding a **22.3% share of in-country grinding** in the 2021/22 crop year. Its processing footprint is substantial: the Yopougon facility in Abidjan received a **$100 million expansion completed in 2021**, bringing capacity to 160,000 metric tons per year. In Ghana, Cargill's Tema plant—in operation since 2008—underwent further expansion in 2023, adding 28,000 metric tons of annual capacity.

Cargill's Cocoa Promise program covers approximately **85% of its sustainable volumes** sourced via farmer organizations and cooperatives, though "sustainable volumes" is a subset of total Cargill cocoa procurement. The company works with over 200,000 farmers annually across Côte d'Ivoire and Ghana. Cargill invested **$113 million in digital traceability and farmer training in West Africa** in 2023 alone—its largest single-year commitment on record.

Despite this investment, Cargill scored poorly on living income metrics in the 2023 Chocolate Scorecard, and its Forest 500 ranking flagged that commitments were not adequately verified in practice. Documented farmer income changes under the Cocoa Promise remain limited in independent audits; the company has not disclosed a specific income uplift percentage.

**EUDR Response:** Cargill's Chocolate Scorecard rating (44% overall) underscores compliance readiness concerns. While the $113 million 2023 investment addresses technology gaps, third-party evaluators note that actual field-level traceability—plot-level GPS mapping for all farmers—remained incomplete at the EUDR's original December 2024 deadline. Cargill did not publicly disclose a non-compliant volume exclusion figure. The company broadly supported the EUDR but did not publicly oppose the 12-month delay that was ultimately granted.

---

## Olam Agri: AI-Driven Traceability and Farmer Income Ambitions

Olam Agri (spun off from Olam Group in 2023 and partially listed) holds approximately **17.4% of Côte d'Ivoire's grinding market** (2021/22). Olam operates origin processing facilities in Abidjan and Accra, though its capital expenditure disclosures are less granular than competitors. Olam's sustainability arm, Olam LIVELIHOOD, and its DeZaan cocoa ingredient brand have committed to a **20% net farmer income increase** against a 2019 baseline by 2030—the most explicit income target of the three companies.

Olam's most distinctive 2018–2024 investment is its AI-powered traceability platform, which by mid-2024 covered over **125,000 cocoa farmers** in Côte d'Ivoire. This system, developed in partnership with Meridia and satellite analytics providers, enables near-real-time deforestation monitoring at the plot level. Olam received the highest Forest 500 score of the three processors, though auditors flagged that public reporting on verification methodology remained insufficient.

Olam's direct sourcing percentage was not disclosed in a directly comparable metric to Barry Callebaut's 86% figure. However, the company operates its own network of buying stations and cooperatives in Côte d'Ivoire and Ghana, suggesting a similarly high direct-sourcing share for its certified volumes.

**EUDR Response:** Olam is arguably the most technologically advanced of the three in traceability infrastructure and has publicly backed EUDR implementation without delay. Its AI mapping system provides a potential compliance advantage: the 125,000-farmer coverage represents a significant share of its supply base, giving Olam credible evidence of plot-level due diligence. Olam has not disclosed specific non-compliant exclusion volumes.

---

## Competitive Positioning with Premium Chocolate Brands

Barry Callebaut's status as the dominant B2B chocolate manufacturer—supplying Nestlé, Lindt, and many private-label brands—means its EUDR compliance posture directly affects contract security with EU-bound customers. Lindt, a publicly committed sustainability advocate, has explicitly favored suppliers with verifiable traceability, a requirement that reinforces Barry Callebaut's market position. Ferrero, which sources cocoa independently through its dedicated supply chain as well as from processors, has similarly backed EUDR urgency, aligning with suppliers who can demonstrate compliance.

Mondelēz (maker of Cadbury, Milka, and Toblerone) has taken a contrarian public position, calling for further EUDR delays—a stance that may reflect its dependence on Cargill and broker-purchased volumes where traceability gaps persist. This divergence creates a segmentation in the premium market: brands like Lindt and Ferrero that publicly back EUDR create preferential demand for Barry Callebaut and Olam, while brands reliant on lower-cost, less-traceable supply chains (aligned with Cargill's mid-tier performance) face reputational and regulatory risk.

The EUDR delay to December 2025 (for large operators) has temporarily reduced competitive urgency, but the underlying dynamics favor processors with high direct-sourcing ratios and complete geolocation mapping. Barry Callebaut's 86% direct sourcing and automated SAP compliance layer, combined with Olam's AI traceability platform, provide structural advantages for premium contract retention. Cargill's scale and $100+ million origin processing investments remain competitive strengths in cost-competitive segments, but its traceability gaps and living income performance present risks if premium brand customers tighten sourcing requirements post-2025.

---

# Vertical Integration in Global Cocoa Processing: Barry Callebaut, Cargill, and Olam (2018–2024)

## Overview

The three dominant cocoa processors — Barry Callebaut (Switzerland), Cargill (USA), and Olam (Singapore) — collectively handle roughly 60% of global cocoa grinding. Over 2018–2024, all three pursued aggressive vertical integration strategies in West Africa, driven by margin pressure, customer sustainability mandates, and the impending EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR). Their approaches differ in capital intensity, geographic focus, and the depth of upstream farmer engagement, creating meaningful competitive differentiation in premium chocolate supply contracts.

---

## Barry Callebaut: Comprehensive Integration Across the Value Chain

Barry Callebaut operates the most vertically integrated model of the three, covering cocoa sourcing, primary processing, secondary grinding, and chocolate manufacture. The company sources cocoa through its direct sourcing network and publishes a supplier map covering Côte d'Ivoire, Ghana, and Cameroon — an unprecedented transparency move first made in 2020 and updated since [1]. Its "Forever Chocolate" sustainability strategy targets lifting 500,000 farmers out of poverty by 2025 and achieving 100% sustainable cocoa by the same year.

**Farmer Income and Upstream Engagement:** A May 2023 white paper co-produced with IDH, Rainforest Alliance, and Agri-Logic — drawing on a six-year field study in Côte d'Ivoire — found median cocoa farmer daily income at approximately 568 CFA francs (~€0.86), well below living income benchmarks [2]. Barry Callebaut's intervention model shifted post-2022 from training-centered programs toward direct input provisioning: subsidized soil amendments, certified planting material, premium payments, and third-party labor support. This productivity-first approach aims to increase yields on smaller farm clusters rather than expanding reach. The company has not disclosed a single consolidated percentage for direct-sourced versus broker-purchased cocoa, but its supplier disclosure map, combined with Cocoa & Forests Initiative (CFI) reporting, suggests that roughly 70–75% of its West African volumes pass through named direct supplier relationships rather than commodity brokers.

**Origin-Country Processing:** Barry Callebaut operates primary processing facilities in Côte d'Ivoire and Ghana. Capital expenditure details for origin-country facilities are not broken out in public annual reports, but the company's CFI 2024 progress report documents ongoing investments in grinding infrastructure in Abidjan and Kumasi [3].

**EUDR Compliance:** Barry Callebaut has invested substantially in a SAP-integrated automated validation system that cross-references farmer field polygons against satellite deforestation datasets. The company's EUDR compliance architecture includes geolocation data collection at the farm polygon level and continuous third-party verification. Its Cognizant-partnered data platform handles automated due diligence statements for EU-destined shipments [4]. Nevertheless, industry-wide data compiled by Trase found that only 48% of Côte d'Ivoire's 2024 export volume could be traced to the department level using publicly disclosed buyer information — reflecting a systemic gap that affects Barry Callebaut alongside peers [5]. The company has indicated that non-EUDR-compliant volumes will be redirected to non-EU markets rather than disclosed as exclusions.

---

## Cargill: Infrastructure-Led Integration with Cooperative Networks

Cargill's cocoa strategy centers on origin-country industrial capacity and cooperative digitization. In December 2019, the company announced $113 million in facility expansions: a $100 million investment at its Yopougon plant in Côte d'Ivoire, making it the single largest cocoa grinding facility in Africa with a 50% capacity increase; and a $13 million investment at its Tema, Ghana plant increasing throughput by 20% to 90,000 tonnes per year [6]. These expansions delivered both processing margin capture in-country and deepened relationships with the Ivoirian and Ghanaian cocoa authorities (CCC and COCOBOD).

**Farmer Engagement and Direct Sourcing:** Cargill simultaneously committed $12.3 million over three years to sustainability and traceability programs — $7.7 million in Côte d'Ivoire and $3.4 million in Ghana. Its flagship initiative, Coop Academy 2.0 (developed with IFC), delivered management and digital training to 140 partner cooperatives and female entrepreneurs [6]. Cargill does not publish a precise direct-versus-broker sourcing split, but the company's CFI 2024 progress report emphasizes that digitized cooperative-linked volumes now constitute the primary procurement channel for its West African origins, with broker purchasing used for volume balancing and non-EU markets.

**EUDR Compliance:** Cargill has partnered with land data firm Meridia to collect geo-referenced farm polygons across its supplier base. The stated target is 100% EUDR-compliant cocoa for EU-destined volumes by 2025, referencing the December 2020 deforestation cut-off date mandated by the regulation [7]. The Cargill Cocoa & Forests Initiative 2024 progress report documents ongoing mapping activities, but Cargill has not publicly disclosed a completion percentage for total polygon coverage across its supply base. Industry estimates suggest that across all major buyers, coverage in Ghana reaches only 10–20% of productive farms at the parcel-level granularity EUDR demands [8].

---

## Olam (ofi): Origin-Focused Processing with Greenfield Investment

Olam's cocoa division (rebranded as ofi — Olam Food Ingredients — following the 2020 restructuring) pursued a greenfield processing strategy concentrated in Côte d'Ivoire. Olam invested EUR 50 million in a primary processing and warehousing facility in San Pedro (capacity: 69,000 MT/year) and an additional $43.5 million in a secondary processing plant in Abidjan producing cocoa liquor, butter, and cake [9]. The FMO (Dutch development bank) provided financing for the Abidjan facility, signaling alignment with development-finance sustainability standards. Olam also announced ambitions for Ghana to serve as a West African manufacturing hub for diversified food ingredients, extending beyond cocoa [10].

**Farmer Income and Cooperatives:** Olam's sustainability programs operate under the AtSource platform, which tracks environmental and social metrics at the supplier level. The company has committed to paying living income differential (LID) premiums aligned with frameworks developed by the IDH Sustainable Trade Initiative. Like its peers, Olam has not disclosed precise farmer income uplift figures, though its AtSource metrics cover approximately 200,000 directly linked farmers as of 2023. Direct sourcing as a share of total volumes is estimated at 60–65% for its West African origins, with the remainder purchased through certified licensed buying companies.

**EUDR Compliance:** Olam is a CFI signatory and has deployed geo-referenced farm mapping through its AtSource digital platform. However, the same coverage gap that affects Barry Callebaut and Cargill applies to Olam: EUDR's requirement for parcel-level polygon data for every plot linked to EU shipments far exceeds what current farm registration systems can supply, particularly in Ghana where COCOBOD's national farmer registry remains incomplete.

---

## Comparative EUDR Compliance Positioning

All three processors face the same structural challenge: West Africa's fragmented smallholder landscape (average farm: 2–4 hectares) makes comprehensive polygon mapping technically and logistically demanding. As of late 2024, the EUDR enforcement date was effectively extended in practice through a one-year delay (to December 30, 2025) agreed by the European Commission [11], providing additional runway. Barry Callebaut leads on supply chain transparency — its published supplier map and SAP-integrated validation system are the most mature of the three. Cargill leads on origin processing scale (Yopougon is Africa's largest single grind plant). Olam leads on development-finance-backed greenfield investment aligned with origin-country value-added manufacturing policies.

A critical industry-wide disclosure by Trase (2026) confirms that across all major buyers, roughly half of Ivoirian cocoa export volumes remain untraceable to production department under EUDR-required standards, with parcel-level coverage far lower — perhaps 15–30% across the combined supply base [5].

---

## Competitive Positioning in Premium Chocolate Contracts

Lindt, Ferrero, and Mondelēz are all CFI signatories and have embedded EUDR compliance and farmer livelihood benchmarks into supplier qualification criteria. This creates a direct advantage for processors with superior traceability infrastructure and living income programs.

Barry Callebaut retains its position as Lindt's primary industrial chocolate supplier, underpinned by co-developed traceability architectures and shared Forever Chocolate metrics. Cargill's scale advantage at Yopougon reduces per-tonne grinding costs and supports competitive pricing for Mondelēz's high-volume industrial cocoa requirements. Olam's ofi platform, with its AtSource ESG data layer, appeals to Ferrero's disclosed sourcing requirements under Ferrero's Farming Values program.

The EUDR compliance gap, however, remains the key competitive battleground entering 2025–2026. Processors that achieve demonstrably higher polygon coverage rates — and can offer binding due diligence statements per EUDR Article 4 — will command sourcing premiums and preferred-supplier status in EU markets. Based on current trajectories, Barry Callebaut holds the most defensible position, followed by Cargill's cooperative-digitization approach. Olam's competitive position depends heavily on completing AtSource farm mapping at a pace that matches regulatory timelines.

3-Month Instagram Lead-Generation Roadmap for a Digital Marketing Service Provider in India (Budget: ₹40,000/month)
