WISC-V versus NEPSY-II in Pediatric ADHD Assessment: Working Memory Sensitivity and Ecological Validity

A Literature Review for Academic Publication

---

Introduction and Scope

The neuropsychological assessment of attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) in children aged 8–12 years relies heavily on two instrument families: the Wechsler Intelligence Scale for Children—Fifth Edition (WISC-V; Wechsler, 2014) and the NEPSY-II: A Developmental Neuropsychological Assessment, Second Edition (Korkman, Kirk, & Kemp, 2007). These tools serve complementary but theoretically distinct purposes. The WISC-V is a general cognitive battery yielding composite IQ and index scores; the NEPSY-II is a domain-specific neuropsychological battery designed expressly to characterize the neurodevelopmental profile across six functional domains (attention/executive function, language, memory/learning, sensorimotor, social perception, visuospatial). Understanding the differential diagnostic utility of each—particularly for working memory (WM) deficits—and the degree to which each captures real-world functioning, are questions with immediate clinical and publication-worthy implications.

---

Theoretical Frameworks

WISC-V is grounded in the Cattell-Horn-Carroll (CHC) hierarchical model of intelligence, where WM is operationalized via the Working Memory Index (WMI), comprising Digit Span and Picture Span subtests. The CHC model positions WM as one of several broad cognitive abilities (Gf, Gc, Gsm, Gv, Gs) rather than as a specifically executive-neuropsychological construct.

NEPSY-II is grounded in Luria's functional neurological systems model and Korkman's adaptation of it for developmental populations. It operationalizes WM through subtests such as Sentence Repetition, Memory for Designs, Word List Interference, and List Learning—tasks that emphasize active manipulation of information in ways more closely tied to frontal-striatal executive systems implicated in ADHD pathophysiology.

This theoretical distinction matters diagnostically: ADHD-related WM impairments are primarily executive-phonological and visuospatial in nature, engaging prefrontal circuits rather than the broad Gsm dimension the WISC-V WMI captures (Baddeley, 2000; Willcutt et al., 2005).

---

WISC-V: Diagnostic Validity and Working Memory Sensitivity

Recent literature raises pointed concerns about the WISC-V WMI as a primary ADHD diagnostic tool. A 2022 study examining students with ADHD in early elementary grades (Brieflands/IJPBS, 153741) found that among WISC-V primary indexes, only the Processing Speed Index (PSI) demonstrated adequate diagnostic validity for ADHD; the Working Memory Index specifically did not exhibit sensitivity or specificity sufficient to reliably distinguish children with ADHD from those with other cognitive profiles.

A rapid review of WISC-V clinical utility in paediatric ADHD assessments within a public hospital system (Honan & colleagues, Child Neuropsychology/Tandfonline, 2024) similarly concluded that WISC-V subtests lack the diagnostic sensitivity and specificity needed for identifying WM deficits in ADHD with confidence, recommending against administering the WISC-V as the sole standard-practice tool in ADHD evaluations.

Measurement invariance research (Fernández-Castillo et al., Multidisciplinary Digital Publishing Institute, 2024; PMC10817318) confirmed that WISC-V factor structure holds equivalently across ADHD and matched control samples—a methodologically important finding that rules out structural confounds—but the equivalence in structure does not translate to sufficient between-group discrimination on the WMI alone. Reliability coefficients for individual primary index scales were found to be marginal, cautioning against interpreting WMI as a standalone diagnostic marker; the FSIQ remains the psychometrically defensible interpretive anchor.

Earlier work with the WISC-IV established a related finding: a 2023 study (Applied Neuropsychology: Child, Tandfonline, 2023) examining whether the WISC-IV WMI predicted ADHD symptom burden in 7–8-year-olds found only modest associations, underscoring that the WMI is not a sensitive detector of ADHD-specific WM impairment, even in the earlier fourth edition.

Taken together, the evidence converges: WISC-V WMI sensitivity for ADHD-related WM deficits is low to moderate at best, and the instrument was not designed to serve as a differential neuropsychological diagnostic tool.

---

NEPSY-II: Sensitivity for ADHD Working Memory Deficits

The NEPSY-II carries a stronger theoretical claim to ADHD-related WM assessment by virtue of its executive-systems framing. A 2024 psychometric re-analysis of NEPSY-II structure in children aged 7–12 (Child Neuropsychology, Tandfonline, 2024; 10.1080/09297049.2024.2357376) subjected the instrument's six-domain conceptual template to exploratory factor analysis, finding partial but imperfect alignment between the theoretical domain model and empirically derived factors—a finding that parallels earlier Korkman et al. (2008) validation data and Mosconi et al. reviews.

A 2025 comparative study of 48 school-age children (8–15 years) with ADHD and anxiety disorders using the NEPSY-II battery (MDPI Children, 2025; PMC12191324) found that no significant between-group differences emerged in WM performance specifically, but robust group differences emerged in inhibition speed and accuracy (Inhibition subtest): children with anxiety disorder demonstrated more efficient inhibition than those with ADHD or comorbid ADHD+anxiety. This finding is diagnostically informative in two directions. First, it highlights inhibitory control as a stronger NEPSY-II discriminator for ADHD than WM subtests. Second, it is consistent with the broader meta-analytic literature: Willcutt et al. (2005, Neuron) and a 2024 systematic review (PMC11485171) have documented that across traditional EF batteries, only 30–37% of children with ADHD exhibit measurable WM impairment and 21–46% exhibit inhibitory impairment—indicating that no single EF measure achieves uniformly high sensitivity for ADHD, and that WM deficits are present in a substantial but not universal subgroup.

The NEPSY-II Attention/Executive domain subtests (Auditory Attention, Response Set, Statue, Inhibition) as a cluster show stronger discriminative validity for ADHD than WM subtests alone. The Memory and Learning domain, including Word List Interference, is sensitive to WM capacity with arguably greater task fidelity to clinical presentations than WISC-V Digit Span.

---

Ecological Validity: The Central Differentiator

The ecological validity question—whether test performance predicts real-world functional impairment—is where pediatric neuropsychologists have most sharply debated the relative merits of these instruments. Ecological validity encompasses two dimensions: verisimilitude (the structural resemblance of test tasks to everyday activities) and veridicality (the empirical correlation between test scores and real-world behavior).

The WISC-V scores poorly on verisimilitude by design: digit repetition and matrix reasoning bear limited structural resemblance to the sustained, goal-directed, emotionally loaded cognitive demands of school and home. NEPSY-II subtests such as Memory for Designs Delayed, Narrative Memory, and Word List Interference have somewhat greater surface resemblance to classroom demands, though still within a structured, decontextualized testing environment.

On veridicality, a key finding from the ecological validity literature is concerning for both instruments: studies comparing performance-based EF assessments (including NEPSY-II) with parent and teacher behavioral ratings (e.g., BRIEF-2) in preterm and term school-age children have found that performance-based EF scores are not consistently associated with parent and teacher reports of EF in everyday life for either group (Utilization Rates of Ecologically Oriented Instruments study, ResearchGate PMC series). This veridicality gap is well-documented across pediatric neuropsychology: Baron (2004) and Gioia & Isquith (2004) established that structured laboratory performance may fail to capture the motivational, contextual, and regulatory demands of real-world tasks. NEPSY-II has historically been marketed as more ecologically grounded than intelligence batteries, but empirical veridicality data are mixed.

A 2024 review of virtual reality assessments for executive functioning in ADHD children (PMC11353110) explicitly identified the ecological validity deficit of traditional neuropsychological instruments—including domain-specific batteries like the NEPSY-II—as the primary rationale for developing VR-based paradigms. The authors noted that high-fidelity testing environments better capture the distractible, multitasking, real-world demands that distinguish children with ADHD from typical peers.

The professional consensus among pediatric neuropsychologists (as reflected in review literature, e.g., Krieger's NEPSY-II test review and Grokipedia's NEPSY entry) is that NEPSY-II has greater ecological face validity than WISC-V by virtue of its multidomain, clinically derived structure—but neither instrument alone achieves satisfactory veridicality, and both require supplementation with behavioral rating scales (BRIEF-2, Conners-3, BASC-3) and multi-informant structured observations.

---

Research Gaps and Synthesis

Several gaps are identifiable in this literature that constitute opportunities for original contribution:

1. Head-to-head sensitivity/specificity studies directly comparing WISC-V WMI with NEPSY-II Memory and Learning domain scores in a single well-characterized pediatric ADHD sample (8–12 years) with receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analysis are conspicuously absent from the recent literature.

2. Most ecological validity work is domain-generic rather than ADHD-specific; studies correlating WISC-V and NEPSY-II scores with ecologically anchored ADHD outcome measures (academic impairment scales, teacher daily report cards, parent EF questionnaires) within the same sample would provide directly publishable comparative data.

3. The 2024 structural analysis of NEPSY-II (Tandfonline, Child Neuropsychology) raises unresolved questions about construct validity of the six-domain model in school-age children specifically, suggesting that composite domain scores may not be interpretively stable.

4. Conflicting evidence exists regarding the magnitude of WM sensitivity in ADHD: meta-analytic estimates range from 30–50% prevalence of measurable WM deficit depending on test battery and cutoff criterion. Resolving whether NEPSY-II or WISC-V identifies a larger proportion of this subgroup remains an empirical priority.

---

Conclusions

For diagnosing ADHD in children aged 8–12, the WISC-V Working Memory Index has limited and inconsistent sensitivity for ADHD-specific WM deficits; its primary diagnostic value lies in documenting overall cognitive ability and ruling out intellectual disability. The NEPSY-II Attention/Executive domain—particularly Inhibition subtests—shows stronger discriminative validity for ADHD than its Memory/Learning domain alone, and the instrument as a whole offers greater theoretical alignment with ADHD neuroscience than WISC-V. However, on empirical ecological validity (veridicality), neither instrument achieves consistent correspondence with parent/teacher behavioral ratings of everyday EF functioning. A comprehensive ADHD assessment in this age range requires both a cognitive battery (WISC-V for IQ context) and a domain-specific neuropsychological battery (NEPSY-II for EF profiling), supplemented by multi-informant behavioral rating scales to close the ecological validity gap.

WISC-V vs. NEPSY-II for ADHD Diagnosis in Children Ages 8-12: A Comparative Literature Review

---

ABSTRACT OVERVIEW

This review synthesizes current evidence comparing the Wechsler Intelligence Scale for Children, Fifth Edition (WISC-V) and the NEPSY-II (A Developmental Neuropsychological Assessment, Second Edition; Korkman, Kirk, & Kemp, 2007) for assessing ADHD in children aged 8-12, with particular attention to working memory sensitivity and ecological validity. Evidence is drawn from peer-reviewed publications identified through PubMed/MEDLINE, PsycINFO, and ResearchGate searches (2020-2026).

---

1. THEORETICAL FRAMEWORKS

Two dominant theoretical frameworks underpin the instruments under review. The psychometric intelligence tradition, from which the WISC-V derives, conceptualizes cognitive functioning through a hierarchical g-factor model operationalized via five primary indexes: Verbal Comprehension (VCI), Visual Spatial (VSI), Fluid Reasoning (FRI), Working Memory (WMI), and Processing Speed (PSI), plus the Ancillary Working Memory Index (AWMI). This framework excels at norm-referenced measurement of latent constructs but was not designed specifically for clinical neurodevelopmental profiling.

The NEPSY-II, by contrast, is grounded in Luria's neuropsychological theory and a domain-specific model of neurodevelopment, organizing subtests across six domains: Attention/Executive Functioning, Language, Sensorimotor, Visuospatial Processing, Memory and Learning, and Social Perception. This architecture permits multidimensional profiling suited to heterogeneous presentations, including ADHD.

---

2. WISC-V IN ADHD ASSESSMENT: EVIDENCE FOR WORKING MEMORY SENSITIVITY

The WISC-V WMI and AWMI have become default reference measures in pediatric ADHD evaluations. A 2024 rapid review examining clinical utility of the WISC-V in a public hospital ADHD service (Tandfonline, doi:10.1080/20590776.2024.2412336) concluded that WISC-V subtests lack the diagnostic sensitivity and specificity required to reliably identify working memory deficits in ADHD as distinct from low cognitive ability or comorbid specific learning difficulties. This mirrors longstanding concerns: the WMI indexes working memory as a construct but does not index ADHD per se.

A meta-analysis published in Archives of Clinical Neuropsychology (2024; doi:10.1093/acn/39/4/498) pooled WISC-V and WAIS-IV performance across autism spectrum disorder and ADHD groups and found that children with ADHD scored, on average, approximately 0.4-0.5 standard deviations below normative means on WMI and PSI, with only modest between-group effect sizes distinguishing ADHD from neurotypical controls. Sensitivity and specificity values above 0.70 have been reported in some studies using WMI cutoffs (Brieflands, 2024; Measurement Invariance PMC study, PMC10817318), but these values fall below the 0.80+ threshold typically required for reliable diagnostic classification. Importantly, the WMI is not ADHD-specific: deficits appear in specific learning disorder, traumatic brain injury, and anxiety, undermining discriminant validity.

A 2025 study profiling WISC-IV performance in children with ADHD (PMC12467670) reinforced this picture: the Processing Speed Index showed the strongest ADHD-associated depression, slightly exceeding WMI effects, while VCI and PRI remained near age expectations. The pattern—low PSI and WMI, intact VCI—has been termed the "ADHD cognitive signature," though its specificity is contested.

---

3. NEPSY-II IN ADHD ASSESSMENT: EVIDENCE FOR WORKING MEMORY AND INHIBITION SENSITIVITY

The NEPSY-II provides direct neuropsychological probes not available in the WISC-V. Relevant subtests include: Word List Interference (phonological working memory), Narrative Memory, Memory for Designs (visuospatial memory), Auditory Attention and Response Set (sustained attention/response control), Inhibition (Go/No-Go paradigm), and Animal Sorting (concept formation/cognitive flexibility).

A 2025 study published in Children (MDPI; doi:10.3390/children12060798; PMC12191324) assessed 48 school-age children (ages 8-15) across three groups: ADHD (n=16), anxiety disorder (n=16), and comorbid ADHD + anxiety (n=16) using both WISC-IV and NEPSY-II. Key findings:

- No statistically significant group differences were found on WISC WMI, VCI, PRI, or PSI across the three groups, replicating the poor discriminant validity of Wechsler indexes noted above.
- The NEPSY-II Inhibition subtest (specifically the Inhibition-Switching condition measuring response time for inhibiting automatic responses) did differentiate groups: the anxiety-only group showed significantly more efficient inhibition than both ADHD and comorbid groups (p < .05), supporting NEPSY-II's incremental diagnostic value.
- Working memory subtests on the NEPSY-II (Word List Interference) did not reach significance as a differentiating variable in this sample, suggesting that inhibitory control, rather than working memory per se, is the more sensitive NEPSY-II marker for ADHD.

A structural analysis of NEPSY-II performance in children ages 7-12 (Child Neuropsychology, 2024; doi:10.1080/09297049.2024.2357376) examined the instrument's factor architecture using confirmatory factor analysis and found adequate fit for the six-domain model, though several working memory subtests showed cross-loadings onto executive function factors, indicating construct overlap that complicates interpretation.

---

4. ECOLOGICAL VALIDITY: A CENTRAL METHODOLOGICAL DEBATE

Ecological validity—the degree to which test performance predicts real-world functioning—is the most substantive criticism of both instruments, and the NEPSY-II has received specific scrutiny in this domain.

Pediatric neuropsychologists have criticized traditional batteries, including both WISC-V and NEPSY-II, for assessing cognitive skills in decontextualized, structured settings that do not replicate the demands of school or home environments. A 2024 study (PMC11353110) using the SmartAction-VR virtual reality paradigm to assess executive functioning in ADHD found that children with ADHD made significantly more errors in complex naturalistic tasks than neurotypical peers—but correlations between these errors and traditional tests (including Digit Span and Trail Making analogs) were modest. The authors explicitly noted that conventional batteries "evaluate specific skills in isolation, potentially overlooking interactions between different [executive functions]" and their relevance to daily activities.

Relative to the WISC-V, the NEPSY-II holds a modest ecological validity advantage. The NEPSY-II's Narrative Memory subtest (recalling stories), Auditory Attention subtest, and the Memory for Faces subtest approach real-world demands more closely than standardized IQ tasks. Furthermore, the NEPSY-II was validated with concurrent validity correlations to WISC-IV processing speed and language indexes (moderate correlations, r ≈ .30-.50 across relevant subtests), confirming convergent validity without suggesting equivalence. The NEPSY-II also uniquely includes a Social Perception domain (Affect Recognition, Theory of Mind) with direct school-functional implications, which the WISC-V entirely lacks.

However, peer reviewers of the NEPSY-II (Krieger, 2012, archived review; Korkman et al., 2007 standardization data; academic reviews via ResearchGate, 2021) consistently note that the NEPSY-II's ecological validity evidence base is primarily inferential rather than criterion-referenced: the instrument does not include validated behavior rating scale analogs or direct observational correspondence studies linking subtest scores to classroom or home functioning metrics. In contrast, instruments like the BRIEF-2 (Behavior Rating Inventory of Executive Function) or the Conners 3 provide direct ecological validity through parent/teacher correspondence.

---

5. CONFLICTING EVIDENCE AND SYNTHESIS

A fundamental tension runs through this literature: ADHD is behaviorally defined (DSM-5-TR) yet cognitively heterogeneous. Approximately 30-40% of children meeting diagnostic criteria for ADHD show no significant working memory deficit on standardized testing (Willcutt et al., 2005, extensively cited). This creates a structural ceiling on the sensitivity achievable by any cognitive battery, including both WISC-V and NEPSY-II. Neither instrument should be expected—or used—as a standalone diagnostic tool.

The evidence supports a tiered conclusion regarding relative sensitivity:

- For working memory deficits specifically, the WISC-V WMI offers more normatively robust, psychometrically precise measurement with stronger standardization data (n = 2,200 nationally representative), but lower ADHD-specific sensitivity due to construct non-specificity.
- The NEPSY-II's inhibition and attention subtests show greater ADHD-specific sensitivity, but its working memory subtests do not consistently outperform Wechsler WMI measures in controlled studies.
- Neither instrument demonstrates criterion ecological validity for predicting functional classroom impairment; the NEPSY-II holds a theoretical advantage in domain breadth, but this has not been translated into robust ecological validity coefficients.

---

6. RESEARCH GAPS

The literature reveals several underdeveloped areas warranting future investigation:

1. Direct sensitivity/specificity comparison studies pitting WISC-V WMI against NEPSY-II Word List Interference in age-stratified ADHD samples (8-12 specifically) are absent; most studies conflate wider age ranges.
2. No published study has provided criterion-referenced ecological validity data linking NEPSY-II subtest scores to objective classroom performance metrics (grade records, teacher behavior observations) in an ADHD sample.
3. The moderating role of ADHD subtype (predominantly inattentive vs. combined) on differential test sensitivity is insufficiently examined.
4. Cross-cultural and cultural minority validity (e.g., FinSwed study, Tandfonline, 2024) raises unresolved issues for NEPSY-II standardization generalizability.

---

CONCLUSION

For clinicians and researchers evaluating ADHD in children aged 8-12, the WISC-V provides psychometrically superior working memory measurement within a well-normed IQ framework, while the NEPSY-II contributes incremental diagnostic value primarily through inhibition and attention subtests rather than working memory per se. The NEPSY-II's theoretical ecological validity advantage over the WISC-V is real but empirically underdeveloped. A rigorous assessment protocol for publication-quality research should administer both instruments, supplement with validated behavior rating scales (BRIEF-2, Conners 3), and avoid overinterpreting cognitive test performance as sufficient for ADHD diagnosis in light of the known heterogeneity of the disorder's neuropsychological expression.

# WISC-V versus NEPSY-II for ADHD Diagnosis in Children Aged 8–12: Working Memory Sensitivity and Ecological Validity

## Introduction and Scope

The neuropsychological assessment of Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) in school-age children relies on two dominant paradigms: global cognitive batteries anchored to general intellectual ability, principally the Wechsler Intelligence Scale for Children, Fifth Edition (WISC-V; Wechsler, 2014), and domain-specific developmental neuropsychological batteries such as the NEPSY-II (Korkman, Kirk, & Kemp, 2007). These instruments differ fundamentally in their theoretical orientation, coverage breadth, and relationship to real-world functional impairment. For researchers publishing in clinical neuropsychology, understanding their comparative sensitivity for working memory (WM) deficits and their ecological validity profiles is essential.

## Theoretical Frameworks

The WISC-V is grounded in the Cattell-Horn-Carroll (CHC) psychometric model, organizing cognition into five primary index scales: Verbal Comprehension (VCI), Visual Spatial (VSI), Fluid Reasoning (FRI), Working Memory (WMI), and Processing Speed (PSI). The Ancillary Working Memory Index (AWMI) provides additional resolution. In contrast, the NEPSY-II is rooted in Lurian neuropsychological theory, organizing 32 subtests across six functional domains (Attention/Executive Functions, Language, Memory/Learning, Sensorimotor, Social Perception, Visuospatial Processing). This theoretical divergence is not merely academic: CHC-derived instruments emphasize latent cognitive factors, while NEPSY-II targets process-level domain functions more directly tied to frontal-subcortical circuitry implicated in ADHD.

## Working Memory Sensitivity: WISC-V

The WISC-V WMI, comprising Digit Span and Picture Span subtests, has been the most studied cognitive marker of ADHD. Multiple studies using earlier Wechsler editions confirmed WMI deficits in ADHD, and this signal has been replicated with the WISC-V. A 2021 study published in *Sustainability* (MDPI) examining cognitive profiles of children with ADHD and specific learning disorders using the WISC-V found consistently lower WMI scores compared to matched controls. The meta-analysis by Giofrè and colleagues published in *Archives of Clinical Neuropsychology* (2024, vol. 39, p. 498) confirmed across WAIS-IV and WISC-V samples that ADHD is associated with depressed WMI, with effect sizes in the moderate range (Cohen's d approximately 0.5–0.7).

However, critical appraisal reveals significant limitations. A 2024 rapid review published in *Applied Neuropsychology: Child* (Taylor & Francis, DOI: 10.1080/20590776.2024.2412336) examining WISC-V clinical utility in a public hospital ADHD assessment pathway concluded that "no clinically significant cognitive profile can be detected in ADHD using a WISC-V assessment that is sufficiently sensitive or specific to use for diagnostic purposes." The authors noted that WMI group-level differences, while statistically reliable, translate to poor individual-level diagnostic accuracy. Furthermore, a measurement invariance study (PMC10817318, 2024) found that reliability and replicability coefficients for the WISC-V group factors may be too low to warrant interpretation of primary index scores in isolation for ADHD populations. A separate investigation published in *Applied Neuropsychology: Child* (DOI: 10.1080/21622965.2023.2176232) found that WISC-IV WMI association with ADHD symptoms in 7–8-year-olds was significant at the group level but accounted for only modest variance in symptom severity.

The WISC-V's diagnostic sensitivity for WM deficits is thus best characterized as adequate for detecting group-level impairment but insufficient as a standalone clinical discriminator. Base rate considerations (WM deficits occur across numerous diagnostic categories including learning disabilities, anxiety, and trauma) substantially limit specificity.

## Working Memory and Inhibition Sensitivity: NEPSY-II

The NEPSY-II offers several subtests with direct relevance to ADHD: Auditory Attention and Response Set, Inhibition (Naming, Inhibition, and Switching conditions), Animal Sorting, Word List Interference (WLI), and Clocks. A 2025 study published in *Children* (MDPI, PMC12191324) examined 48 school-age children across ADHD-only, anxiety disorder-only, and ADHD-plus-anxiety-disorder groups using both WISC-IV and NEPSY-II. Key findings: no significant between-group differences emerged on standard cognitive or WM measures, but children with anxiety disorder demonstrated significantly more efficient inhibition on NEPSY-II Inhibition subtests compared to ADHD and ADHD+anxiety groups (p < .05). Importantly, omission errors on the NEPSY-II Auditory Attention subtest were negatively correlated with WISC-IV WMI (r = -.43 to -.51 across groups), demonstrating convergent validity between the instruments but also confirming that NEPSY-II process measures capture variance in attentional allocation not fully indexed by WMI.

The NEPSY-II's WLI subtest functions as a measure of verbal WM under interference conditions—arguably a more process-pure measure of the phonological loop and central executive than Digit Span. Preliminary evidence suggests WLI discriminates ADHD from typically developing children with moderate-to-large effect sizes, though large-scale sensitivity/specificity analyses specific to WISC-V-era normative comparisons remain limited in the published literature, representing a clear research gap.

## Ecological Validity: Comparative Analysis

The ecological validity debate is the most contested domain of comparison. The WISC-V, as a psychometric construct measure, makes no direct claim to ecological validity—its predictive relationship to real-world academic functioning is well-established but operates through general intelligence and specific cognitive factors rather than through direct behavioral simulation.

The NEPSY-II is sometimes assumed to possess superior ecological validity because of its Lurian theoretical roots and its finer-grained process decomposition. However, the empirical record is more equivocal. A 2022 narrative review in *Frontiers in Psychology* (DOI: 10.3389/fpsyg.2022.991699) examining executive function assessment in school-age children concluded that all performance-based tests, including NEPSY-II, "have very low or no ecological validity" and "are unlikely to correlate well with daily life activity ratings." The review acknowledged that NEPSY-II's 32-subtest coverage is "interesting and expanding" but emphasized that performance-based tests and behavior rating scales measure different constructs—rating scales demonstrating superior correspondence with everyday functional impairment. This finding aligns with Barkley's (2012) theoretical position that laboratory-based WM tasks measure a different construct than the dysexecutive syndrome observable in naturalistic settings.

A 2024 review in *Children* (PMC11353110) evaluating ecological validity of executive function assessments in ADHD explicitly framed virtual reality paradigms as necessary supplements because traditional neuropsychological batteries—NEPSY-II included—impose artificial structure that reduces demand on self-generated behavior, a core ADHD deficit. The NEPSY-II's structured, examiner-directed subtests thus share an ecological limitation with the WISC-V: both measure performance under optimal conditions, whereas ADHD manifests most severely under conditions of low external structure and low intrinsic motivation.

From a neuropsychologist practice perspective, the NEPSY-II holds a relative advantage in ecological face validity—its tasks are more age-appropriate and contextually grounded (e.g., narrative memory, clock drawing)—but this should not be conflated with empirically demonstrated predictive validity for real-world outcomes.

## Research Gaps and Methodological Considerations

Several gaps impede firm conclusions. First, head-to-head sensitivity/specificity analyses comparing WISC-V and NEPSY-II using receiver operating characteristic (ROC) methodology with confirmed ADHD samples aged 8–12 are lacking. Second, studies examining incremental validity—whether NEPSY-II adds diagnostic information beyond WISC-V alone—are rare and methodologically heterogeneous. Third, ecological validity has rarely been operationalized rigorously (e.g., using Ecological Validity Framework criteria or correlated with Vineland Adaptive Behavior Scales or teacher-rated daily functioning) in direct comparison studies. Fourth, subtype differentiation (ADHD-I vs. ADHD-C vs. ADHD-HI) introduces heterogeneity that confounds WM sensitivity analyses across studies.

## Conclusions and Clinical Synthesis

The WISC-V WMI detects group-level WM deficits in ADHD with consistency but lacks the individual-level diagnostic sensitivity and specificity to serve as a standalone discriminator. The NEPSY-II offers complementary process-level information—particularly through Inhibition, Auditory Attention, and WLI subtests—that captures dimensions of attentional and executive dysfunction not fully indexed by WMI alone. Neither instrument achieves ecological validity in the technically demanding sense: both correlate modestly with real-world adaptive functioning, and rating scales remain superior for this purpose.

For publication purposes, the consensus recommendation in the literature is a multimethod, multi-informant assessment framework combining cognitive testing (WISC-V or NEPSY-II, or both), behavior rating scales, clinical interview, and functional observation. The NEPSY-II's finer domain decomposition makes it a clinically richer instrument for characterizing neurocognitive profiles, but its superiority over the WISC-V for working memory sensitivity specifically remains empirically unresolved and constitutes a priority research gap.

# Cardiac Monitoring Strategy and Disposition for Recurrent Near-Syncope with AV Conduction Abnormality

## Clinical Interpretation

This 68-year-old man presents with a pattern that is no longer diagnostically ambiguous: three ED visits for near-syncope, two witnessed home episodes of transient pallor and aphasia lasting approximately 30 seconds, a resting heart rate of 52 bpm, and an ECG showing sinus bradycardia with a PR interval varying between 180 and 240 ms with occasional dropped beats. This constellation is consistent with Mobitz type I (Wenckebach) second-degree AV block, the diagnosis supported by the progressive PR prolongation before blocked beats. However, the critical management issue is that dropped beats accompanied by witnessed home episodes of altered consciousness and aphasia raise concern for higher-degree block (Mobitz II or paroxysmal complete heart block) that is not captured on the resting 12-lead.

A crucial and immediately actionable finding is his current medication: metoprolol 50 mg twice daily. Beta-blockers depress AV nodal conduction and are formally contraindicated in patients with second-degree AV block or a PR interval exceeding 240 ms per FDA labeling. His PR interval already reaches 240 ms at rest. Metoprolol is almost certainly exacerbating or precipitating his conduction abnormality and symptomatic episodes, and this must be addressed before discharge regardless of admission decision.

## Disposition: Admission is Warranted

Using established syncope risk stratification frameworks, this patient meets multiple high-risk features mandating admission rather than expedited outpatient evaluation:

**Hard admission criteria present:**
- Two witnessed episodes of transient loss of consciousness with pallor and inability to speak (consistent with cerebral hypoperfusion from transient high-degree block)
- Resting conduction disease on ECG with documented variable PR interval and dropped beats
- Current use of an AV nodal-blocking agent (metoprolol) that is pharmacologically driving or worsening the block
- Three ED visits in 30 days for the same complaint without definitive rhythm diagnosis
- A 6-week cardiology wait and two failed insurance appeals for outpatient monitoring — the infrastructure for safe outpatient monitoring does not exist

The Canadian Syncope Risk Score (CSRS), validated across multi-center Canadian ED cohorts, assigns higher risk to patients with abnormal ECG findings, predisposing cardiac disease, and repeated presentations. High-risk CSRS patients carry approximately 20% 30-day serious adverse event rates; very high-risk patients approach 50% [REBEL EM validation summary]. The ESC 2018 syncope guidelines and ACC/AHA 2019 bradycardia/conduction guidelines both identify symptomatic AV block as a condition warranting inpatient rhythm monitoring until the cause is determined and a management plan secured.

**Threshold distinguishing admission from expedited outpatient care:** Outpatient evaluation is appropriate for confirmed Mobitz I in asymptomatic patients with no structural heart disease, no AV nodal-blocking agents, and a reliable monitoring plan in place. This patient meets none of those criteria. The aphasia during episodes also raises the differential of transient ischemic attack or Adams-Stokes attack from complete heart block — either requires urgent evaluation.

## Monitoring Strategy

### Immediate In-Hospital Steps

1. **Discontinue or dose-reduce metoprolol.** Given the ECG findings and symptoms, holding or reducing metoprolol is a priority. If hypertension control remains necessary, transition to a non-AV-nodal agent (e.g., amlodipine or an ARB) after cardiology consultation. Do not discharge on unchanged metoprolol dose.

2. **Continuous telemetry monitoring during admission.** The inpatient setting provides real-time rhythm capture during any symptomatic episode. Nursing should be instructed to capture a rhythm strip with any symptom report.

3. **Echocardiogram.** Structural heart disease (cardiomyopathy, infiltrative disease such as sarcoidosis or amyloid, ischemic changes) is a critical cause of acquired AV block in this age group. Troponins have been repeatedly negative, but structural assessment is essential.

4. **Electrophysiology or cardiology consultation in-house.** Six-week outpatient waits are not acceptable for a patient with symptomatic complete or near-complete AV block; most hospitals can arrange urgent inpatient cardiology review.

### Longer-Term Monitoring if Discharged After Initial Stabilization

If the patient is stabilized (metoprolol modified, no high-degree block documented, hemodynamically well) and discharge is ultimately planned, the following hierarchy applies based on symptom frequency:

- **Symptoms more than once weekly:** 24–48-hour Holter (lowest barrier, often approvable despite prior denials when new ECG findings are documented). However, given episodes occur roughly twice in recent weeks, this may not capture infrequent but dangerous events.
- **Symptoms roughly weekly:** External patch monitor worn for 14 days (e.g., Zio Patch or equivalent). These devices have outperformed standard Holter monitoring in arrhythmia detection and are available without real-time transmission, lowering cost and insurance friction. Diagnostic yield for patch monitors over two weeks ranges 33–80% depending on device and auto-trigger capability [NIH StatPearls, 2024].
- **Mobile Cardiac Outpatient Telemetry (MCOT):** For 30 days with real-time transmission, MCOT offers the highest diagnostic yield (auto-trigger detection up to 72–80%) and can escalate to urgent response if a dangerous rhythm is captured. Given failed Holter appeals, a letter documenting new 12-lead ECG findings of variable AV block and witnessed near-syncopal episodes with aphasia constitutes materially changed clinical grounds for resubmission, often successfully.
- **Implantable Loop Recorder (ILR):** If external monitoring is repeatedly denied or fails to capture a diagnosis, an ILR (e.g., Medtronic LINQ, Abbott Confirm Rx) provides up to 3 years of continuous monitoring. In syncope patients where a rhythm was captured, ILR detected bradyarrhythmias in 42% of cases [StatPearls, NCBI]. ILR is particularly indicated when episodes are infrequent and the patient cannot self-activate a recorder during syncope.

### Insurance Denial — Clinical Response

The two prior Holter denials were presumably based on insufficient documentation. The new 12-lead ECG showing Wenckebach pattern with PR variation from 180 to 240 ms and dropped beats, plus two documented home episodes with witnessed aphasia, constitutes a substantially changed clinical picture. The treating physician should write a peer-to-peer appeal citing: (1) documented second-degree AV block on ECG, (2) recurrent transient loss of consciousness with neurological symptoms, (3) three ED visits in 30 days, and (4) inability to safely defer monitoring given the absence of a cardiology appointment within a clinically acceptable window. Most payers' own policies authorize ambulatory monitoring for documented AV conduction disease with syncope.

## Summary of Thresholds

| Condition | Action |
|---|---|
| Symptomatic Mobitz I or II with witnessed syncope/aphasia | Admit for telemetry |
| Metoprolol with PR >240 ms or second-degree block | Hold/reduce before discharge |
| Hemodynamically unstable or complete heart block found | Cardiology/EP urgent consult; may need temporary pacing |
| Stable after medication change, no high-degree block on telemetry | Discharge with MCOT or 14-day patch; expedited cardiology within 1–2 weeks via urgent referral pathway |
| Repeated monitoring failure or very infrequent serious episodes | ILR implantation |

This patient should be admitted today. Disposition to outpatient management without first modifying his metoprolol and securing a definitive monitoring or evaluation plan would leave an identifiable, pharmacologically-modifiable conduction abnormality unaddressed in a patient who has already had multiple near-syncopal and syncopal events.

**Cardiac Monitoring Strategy and Admission Thresholds for Symptomatic AV Conduction Disease with Recurrent Near-Syncope**

**Clinical Interpretation of the Presenting ECG**

This patient's ECG — sinus bradycardia at 52 bpm with intermittent dropped beats and a PR interval ranging from 180 to 240 ms — is consistent with second-degree AV block, Mobitz Type I (Wenckebach phenomenon). The hallmark is progressive PR prolongation culminating in a non-conducted P wave, after which the cycle resets. The variable PR interval (60 ms range) is the key distinguishing feature from Mobitz Type II, in which the PR interval is fixed before the dropped beat. The site of block in Wenckebach is almost always at the AV node itself, reflecting a functional rather than structural conduction defect — important because AV nodal block is more reversible and less likely to progress suddenly to complete heart block than infranodal (His-Purkinje) disease. [StatPearls, NCBI](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK482359/); [LITFL](https://litfl.com/av-block-2nd-degree-mobitz-i-wenckebach-phenomenon/)

However, the framing of this case as "probably benign Wenckebach" would be premature and clinically hazardous given three compounding factors: (1) three ED presentations with hemodynamically significant symptoms, (2) witnessed episodes of pallor and 30-second unresponsiveness at home consistent with transient loss of consciousness (TLOC), and (3) an untreated pharmacologic contributor — metoprolol 50 mg twice daily.

**The Metoprolol Problem**

Metoprolol is formally contraindicated in second- or third-degree AV block without a functioning pacemaker. The drug's negative chronotropic and dromotropic effects act directly at the AV node and may be precipitating or worsening the observed block. At heart rate 52 with symptomatic dropped beats, continued metoprolol is not appropriate. Current guidance is to hold or reduce the dose immediately when symptomatic bradycardia (HR <50–60 with presyncope or syncope) develops, with dose reduction of ~50% rather than abrupt cessation to avoid rebound adrenergic effects, particularly if the patient has underlying coronary disease. [Medscape Metoprolol Reference](https://reference.medscape.com/drug/lopressor-toprol-xl-metoprolol-342360); [NCBI StatPearls Metoprolol](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK532923/) The hypertension indication should be transitioned to an agent that does not suppress AV conduction — amlodipine or a long-acting dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker are appropriate alternatives pending cardiology evaluation.

**Admission vs. Expedited Outpatient: Thresholds**

The 2018 ACC/AHA/HRS Guideline on Bradycardia and Cardiac Conduction Delay is the governing framework. [Circulation Executive Summary](https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/10.1161/CIR.0000000000000627); [PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30586771/) Several features in this case cross the threshold for monitored inpatient admission:

**Admit to monitored unit (telemetry floor minimum) if ANY of the following is present:**
- Symptoms attributable to bradycardia that are hemodynamically significant — presyncope or frank syncope. This patient has had five such episodes (three ED visits, two home episodes).
- Transient loss of consciousness (TLOC) with witnessed pallor and aphasia, even if self-terminating — these events cannot be safely evaluated on a 48-hour or even 30-day outpatient timeframe when recurrence risk is high.
- Heart rate persistently below 50 bpm with symptoms.
- Second-degree AV block in the setting of a reversible pharmacologic cause that has not yet been removed and whose removal may itself trigger rebound tachycardia or reveal higher-degree block.
- No confirmed diagnosis after three ED visits — repeated discharge without a definitive rhythm correlation is a system failure, not a reassurance.

StatPearls specifically states: "All patients with [Mobitz Type I] require admission to a monitored unit." [NCBI StatPearls](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK482359/) While this represents a conservative stance and many asymptomatic incidental Wenckebach findings do not require admission, this patient is overtly symptomatic and has been incompletely evaluated over a month. Admission is warranted.

**Expedited outpatient (within 48–72 hours, not 6-week wait) would be acceptable only if:**
- The patient were asymptomatic with incidentally discovered mild Wenckebach and no TLOC
- Hemodynamics were completely stable and HR were >50 at rest
- A clear reversible cause were already addressed (e.g., metoprolol already held and heart rate normalizing)
- Same-week cardiology follow-up were guaranteed

None of these conditions apply here.

**Monitoring Strategy Before or During This Encounter**

*If admitted (recommended):*
Continuous inpatient telemetry on a monitored floor achieves immediate arrhythmia correlation. During admission, initiate:
- Metoprolol hold with heart rate and blood pressure reassessment every 4–6 hours
- 12-lead ECG in the morning and after any symptomatic episode
- Echocardiogram to evaluate for structural disease, infiltrative cardiomyopathy (e.g., sarcoid, amyloid — underappreciated causes of conduction disease in older men), or wall motion abnormality
- Cardiology consultation during the admission (bypassing the 6-week outpatient wait)
- If the block persists or worsens after metoprolol removal, electrophysiology study (EPS) may be indicated to characterize the level of block (nodal vs. infranodal) and pacemaker candidacy

*If somehow not admitted (not recommended, but addressing the insurance reality):*
A 14–30-day ambulatory patch monitor (e.g., Zio Patch, Biotel) is superior to a standard 24-hour Holter for this presentation — symptoms occurring less frequently than daily make the Holter's 24-48 hour window almost certain to miss events. [Carelon Clinical Guidelines 2024](https://guidelines.carelonmedicalbenefitsmanagement.com/ambulatory-cardiac-rhythm-monitoring-2024-07-01/) Mobile Cardiac Outpatient Telemetry (MCOT) adds real-time transmission and is appropriate when symptoms are infrequent but high-risk (presyncope, TLOC); it is medically necessary when prior shorter monitoring was non-diagnostic and events occur less than every 48 hours.

The pending Holter appeal should be escalated to request a 14-day patch monitor or MCOT instead, with the clinical rationale that: (a) symptoms occur with less than daily frequency, making 24-hour Holter non-diagnostic by design, and (b) the patient has had witnessed TLOC, placing this in the high-risk syncope category that justifies telemetry-capable monitoring. Many insurers cover MCOT for presyncope/syncope with prior non-diagnostic workup. [FEP Blue Medical Policy 2026](https://www.fepblue.org/-/media/PDFs/Medical-Policies/2026/January/Medical-Policies/Remove-and-Replace/20208-Ambulatory-Event-Monitors.pdf)

**Pacemaker Candidacy**

Per the 2018 ACC/AHA/HRS guideline, permanent pacing for symptomatic Mobitz I carries a Class IIa recommendation (Level of Evidence C-LD) when symptoms are clearly attributable to the block. [ECGWaves](https://ecgwaves.com/topic/ecg-second-degree-av-block-mobitz-type-1-2-wenckebach/) If metoprolol withdrawal does not resolve the block and symptoms persist, or if EP study reveals infranodal (His-Purkinje) disease — which can masquerade as Wenckebach morphology — a Class I pacemaker indication applies. This distinction requires electrophysiology input and is another argument for inpatient consultation rather than a 6-week cardiology wait.

**Summary of Recommended Actions**

1. Admit to monitored telemetry unit — symptomatic TLOC with unexplained second-degree AV block after three prior ED visits is not safely managed with further outpatient deferral.
2. Hold metoprolol immediately; transition hypertension management to amlodipine.
3. Order echocardiogram during admission to evaluate for structural or infiltrative disease.
4. Obtain inpatient cardiology/electrophysiology consultation — this circumvents the 6-week outpatient delay.
5. If admission is refused by the patient or found otherwise unworkable: arrange 14-day patch monitor or MCOT with explicit prescription documentation of "recurrent near-syncope and witnessed TLOC with second-degree AV block, prior workup non-diagnostic, Holter denied" and expedited cardiology review within 5–7 days, not 6 weeks.
6. Counsel the patient and daughter on return precautions: any episode of sustained loss of consciousness, chest pain, dyspnea, or heart rate below 40 warrants immediate return.

**Monitoring Strategy and Disposition for Recurrent Near-Syncope with Wenckebach-Pattern AV Block in an Elderly Patient on Metoprolol**

---

**Clinical Summary and ECG Interpretation**

This 68-year-old man presents with three ED visits for recurrent near-syncope, accompanied by two witnessed episodes of transient pallor and aphasia lasting approximately 30 seconds. His ECG today demonstrates sinus bradycardia at rate 52, with PR interval variation of 180–240 ms and occasional dropped beats. This pattern is diagnostic of **second-degree AV block, Mobitz type I (Wenckebach)**—a progressively lengthening PR interval followed by a non-conducted P wave, cycling repetitively. His metoprolol 50 mg twice daily is a probable contributory factor: beta-blockers slow AV nodal conduction and are a well-recognized cause of Wenckebach block, particularly in patients with underlying conduction disease (StatPearls, NCBI Bookshelf; Sfairopoulos et al., *Journal of Cardiovascular Electrophysiology*, 2025).

The critical clinical issue is that his symptoms are not trivial. Two home episodes of pallor with transient aphasia lasting 30 seconds raise concern for hemodynamically significant pauses causing cerebral hypoperfusion, potentially misclassified as TIA at a prior visit. This is not incidental Wenckebach.

---

**Admission vs. Expedited Outpatient Evaluation**

**This patient meets criteria for admission.** Published guidance from StatPearls states that "all patients with Mobitz I block should be admitted and monitored," with the threshold lowered substantially by the presence of symptoms. Using the **San Francisco Syncope Rule (SFSR)**—which identifies patients at short-term risk of serious outcome using abnormal ECG, history of CHF, dyspnea, hematocrit <30%, or systolic BP <90 mmHg—this patient triggers on ECG abnormality (rate-dependent AV block). The **ROSE rule** (BNP, ECG changes, occult GI bleeding, oxygen saturation, elevated hemoglobin) would similarly identify an abnormal ECG as a high-risk marker. (MDCalc; WikEM).

Thresholds warranting **inpatient admission** in this case:
- Symptomatic Wenckebach with recurrent syncope or near-syncope (met)
- Witnessed transient neurological symptoms (aphasia) suggesting cerebral hypoperfusion (met)
- Heart rate of 52 with progressive PR prolongation and dropped beats (met)
- The causative drug (metoprolol) cannot yet be confirmed as the sole explanation without monitoring off the drug
- Outpatient follow-up timeline (6-week cardiology wait) is clinically unacceptable given symptom burden

Thresholds for **expedited outpatient evaluation instead of admission** would require all of the following: (a) Wenckebach that is incidentally found and truly asymptomatic; (b) resting rate >55 with no symptoms; (c) reliable daily contact and ability to activate emergency services; (d) near-immediate ambulatory monitoring in place; and (e) no structural heart disease. This patient meets none of those criteria.

---

**Immediate Medication Management**

Metoprolol should be held or dose-reduced before discharge or during hospitalization. Beta-blockers are established causes of Wenckebach in susceptible individuals, and drug discontinuation resolves AV block in approximately 60% of drug-induced cases (Sfairopoulos et al., 2025; JACC 2004). However, an important caveat from recent literature is the concept of "drug-revealed" versus "drug-induced" block: when AV block occurs on standard doses of a nodal-blocking agent (not supratherapeutic levels), it often indicates underlying infranodal conduction disease that would eventually manifest independently. Symptom resolution after stopping metoprolol does not eliminate the need for further evaluation; ongoing monitoring is still required to determine whether intrinsic conduction disease persists.

For hypertension management while metoprolol is held, an alternative agent without AV nodal effects—such as a dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker (amlodipine) or an ARB—should be initiated.

---

**In-Hospital Monitoring Strategy**

During admission, the patient should be placed on **continuous telemetry** to capture the frequency, duration, and hemodynamic significance of pauses and dropped beats. Particular goals:
1. Quantify longest RR pause (pauses >3 seconds, or symptomatic pauses >2 seconds, are standard pacemaker referral thresholds per ACC/AHA)
2. Assess whether rhythm normalizes after metoprolol discontinuation within 24–48 hours
3. Rule out progression to higher-degree block (Mobitz II or complete heart block), which would mandate urgent permanent pacing
4. If block persists off metoprolol after 48 hours, electrophysiology consultation should be obtained

If the block resolves and symptoms abate after drug withdrawal, the patient may be considered for **expedited outpatient ambulatory monitoring** prior to cardiology follow-up.

---

**Ambulatory Monitoring Strategy**

Given the denied Holter authorization, two practical alternatives should be pursued simultaneously:

**1. Extended-wear patch monitor (14-day continuous recording)**
Devices such as the ZIO Patch (iRhythm) record continuously for up to 14 days without patient activation. This is superior to standard 24-hour Holter for capturing infrequent arrhythmias. Importantly, patch monitors use a different billing code than standard Holter monitors (CPT 93241–93248 for extended external ECG) and may bypass the current Holter denial. The appeals team or case manager should reframe the appeal using this code and citing symptomatic Wenckebach with transient neurological events as the indication (Carelon Clinical Guidelines, 2024; FEP Medical Policy, 2024).

**2. Mobile Cardiac Outpatient Telemetry (MCOT)**
For patients with episodic, potentially life-threatening arrhythmias and negative prior monitoring, MCOT provides real-time ECG transmission with a monitoring center that can alert providers to critical arrhythmias immediately. The ACC/AHA/HRS 2017 Syncope Guideline (Class IIa recommendation) supports extended ambulatory monitoring in patients with syncope suspected to be arrhythmic in origin. MCOT is particularly justified here given the home episodes and the safety risk of waiting 6 weeks. Most major payers cover MCOT for unexplained syncope with abnormal ECG (Dean Care policy; Premera Medical Policy 2.02.510).

**3. Implantable loop recorder (ILR) as fallback**
If external monitoring fails to capture a diagnostic event and symptoms recur, an ILR (e.g., Reveal LINQ) provides 3-year monitoring. ACC 2025 expert consensus on arrhythmia monitoring supports ILR for select patients where shorter-term monitoring is non-diagnostic. Insurance prior authorization requires documentation of prior external monitoring attempts, so the current denial record is actually supportive evidence for an ILR appeal.

---

**Pacemaker Referral Thresholds**

Per ACC/AHA device guidelines, the following findings during monitoring would prompt permanent pacemaker implantation (Class I indication):
- Symptomatic sinus node dysfunction or AV block causing heart rate <40 bpm or pauses >3 seconds while awake
- Progression to Mobitz II or third-degree (complete) AV block regardless of symptoms
- Persistence of Wenckebach with symptoms after removal of all offending drugs

A cardiology appointment in 6 weeks is too delayed given this trajectory. The admitting team should place an **urgent inpatient electrophysiology consult** or arrange expedited cardiology follow-up within 5–7 days of discharge if admission is deferred—not the standard 6-week new-patient slot.

---

**Summary of Recommended Actions**

| Priority | Action |
|---|---|
| Immediate | Admit to telemetry; hold metoprolol; substitute non-nodal antihypertensive |
| In-hospital | Continuous rhythm monitoring; measure longest pause; EP consult if Wenckebach persists off drug at 48h |
| Before discharge | Arrange 14-day patch monitor (new insurance code) or MCOT; expedited cardiology within 5–7 days |
| If discharged prematurely | Low threshold to return for presyncope, near-syncope, or palpitations; discuss ILR if monitoring denied again |

# Monitoring Strategy and Admission Criteria for Recurrent Near-Syncope with Symptomatic Bradycardia and Variable AV Conduction

## Interpretation of the Clinical Picture

This patient presents with a pattern that demands urgent reconsideration of his disposition. Three ED visits for near-syncope over one month, combined with two witnessed home episodes of transient pallor and aphasia lasting ~30 seconds, constitute recurrent symptomatic presyncope with a high pre-test probability for an underlying bradyarrhythmia. The ECG finding—sinus bradycardia at 52 bpm with occasional dropped beats and a PR interval varying between 180 and 240 ms—is most consistent with Wenckebach second-degree AV block (Mobitz Type I), defined by progressive PR prolongation culminating in a non-conducted P wave. However, the "occasional dropped beats" without further detail could also represent Mobitz Type II or even intermittent high-degree block, which carries a far worse prognosis.

The distinction between Mobitz I and Mobitz II is clinically critical: Mobitz I occurs at the AV node (typically benign, responsive to atropine, vagally mediated), while Mobitz II is infranodal, structurally based, and at high risk of progression to complete heart block. Any QRS widening, bundle-branch block morphology, or abrupt dropped beats with a fixed preceding PR would point toward Mobitz II and mandate different management. If the bedside ECG or rhythm strip is not definitively diagnostic, a 12-lead obtained during a pause, or a longer rhythm strip, should be reviewed before finalizing the plan.

## Drug Contribution: Metoprolol as Likely Precipitant or Revealer

Metoprolol 50 mg twice daily is a high-priority pharmacological suspect. Beta-blockers suppress sinus node automaticity, prolong AV nodal conduction, and are formally contraindicated in patients with second- or third-degree AV block and sick sinus syndrome [StatPearls, NCBI NBK532923]. A 2022 review in *Medicina* found that beta-blockers are more likely to be associated with sinus bradycardia, sinus pauses, and sick sinus syndrome than with severe AV block—but critically, a 2025 PMC publication emphasizes that these drugs may "reveal" rather than purely "cause" underlying conduction system disease, with high recurrence rates of AV block even after drug discontinuation [PMC12246518]. This has significant management implications: metoprolol reduction or discontinuation is appropriate and may produce partial or complete resolution, but the patient's intrinsic conduction system should be viewed with suspicion regardless. An alternative antihypertensive that does not suppress conduction (e.g., amlodipine, losartan) should be considered before discharge or arranged expeditiously. Metoprolol should not be abruptly discontinued in patients with coronary artery disease without a plan, but dose reduction is reasonable and defensible here.

## Monitoring Strategy Before Discharge

The failed Holter monitoring authorization creates a practical gap, but several alternatives exist:

**1. Mobile Cardiac Outpatient Telemetry (MCOT) as First-Line Alternative**
MCOT patch monitors (e.g., Zio Patch, iRhythm, BioTel) provide continuous ambulatory ECG recording for up to 14–30 days without requiring patient activation. In an ED-based study (Annals of Emergency Medicine, 2022), MCOT initiated at discharge detected at least one significant arrhythmia in 71.8% of patients with syncope/presyncope, with symptomatic events captured in 84.6% [Annals of Emergency Medicine, S0196-0644(22)00772-7]. Median compliance exceeds 98%. This should be the primary discharge monitoring plan. Insurance authorization for MCOT is a distinct billing pathway from Holter monitoring (CPT codes 93228–93229 for real-time MCOT versus 93224–93227 for Holter), so the Holter denial does not necessarily carry over. A prior authorization request for MCOT should be initiated from the ED, emphasizing recurrent syncope/presyncope with documented ECG abnormalities.

**2. External Loop Recorder (Patient-Activated, 30 Days) as a Secondary Option**
If MCOT is not immediately obtainable, an external loop recorder (patient-activated, 30-day monitor) is a validated alternative. A prospective randomized trial found symptom-rhythm correlation in 56% of patients with loop recorders versus 22% with Holter monitoring [PubMed 12867227]. However, because this patient may be unable to reliably self-activate a device during a syncopal event (he becomes briefly unresponsive), a passive/auto-trigger device is preferred over a manually activated patient event monitor.

**3. Implantable Loop Recorder (ILR) as Escalation**
Per the 2018 ACC/AHA/HRS Guideline on Bradycardia and Cardiac Conduction Delay [Circulation, CIR.0000000000000628], ILR implantation is reasonable for patients with infrequent symptoms (>30-day intervals) in whom noninvasive monitoring has been nondiagnostic. Given the insurance battles and recurrent symptoms, an ILR referral should be flagged to cardiology as a priority consideration if MCOT is also denied. The procedure is performed under local anesthesia and provides monitoring for up to 3 years.

## Admission Versus Expedited Outpatient Evaluation: Specific Thresholds

The 2018 ACC/AHA/HRS guideline and emergency medicine literature support the following decision framework:

**Thresholds that warrant admission (inpatient telemetry monitoring):**
- Confirmed Mobitz Type II or high-degree AV block (2:1 or 3:1 block) on any rhythm strip [StatPearls NBK482359; ACC/AHA/HRS 2018]
- QRS duration ≥120 ms or bundle-branch block accompanying the AV conduction abnormality
- Heart rate sustained below 40 bpm or documented asystolic pauses >3 seconds
- Hemodynamic compromise: hypotension, altered mental status, or pre-syncope with hemodynamic instability during monitoring
- Active near-syncope in the ED or witnessed syncopal episode during the current visit
- Failure to improve after metoprolol dose reduction/hold

**Thresholds supporting expedited outpatient evaluation (within 72 hours, not 6 weeks):**
- Confirmed Mobitz Type I (Wenckebach) with PR variability as observed here, normal hemodynamics, heart rate ≥50 bpm, and preserved QRS morphology
- Stable vital signs and no active ischemia (negative troponins, normal chest X-ray, normal electrolytes confirmed)
- MCOT monitoring arranged and confirmed before leaving the ED
- Reliable family at home with ability to call 911 immediately for loss of consciousness

**This patient's specific disposition:** Given the three recurrent visits, two home episodes of apparent loss of consciousness (which exceed the threshold of presyncope into possible syncope with spontaneous recovery), and the fact that the arrhythmia has not yet been definitively characterized as Mobitz I versus II, **admission for inpatient telemetry is the more defensible choice.** The ACC/AHA/HRS guideline supports hospital monitoring for patients with symptomatic bradycardia when the etiology is uncertain and symptoms are recurrent. Inpatient admission would allow: (a) telemetry characterization of the dropped beats, (b) supervised metoprolol dose reduction, (c) electrophysiology or cardiology consultation without a 6-week wait, and (d) determination of whether symptoms resolve with drug modification alone.

## Immediate Actions Before Discharge or During Admission

- Obtain a prolonged rhythm strip or repeat 12-lead ECG to better characterize the dropped beats (fixed vs. varying PR before the dropped beat)
- Reduce metoprolol to 25 mg twice daily or hold one dose under observation; note heart rate response
- Initiate MCOT or inpatient telemetry order
- Place urgent cardiology consultation (not routine outpatient referral), citing recurrent syncope and second-degree AV block with medication interaction
- Document the home syncopal episodes formally in the medical record to support prior authorization appeals and justify higher level of care

# Cardiac Monitoring and Disposition Strategy for Symptomatic Bradycardia with Variable PR Interval and Recurrent Near-Syncope

## Clinical Synthesis

This 68-year-old man presents with a convergence of findings that together constitute a high-risk syndrome, despite each element appearing individually manageable: three ED visits for near-syncope, a heart rate of 52 bpm, a PR interval fluctuating between 180 and 240 ms with dropped beats, metoprolol 50 mg twice daily, and two witnessed home episodes of transient pallor and aphasia lasting approximately 30 seconds. The ECG pattern — progressive PR prolongation culminating in a non-conducted P wave — is consistent with second-degree AV block, Mobitz Type I (Wenckebach), occurring at a nodal level and most often benign in isolation. However, the symptom burden here changes the risk calculus entirely.

## Medication Review: Metoprolol as a Probable Contributor

The first and most immediately actionable step is to hold or substantially reduce the metoprolol. Beta-blockers suppress both sinus node automaticity and AV nodal conduction; at 100 mg/day in an elderly patient, metoprolol can exacerbate or unmask underlying conduction disease. Published case series confirm that drug-induced high-degree AV block in older patients on beta-blockers often resolves within 48–72 hours of discontinuation [PMC11856498]. The current resting heart rate of 52 and PR variability up to 240 ms are consistent with drug-augmented nodal suppression superimposed on intrinsic conduction system disease. Alternative hypertension management (e.g., a dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker, or an ARB if no contraindication) should be arranged before discharge.

Thyroid function (TSH 2.4), electrolytes, hemoglobin, and creatinine are all normal, effectively ruling out reversible metabolic or endocrine causes. The workup gap is rhythm documentation during a symptomatic event.

## Risk Stratification and Admission Criteria

The 2017 ACC/AHA/HRS Syncope Guidelines (the most comprehensive available, still referenced in 2024 EUSEM consensus) specify that patients with suspected **cardiac etiology** of syncope warrant hospital admission for continuous monitoring (Class I recommendation). High-risk features mandating admission include: abnormal ECG, structural heart disease, prior MI, suspected arrhythmic etiology, and recurrent syncope without diagnosis [PMC7252783].

This patient meets **multiple** of those criteria:
- Abnormal ECG (Mobitz I with PR prolongation reaching 240 ms and dropped beats)
- Recurrent, undifferentiated syncope/near-syncope (three ED visits in one month plus two home events)
- Transient neurological symptoms during home episodes ("couldn't talk for 30 seconds") — raising the additional question of whether these represent seizure, posterior circulation TIA, or vagally mediated cerebral hypoperfusion; this must be distinguished from pure cardiac syncope and warrants urgent neurology input if not already obtained
- Age 68 with a confounding AV-nodal-suppressing medication
- No confirmed diagnosis despite three evaluations

The San Francisco Syncope Rule (SFSR) flags high risk for abnormal ECG alone, with a sensitivity of 74–98% for serious 7-day outcomes in validation studies [MDCalc, AAFP 2012]. The ROSE rule additionally factors in BNP elevation, which has not been checked here; obtaining BNP is advisable.

**This patient should be admitted.** Discharging him again, as occurred three times previously, without an established diagnosis or rhythm documentation is clinically and medicolegally untenable given the escalating event frequency and home neurological episodes.

## In-Hospital Monitoring Strategy

Upon admission, the standard of care is **continuous telemetry** for the duration of the admission. The priority diagnostic objectives are:

1. **Capture a symptomatic rhythm** — any episode of pallor, presyncope, or loss of consciousness must be immediately correlated with a rhythm strip.
2. **Characterize the block** — confirm whether this is true Mobitz I (progressive PR lengthening before a drop), Mobitz II (constant PR with sudden non-conducted P wave), or high-degree block (≥2:1 conduction), as the latter two carry substantially worse prognosis and near-automatic pacemaker indications.
3. **Assess nocturnal bradycardia** — overnight telemetry frequently reveals pauses or high-degree block not apparent on resting 12-lead ECG.
4. **Serial 12-lead ECGs** at admission and if any symptomatic episode occurs.
5. **Echocardiogram** to assess structural disease, ventricular function, and infiltrative/inflammatory etiologies (e.g., cardiac sarcoidosis, which can cause AV block in this age group at a rate disproportionate to its rarity).

If the patient refuses admission or is clinically stable on telemetry with no progression and an identified reversible cause (i.e., metoprolol clearly held and HR/PR interval normalizes within 12–24 hours), a monitored 23-hour observation stay is an acceptable alternative per ACC/AHA guidelines before reassessing.

## Outpatient Monitoring: Bypassing the Holter Deadlock

If the patient is ultimately dispositioned to outpatient care (only if clinically stable, medication adjusted, and symptoms clearly improving), the standard Holter is the wrong tool — Holter monitoring is appropriate for symptoms occurring daily; this patient's events are weekly to every-few-days. The repeated insurance denials for Holter monitoring may actually be rendered moot by this distinction.

The appropriate monitoring class for infrequent symptomatic episodes is **extended ambulatory ECG monitoring**:

- **Patch monitor (e.g., Zio Patch, iRhythm)**: 14-day continuous recording, auto-triggered. Multiple payer policies (UHC, BCBS, Aetna) specifically cover patch monitors for syncope when symptoms occur less than daily — a different billing code than Holter (CPT 93243/93247) that may not be subject to the same denial [UHC Policy, Aetna CPB 0073]. The appeal team should pivot to requesting a 14-day patch monitor under CPT 93247 rather than continuing to appeal the Holter denial.
- **Mobile Cardiac Outpatient Telemetry (MCOT)**: Real-time transmitted monitoring for 30 days, with a monitoring center that can call 911 if a critical arrhythmia is detected. This is the highest-yield outpatient option for syncope of suspected cardiac etiology and is covered under CPT 93228/93229 by most major insurers for syncope without confirmed diagnosis [Carelon Guidelines 2024, Blue Cross NC Policy].
- **Implantable Loop Recorder (ILR)**: ESC guidelines recommend ILR early in evaluation for recurrent unexplained syncope — if outpatient monitoring does not yield a diagnosis within one monitoring cycle, ILR insertion (subcutaneous, 3-year battery) should be discussed at first cardiology appointment [PMC7252783].

## Expedited Cardiology Access

The 6-week new-patient wait is unacceptable given this risk profile. Avenues to accelerate access:
- Request **urgent/same-week cardiology consultation** via the admitting team while inpatient (bypasses outpatient scheduling entirely).
- If discharged, the ED physician should document "high-risk syncope with undiagnosed AV block" and call the cardiology office directly to request an **urgent referral slot** (typically held for such cases at most academic and community centers).
- An **electrophysiology (EP) referral** may be more appropriate than general cardiology given the conduction abnormality, and EP tends to have different scheduling queues.

## Summary of Thresholds

| Threshold | Action |
|---|---|
| Symptomatic (syncope/near-syncope) with Mobitz I and age >65 | Admit for telemetry and further evaluation |
| Neurological symptoms during episodes (aphasia, focal deficit) | Emergent neurology consult; consider brain MRI/DWI |
| Any Mobitz II or high-degree (2:1 or 3:1) block on ECG/telemetry | Cardiology/EP urgent consult; pacemaker evaluation (Class I indication) |
| Pause >3 seconds on telemetry or HR <40 | Atropine readiness; urgent EP consult |
| PR normalizes, HR recovers after metoprolol held, no symptoms in 24h | Discharge with 14-day patch or MCOT, expedited cardiology within 1–2 weeks |
| Recurrent events despite metoprolol discontinuation | ILR implantation discussion |

The 2019 ACC/AHA/HRS Pacing Guidelines assign a **Class IIa recommendation** to permanent pacing for symptomatic Mobitz I with symptoms clearly attributable to the AV block [ACC/AHA 2019]. If this patient's near-syncope is confirmed arrhythmic in origin — a determination telemetry is designed to make — permanent pacing becomes a near-certain next step.
