# The Bubble Gum Run 5K at California's Great America

## Background: California's Great America

California's Great America is a major regional amusement park located in Santa Clara, California, in the heart of Silicon Valley. Originally developed by the Marriott Corporation and opened in 1976, the park changed ownership several times over the decades — passing from Marriott to Kings Entertainment, then to Paramount Parks, and eventually to Cedar Fair in 2006. In 2022, Cedar Fair announced an agreement to sell the land underlying the park to the City of Santa Clara for stadium-adjacent development, and the park formally closed to the public after the 2023 season, making it one of the most prominent theme park closures in recent California history.

During its operational life, California's Great America hosted a variety of special events, including races and fun runs that took advantage of the expansive park grounds — an increasingly popular format that allowed participants to run through amusement parks before or after regular operating hours.

## The Race: Bubble Gum Run 5K

The 5K race hosted at California's Great America that featured "bubble gum" in its title was called the **Bubble Gum Run**. Multiple references to this event appear consistently across social media searches, with Instagram page titles such as "Bubble Gum Run Great America Santa Clara," "Bubble Gum 5K Run Great America Santa Clara," and "Bubble Gum 5K Great America California" all pointing to the same event. The event was also referenced under the closely related name "Bubble Gum Dash 5K Great America," suggesting that over the years or across different editions, the race may have carried slight title variations, but "Bubble Gum Run" emerges as the most commonly cited form.

The event was part of a broader trend in the 2010s of themed, family-friendly fun runs held at amusement parks. These park-based races were typically non-competitive or lightly timed, and participants often received perks such as park admission tickets, finisher medals, and themed merchandise. California's Great America hosted several such events over its history, including the well-documented Peanuts Run & Ride (held in December 2019), which offered distances ranging from a 1-Mile Fun Run to a Half Marathon and even a Snoopy Loopathon endurance event, all winding through the park's themed areas before the gates opened to the general public.

The Bubble Gum Run followed a similar model, using the park grounds as the race course and incorporating a festive, candy-themed identity aimed at families and casual runners.

## Context: Theme Park Races in the Bay Area

The Bubble Gum Run at Great America was consistent with a wave of novelty 5K events that proliferated across the United States in the 2010s. Events like Color Runs, Foam Runs, Mud Runs, and bubble-themed races attracted participants who were less focused on competitive times and more interested in the experience. Amusement parks proved ideal venues because they offered established pathways, distinctive backdrops, and built-in entertainment infrastructure.

California's Great America, with its roughly 100-acre footprint in Santa Clara, provided a compact but visually engaging course. Running past roller coasters, midway games, and themed lands gave participants a behind-the-scenes feel that simply running a street race could not replicate.

## The Park's Closure and Legacy

The closure of California's Great America after the 2023 season marked the end of nearly five decades of operation. The Santa Clara stadium district — home to Levi's Stadium, where the San Francisco 49ers play — had long eyed the surrounding land for development, and the sale and subsequent closure ended the park's ability to host further events of any kind, including races like the Bubble Gum Run.

Events like the Bubble Gum Run now stand as a part of the park's legacy, representative of the creative programming the park undertook in its later years to attract guests beyond its traditional roller-coaster-and-ride audience. For many local runners, these park events served as an accessible, low-pressure introduction to running events, wrapped in a nostalgic setting.

## Conclusion

The 5K race held at the old California's Great America theme park in Santa Clara that contained "bubble gum" in its title was the **Bubble Gum Run** (also referred to in some sources as the Bubble Gum 5K or Bubble Gum Dash 5K). It was a themed fun run held on the park's grounds, consistent with the broader trend of novelty amusement-park races that characterized the 2010s running-event landscape. With the park's permanent closure after 2023, the Bubble Gum Run joins the roster of events that can no longer be held at the site.

# Nature's Castles: The Treehouse Reimagined at Longwood Gardens (2008)

## Overview

In spring 2008, Longwood Gardens in Kennett Square, Pennsylvania, unveiled "Nature's Castles: The Treehouse Reimagined," an exhibition of three artisan-crafted treehouse structures embedded within the gardens' mature tree canopy. After approximately four months of construction, the installation opened to the public and attracted hundreds of thousands of visitors. The total project cost was roughly $1 million — comparable, as multiple contemporary accounts noted, to building three conventional homes. The structures remained popular enough that Longwood extended the exhibition beyond its original run.

## Designers and Architects

The project was a three-way collaboration involving a Philadelphia-area architectural firm and two nationally recognized treehouse builders.

**Matthew Millan Architects** served as the lead architectural design firm for all three structures. The firm's portfolio pages for each treehouse describe how Matthew Millan Architects partnered with Longwood Gardens and "two premier treehouse companies in the U.S." to realize the vision. Millan's office provided overall architectural coherence and site-specific design direction, ensuring the three structures read as a unified exhibition while responding to their individual woodland settings.

The two builder-collaborators were:

- **TreeHouse Workshop** (Seattle, Washington), founded in 1997 by **Pete Nelson** and **Jake Jacob**. This firm built two of the three structures: The Birdhouse and The Canopy Cathedral.
- **Forever Young Treehouses** (Burlington, Vermont), a nonprofit organization founded in 2000 with a mission of creating treehouses accessible to children in wheelchairs. Forever Young built The Lookout Loft, the largest of the three structures.

The division of labor — an architectural firm working alongside craft-focused treehouse builders — was a deliberate design strategy. Millan's office provided formal and conceptual grounding while the builders contributed specialized knowledge of elevated timber construction.

## The Three Structures

**The Lookout Loft** is the largest of the three, at approximately 900 square feet. Designed in a rustic Adirondack style and built by Forever Young Treehouses, it is distinguished by being fully ADA-accessible, featuring 75 feet of ramps connecting a series of pavilions rather than stairs. Matthew Millan Architects described the spatial logic as a "progression from open ramp to more enclosed pavilions" that guides a visitor's attention "from the forest floor up to the canopy above and then outwards through the tree trunks and towards a farm field to the East." Four primary wood species — northern white cedar, hop hornbeam, spruce, and ipe — were selected for durability, workability, and varied tactile texture.

**The Birdhouse** is the smallest and highest of the three — 255 square feet in area and perched approximately 20 feet off the ground. Built by TreeHouse Workshop, its design concept references steel forest fire lookout towers and a bluebird box suspended among the trees. The compact, elevated form offers a bird's-eye view of tree canopies and the surrounding landscape.

**The Canopy Cathedral** is the most architecturally ambitious structure. Also built by TreeHouse Workshop, it draws inspiration from Norwegian stave churches — historic medieval timber-frame churches found throughout Scandinavia. It is a two-story structure with a 589-square-foot interior, defined by hand-adzed posts, a glazed gable with diamond-patterned windows, reclaimed Douglas fir timbers, cedar planking with an oiled finish, and a wraparound balcony with indentations cut to accommodate the trunks of the 100-year-old tulip poplars that surround it. The balcony commands views over the Italian Water Gardens and Longwood's Large Lake.

## Construction Approach and Sustainability

A defining constraint was Longwood's insistence that none of the structures be fastened directly to the trees. All three were instead built on elevated steel support systems: pipes resting on small concrete blocks, forming what engineers call a pin foundation. This system was deliberately positioned to avoid root zones and minimize soil compaction, protecting the mature trees that formed both the structural context and the aesthetic subject of the exhibition.

Materials were selected and sourced with sustainability in mind. The Canopy Cathedral's reclaimed Douglas fir and the varied wood species used in the Lookout Loft both reflect Longwood's institutional commitment to environmental stewardship. The entire project was framed as a celebration of Longwood's great trees — the same trees that had inspired Pierre du Pont to purchase the land in 1906 — and the treehouses were conceived as a means of drawing visitors into direct physical relationship with the canopy.

## Contemporaneous Coverage

The Washington Post published a feature article on October 2, 2008 (originally dated October 1, 2008) titled "Artisan Treehouses Extend Their Lease at Longwood Gardens," which describes the exhibition, its designers, the three structures, the construction methodology, and Longwood's decision to extend the run of the installation. This article — available in the Post's online archive at `https://www.washingtonpost.com/wp-dyn/content/article/2008/10/01/AR2008100100715.html` — constitutes a contemporaneous source written while the exhibit was actively on display. **"Nature's Castles: The Treehouse Reimagined" at Longwood Gardens (2008)**
**Design, Architecture, and Construction**

---

In the spring of 2008, Longwood Gardens — the celebrated 1,077-acre horticultural showplace in Kennett Square, Pennsylvania — unveiled one of its most unusual and ambitious visitor experiences: "Nature's Castles: The Treehouse Reimagined." The installation comprised three architecturally distinct treehouse structures nestled among the Gardens' historic champion trees, each designed to blend craft, nature, and narrative into a wholly original visitor attraction. The project was a collaboration between a Philadelphia-area architectural firm and two of the country's foremost treehouse-building specialists.

---

**The Architect: Matthew Millan Architects**

The design of all three treehouses was the work of **Matthew Millan Architects**, a firm with deep roots in institutional and experiential design. Millan Architects served as the overarching design authority for "Nature's Castles," conceiving each structure to respond specifically to its individual setting, the surrounding trees, and the interpretive stories those trees could tell. As stated in the firm's own project documentation, the approach centered on creating structures that "respond to their individual settings and offer different interpretive" opportunities — marrying nature appreciation with architectural excellence rather than treating the treehouses as generic playground installations.

Critically, the firm did not build the structures themselves. Matthew Millan Architects worked in concert with **two premier treehouse construction companies** to execute the designs. For the Canopy Cathedral and the Birdhouse, the builders were **Peter Nelson and Jake Jacobs of TreeHouse Workshop**, based in Seattle, Washington — among the most recognized treehouse artisans in the United States. The third structure, the Lookout Loft, was built by **Forever Young Treehouses** of Burlington, Vermont, which handled that project's particular demands around universal accessibility and ADA compliance.

---

**Design Concept and Driving Inspiration**

The broader initiative was rooted in Longwood Gardens' multi-year campaign to celebrate the estate's significant historic trees — the very trees that originally motivated industrialist Pierre S. du Pont to purchase the land in 1906. The treehouses were conceived not merely as playful novelties but as interpretive structures that would draw visitors' attention upward and outward, into the canopy itself, while telling the ecological and cultural story of the trees they occupied.

Each treehouse was designed around a specific tree or grove, a specific visitor experience, and a specific architectural vocabulary. The overarching philosophy was that the three structures should together form a coherent visitor journey while individually offering distinct aesthetic and sensory encounters.

A key sustainability principle ran through all three designs: to avoid damaging the living trees, all of the houses were **anchored to pipes set in the ground** rather than bolted or screwed directly into the trees. Construction materials leaned heavily toward salvaged and reclaimed timber, consistent with Longwood Gardens' broader sustainability commitments.

---

**The Three Structures**

**The Canopy Cathedral** is the largest and most visually dramatic of the three treehouses. Centered on towering tulip poplars, it draws its architectural language from **Norwegian stave churches** — the medieval timber-frame structures of Scandinavia, characterized by elaborately carved wooden frames, steep pitched roofs, and intricate joinery. The Canopy Cathedral's interior measures approximately 589 square feet across two stories and features reclaimed Douglas fir timbers, cedar planking finished with an oil that produces what the architects describe as "a warm, rich glow," hand-adzed posts supporting the porch roof, and diamond-shaped windows. An expansive wrap-around balcony accessed by dual staircases frames views of the Gardens' Large Lake and Italian Water Gardens. Peter Nelson and Jake Jacobs of TreeHouse Workshop executed the construction.

**The Birdhouse** takes its design cues from **historic steel forest fire towers** — the tall, utilitarian observation platforms once used by forest rangers across the American wilderness. Positioned around the trunk of a large tulip poplar, a spiraling staircase winds upward around the tree, creating a deliberate interplay between height, increasing distance from the ground, and intimate proximity to the living wood. The Birdhouse is specifically sited to tell an ecological story about the life cycle of trees: visitors can observe a healthy tulip poplar, a standing hollow snag (a dead tree still providing wildlife habitat), and a decomposing trunk on the ground below — all within the same view. Design details include organic-shaped door pulls and live-edge wooden benches that encourage tactile engagement with the natural materials. This structure was also built by TreeHouse Workshop.

**The Lookout Loft** is the most accessibility-focused of the three and the one most oriented toward universal visitor inclusion. Inspired by **Adirondack-style rustic architecture**, it employs a series of ramps and open pavilions that ascend gradually through the tree canopy rather than relying on stairs. The structure is fully compliant with the Americans with Disabilities Act, making it accessible to visitors of all ages and physical abilities — a deliberate design choice that 75 feet of ramps help achieve. Four wood species — Northern white cedar, hop hornbeam, spruce, and ipe — were incorporated, each selected for its durability and aesthetic properties, and their varied textures were intentionally exposed to encourage tactile exploration by visitors. Forever Young Treehouses of Burlington, Vermont, was responsible for its construction.

---

**Contemporaneous Coverage**

The Washington Post covered the project at the time of its opening, with an article published on **October 2, 2008**, titled "Artisan Treehouses Extend Their Lease at Longwood Gardens." The piece documented the four-month construction timeline, the involvement of Matthew Millan Architects alongside TreeHouse Workshop and Forever Young Treehouses, and specific design details including the ADA-compliant ramp system and the Norwegian stave church inspiration for the Canopy Cathedral. The article noted the sustainability rationale for ground-anchoring the structures rather than attaching them to the trees, and it described the interpretive intention behind the placement and design of each structure. This contemporaneous source (Washington Post, October 2, 2008) provides primary documentation of the design concept and construction approach as understood at the time of the opening.

Additional contemporaneous coverage appeared in regional publications including *Main Line Today*, which ran a feature ("Lofty Digs") describing the structures' construction and design philosophy.

---

**Summary**

The designer of the three treehouses in Longwood Gardens' "Nature's Castles: The Treehouse Reimagined" (2008) was **Matthew Millan Architects**, collaborating with **TreeHouse Workshop** (Seattle) for the Canopy Cathedral and the Birdhouse, and **Forever Young Treehouses** (Burlington, Vermont) for the Lookout Loft. The design concept centered on site-specific interpretive architecture — each structure given its own historical architectural vocabulary (Norwegian stave church, fire tower, Adirondack cabin) while sharing a commitment to sustainability through ground-anchoring and salvaged materials. The most accessible contemporaneous source documenting these details is the *Washington Post* article of October 2, 2008.

**"Nature's Castles: The Treehouse Reimagined" at Longwood Gardens (2008)**

**Designer and Architectural Team**

The three treehouses that constituted Longwood Gardens' 2008 exhibition "Nature's Castles: The Treehouse Reimagined" were designed by Matthew Millan Architects in collaboration with two specialist treehouse construction firms: Forever Young Treehouses of Burlington, Vermont, and Treehouse Workshop (also rendered as "TreeHouse Workshop") of Seattle, Washington, whose principals included Peter Nelson and Jake Jacobs. **Contemporaneous Source**

A contemporaneous article published April 24, 2008 in the Lancaster Online (LancasterOnline.com), titled "Treehouses get the Longwood Gardens treatment," provides detailed coverage of the exhibit's opening and construction. The Pittsburgh Post-Gazette also ran a contemporaneous piece — Gretchen McKay's "Castles in the Air — At Longwood Gardens, Fantastic Treehouses Grow," dated July 5, 2008 — offering a second independent 2008 source.

**Design Concept and Construction Process**

The broader curatorial premise for the exhibition was a celebration of Longwood Gardens' great trees, specifically the ancient specimens whose magnificence had originally inspired founder Pierre du Pont to purchase the Kennett Square, Pennsylvania estate in 1906. The three treehouses were conceived not merely as whimsical play structures, but as architecturally serious, material-rich structures that would direct adult visitors' attention to the scale, ecology, and sensory texture of the forest canopy. Construction took approximately four months and the structures opened to the public in spring 2008, remaining on view through November 23, 2008.

A key design and engineering decision across all three structures was that none of them is literally bolted into living trees. To avoid damaging root systems or trunks, all three treehouses are anchored to steel columns and beams driven into the ground — a structural approach that allowed Longwood's arboricultural staff to protect the very trees the exhibit was meant to honor. The Lancaster Online article records that the Canopy Cathedral alone incorporates 37 steel columns, 34 steel beams, and 8 steel braces. Materials across all three structures were chosen primarily from salvaged or reclaimed sources, consistent with Longwood's sustainability program.

**The Lookout Loft**

Designed by Matthew Millan Architects and constructed by Forever Young Treehouses, the Lookout Loft is the largest of the three at roughly 900 square feet, rising to 14 feet and located along the shaded Forest Walk with views eastward over a farm field. Its design concept is explicitly Adirondack rustic in character: rough-hewn, tactile, and emphatically accessible. The structure features 75-plus feet of gently inclined ramps shaped to resemble tree limbs rather than institutional ramps, enabling universal access in compliance with the Americans with Disabilities Act — a deliberate move to allow visitors of all ages and physical abilities to experience an elevated forest perspective typically unavailable to non-climbers. Wood species employed include northern white cedar, hop hornbeam (harvested from Vermont), spruce, and Brazilian ipe, chosen for durability, workability, and visual warmth. Interpretive signage throughout educates visitors on forest ecology and the distinctive properties of each wood species.

**The Birdhouse**

Built by Treehouse Workshop and the tallest of the three at more than 20 feet, the Birdhouse is centered on a large living tulip poplar in a tulip tree grove and covers approximately 255 square feet — the most compact of the trio. The design concept, as described in the 2008 Lancaster Online article, draws on the vernacular of steel fire lookout towers and interprets the life cycle of a tree from seedling through mature canopy to eventual decay. Visitors ascend a winding staircase that spirals around the tulip poplar's trunk, bringing them into close tactile contact with living bark at increasing heights; beside the structure stands a dead standing snag and a decaying fallen trunk, completing the ecological narrative. Interior details include live-edge wooden benches and organically shaped door hardware. Materials were reclaimed from a dairy barn and a chocolate factory.

**The Canopy Cathedral**

The Canopy Cathedral, the most architecturally elaborate of the three, draws its formal vocabulary from Norwegian stave churches — medieval timber-frame structures characterized by interlocking vertical planks and steeply pitched gabled roofs. Matthew Millan Architects and Treehouse Workshop located it near Longwood's Italian Water Garden, where it commands views over the Large Lake. The two-story structure covers 589 square feet on the main floor plus a 118-square-foot wrap-around balcony accessible by a pair of external staircases. Key features include hand-adzed posts, a grand gable window, and diamond-shaped secondary windows. Primary timber materials — reclaimed Douglas fir from logs salvaged after Oregon's 2002 wildfires, and lumber from a dismantled Toronto toothpaste factory — were sourced before design was finalized; the Millan Architects team first scouted the site during a February 2007 snowstorm. Oiled cedar planking and the fir timbers together produce what the Millan Architects portfolio describes as "a warm, rich glow."

# "Nature's Castles: The Treehouse Reimagined" at Longwood Gardens (2008)
## Architecture, Design, and Construction

### Background and Overview

In the spring of 2008, Longwood Gardens — the world-renowned horticultural showplace in Kennett Square, Pennsylvania — opened "Nature's Castles: The Treehouse Reimagined," an exhibition of three extraordinary treehouse structures built among the Gardens' historic tulip poplars and other mature trees. The project was part of Longwood's multi-year campaign to celebrate the great trees that had inspired Pierre du Pont's original 1906 land purchase. After approximately four months of construction, the treehouses opened to visitors and ran through November 23, 2008. Total cost for the three structures was approximately $1 million. [Lancaster Online, April 24, 2008]

### The Architectural Team: A Three-Way Collaboration

The project was designed through an unusual and deliberate collaboration between three parties: **Matthew Millan Architects**, a Pennsylvania-based firm, served as the lead architect coordinating the overall vision and ensuring each structure responded to its individual setting. Millan's practice worked alongside two specialized treehouse-building companies — each assigned to different structures based on their particular expertise and aesthetic sensibility. [Millan Architects portfolio pages; Washington Post, October 2008]

**Forever Young Treehouses**, based in Burlington, Vermont, was responsible for the Lookout Loft. The company, founded in 2000 by Bill Allen, had built 26 accessible treehouses in 17 states for children in wheelchairs; its experience with universally accessible design made it the natural choice for the one structure required to meet ADA standards.

**TreeHouse Workshop, Inc.**, based in Seattle, designed and built the other two structures: the Birdhouse and the Canopy Cathedral. The firm was founded in 1997 by **Pete Nelson** and **Jake Jacobs**, who had already established a reputation for highly crafted, architecturally ambitious arboreal buildings. Jacobs recounted the initial site visit: "We walked through the gardens on a snowy February day and we could see the tall tulip trees without their leaves. These trees sort of jumped out at us and Pete immediately began sketching." That visit took place in February 2007, roughly a year before construction began. [Search results citing Lancaster Online]

### The Three Structures

**1. The Lookout Loft**

The Lookout Loft is a 900-square-foot, Adirondack-style structure that rises to 14 feet at its highest point, with two separate viewing platforms. Designed by Bill Allen / Forever Young Treehouses and detailed by Millan Architects, it is the only one of the three explicitly engineered for universal accessibility, featuring more than 75 feet of ramps — shaped to evoke tree limbs — that lead visitors from the forest floor up through the canopy to views over adjacent meadows and farm fields. The material palette highlights multiple wood species: Vermont hop-hornbeam, northern white cedar, spruce, and Brazilian ipe were selected for their durability, workability, and contrasting textures, with signage educating visitors about the role of each species. [Millan Architects, Lookout Loft page; Lancaster Online, April 24, 2008]

**2. The Birdhouse**

The smallest and highest of the three at 255 square feet and more than 20 feet off the ground, the Birdhouse was designed and built by TreeHouse Workshop. The design concept drew on the imagery of a bluebird box perched in the forest canopy, and on the visual language of steel fire lookout towers. A spiraling staircase winds around the trunk of a large tulip poplar, giving climbers an intimate encounter with tree bark while simultaneously experiencing the increasing distance to the ground. Organic design details — hand-carved door pulls, live-edge slab benches — complement recovered materials: wood from an old dairy barn and a chocolate factory were incorporated into the structure, and a prominent barn door features a handle fashioned from a rhododendron branch. Near the entrance, a standing hollow snag and a decaying log were deliberately included to illustrate the full arc of tree life from growth through decomposition. [Millan Architects, Birdhouse page; Lancaster Online, April 24, 2008]

**3. The Canopy Cathedral**

The largest and most architecturally imposing structure, the Canopy Cathedral was designed by TreeHouse Workshop and inspired by historic Norwegian stave churches — timber-framed medieval structures known for their steep gabled roofs and intricate joinery. At two stories, with a 589-square-foot main interior and an attached 118-square-foot wrap-around balcony, it is the centerpiece of the exhibition, positioned near Longwood's Italian Water Garden with a prominent gable window framing views of the Large Lake. Hand-adzed posts support the porch roof, and the interior showcases reclaimed Douglas fir timbers and cedar planking with an oiled finish that creates, in the architects' description, "a warm, rich glow."

The structural engineering details are notable: the building rests on 37 steel columns, 34 steel beams, and 8 steel braces anchored to pipes driven into the ground — a system that deliberately avoids penetrating or loading the living trees, consistent with Longwood's sustainability commitments. Materials throughout were heavily reclaimed: Douglas fir timbers came from logs salvaged after the 2002 Oregon wildfires, and the floors were crafted from Douglas fir recovered from a dismantled toothpaste factory in Toronto. Diamond-shaped windows in the glazed gable façade echo the decorative vocabulary of Norwegian church architecture. [Millan Architects, Canopy Cathedral page; Lancaster Online, April 24, 2008]

### Engineering Philosophy and Sustainability

A distinguishing feature of all three treehouses is that none of them is truly "in" the trees in the traditional sense. Rather than bolting platforms to living trunks, all three structures are anchored to steel pipes driven into the ground, with the trees growing through or beside the buildings. This approach protected the century-old tulip poplars from structural loading and damage, and reflected Longwood's broader sustainability ethic. Where tree trunks pass through decks or walls, the openings are sized generously to accommodate continued growth. The extensive use of salvaged and reclaimed wood — from wildfire timber, a dairy barn, a chocolate factory, and a toothpaste factory — further reduced the environmental footprint of what is, in aggregate, a substantial timber construction project. [Lancaster Online, April 24, 2008; Millan Architects portfolio]

### Contemporaneous Source

The most detailed contemporaneous account is an article published by **Lancaster Online on April 24, 2008** — well within the exhibition year — which covered the opening of the treehouses, described all three structures, identified the builders (Forever Young Treehouses and TreeHouse Workshop), and provided dimensions, materials, and the project timeline. The **Washington Post** also published a substantive article on October 1, 2008, under the headline "Artisan Treehouses Extend Their Lease at Longwood Gardens," which confirmed builder attributions and provided additional construction details. Both articles appeared while the exhibition was still running.

## Nature's Castles: The Treehouse Reimagined at Longwood Gardens (2008)

### Overview

In spring 2008, Longwood Gardens in Kennett Square, Pennsylvania, unveiled "Nature's Castles: The Treehouse Reimagined," a trio of handcrafted arboreal structures set within the Gardens' historic tree collection. The exhibition opened on Arbor Day weekend, with the structures on view through November 23, 2008. The project grew out of a multi-year Longwood initiative to draw attention to the great trees on the property — trees whose significance traces back to Pierre du Pont's 1906 land purchase that established the Gardens.

### The Design Team

The creative team behind the three treehouses was a deliberate collaboration between an architect of record and two specialist treehouse-building firms widely regarded as among the best in the country.

**Matthew Millan Architects** (based in Chadds Ford, Pennsylvania) served as the architect of record for all three structures. Millan's firm coordinated the structural and engineering requirements needed to bring each design into compliance with building codes, while working closely with the two treehouse specialists to ensure each structure responded to its individual site and offered distinct interpretive opportunities. Millan Architects describe their role as one of "commitment to design excellence, collaboration with talented artisans, and enhancing people's connections with the natural world."

The two treehouse construction firms were:

- **Treehouse Workshop** (Seattle, Washington), founded by Pete Nelson and Jake Jacob, designed and built two of the three structures — the Canopy Cathedral and the Birdhouse.
- **Forever Young Treehouses** (Burlington, Vermont), a nonprofit organization founded in 2000, designed and built the third structure, the Lookout Loft, with a specific emphasis on universal accessibility.

### The Three Structures

**1. The Canopy Cathedral**

The largest and most architecturally elaborate of the three, the Canopy Cathedral measures 589 square feet on the main floor with an additional 118-square-foot balcony. It stands more than ten feet high across two stories and is sited near Longwood's Italian Water Garden, affording views of both the Water Garden and the Large Lake.

The design was inspired by Norwegian stave churches — a longtime dream of Pete Nelson of Treehouse Workshop, who had long wanted to build a treehouse modeled on traditional Scandinavian ecclesiastical architecture. The structure is built from reclaimed Douglas-fir timbers sourced from two unusual origins: salvaged logs from Oregon wildfire-damaged forests and dismantled timbers from a Toronto toothpaste factory. Hand-adzed posts support a porch roof, and the cedar planking carries an oiled finish that gives the interior a warm, rich glow. Structurally, the Canopy Cathedral relies on 37 steel columns, 34 steel beams, and 8 steel braces. Design work began in February 2007, making the full project timeline roughly 14 months from initial design to public opening.

**2. The Birdhouse**

The smallest (255 square feet) but tallest (more than 20 feet off the ground) of the trio, the Birdhouse was also designed and built by Treehouse Workshop. The design concept takes its cue from the steel forest fire towers that once dotted the American landscape, offering an elevated vantage point amid the tree canopy.

A winding staircase ascends around a large tulip poplar trunk, creating what the Millan Architects site describes as "an intimate encounter with the tree bark" as visitors climb. The Birdhouse uses reclaimed wood recovered from an old dairy barn and a chocolate factory, and features a barn door fitted with a rhododendron branch as a handle. The structure's ecological narrative threads through the design: visitors encounter a healthy tulip poplar, a hollow dead snag, and a decaying log, tracing the full life cycle of a tree from living growth through forest-floor decomposition. Organic door pulls and live-edge slab benches complete the tactile, naturalistic character of the space.

**3. The Lookout Loft**

Designed and built by Forever Young Treehouses, the Lookout Loft is sited past Pierce House and overlooks a meadow. It rises to a height of up to 14 feet and is distinguished above all by its accessibility: more than 75 feet of ramps connect to three successive platforms, making the structure reachable by visitors of all ages and physical abilities — a "universally accessible" design in the language of the project team.

The material palette reflects an Adirondack rustic aesthetic: Vermont hop-hornbeam and Brazilian ipe wood, some pieces shaped to suggest branching tree limbs, complement wrought iron and metal accents. Multiple wood species — also including northern white cedar and spruce — were selected both for durability and to offer visitors a tactile survey of different wood types and forms. The ramp sequence is designed to gradually shift visitor attention from the forest floor upward to the canopy.

### Construction Philosophy and Structural Approach

A significant design constraint governed all three structures: none of them rests directly on or through living trees. All three use pin foundations nestled among tree groupings. This approach protects the root zones and trunks of the historic trees — a non-negotiable requirement given Longwood's stewardship mission — while still achieving the visual and experiential impression of structures genuinely embedded in the tree canopy.

Patricia Evans, Longwood's Communications Manager, described the ensemble as offering "a little more rustic and relaxed" atmosphere, representing "a different kind of wow from nature than you get in the designed gardens" — an intentional counterpoint to the formal horticultural displays for which Longwood is known.

Designer presentations were held on the Saturday following opening, from 11 a.m. to 2 p.m., allowing visitors to hear directly from the creators about the design process.

# Nature's Castles: The Treehouse Reimagined at Longwood Gardens (2008)

## Overview

In spring 2008, timed to coincide with Arbor Day, Longwood Gardens in Kennett Square, Pennsylvania debuted "Nature's Castles: The Treehouse Reimagined," a permanent outdoor exhibition comprising three architecturally distinct treehouse structures. The project represented a significant horticultural and architectural undertaking: the treehouses collectively took approximately four months to construct and cost around $1 million — comparable to building a traditional home. Two separate firms were engaged to design and build the three structures, each bringing a philosophically distinct approach to treehouse craft.

---

## The Designers

### TreeHouse Workshop (Seattle, Washington)

The lead creative force behind two of the three structures was **TreeHouse Workshop**, a Seattle-based firm founded in 1997 by **Jake Jacob** and **Peter Nelson**. The partnership united complementary skills: Jacob brought technical design expertise while Nelson, a master builder, had already established himself as a leading voice in the field — he authored *Treehouses: The Art and Craft of Living Out on a Limb* (1994), a foundational text in the discipline. By 2008, TreeHouse Workshop had completed approximately 40 structures, working primarily with recycled and reclaimed materials.

Jacob and Nelson's design methodology begins with close observation of the site. For Longwood Gardens, the process started when the two walked the grounds on a snowy February day — the bare winter trees revealing the garden's tall tulip poplars in their full structural form. As Jacob later recounted, the trees "sort of jumped out at us and Pete immediately began sketching." From those initial sketches, components were fabricated in their Seattle shop before being assembled on-site. Their guiding structural philosophy is encapsulated in the phrase **"perch don't pin"**: wherever possible, structures rest on engineered support systems rather than being bolted directly into the living wood, allowing trees to grow and sway naturally without damage. Where attachment to living wood is unavoidable, the firm uses specialized **Garnier Limb (GL) anchor bolts** — designed to function as artificial limbs — spaced 12 to 18 inches apart to minimize disruption to nutrient channels within the bark.

TreeHouse Workshop designed and built two structures for Longwood:

**1. The Canopy Cathedral**
The centerpiece of the exhibition, the Canopy Cathedral is an ornate two-story structure centered on a stand of towering tulip poplars. Its design was inspired by **Norwegian stave churches** — the medieval Scandinavian timber-frame ecclesiastical buildings famous for their intricate joinery and dramatically vertical massing. The interior encompasses 589 square feet across two floors, with diamond-shaped windows admitting filtered light through the canopy. The flooring was crafted from Douglas fir salvaged from a decommissioned toothpaste factory in Toronto, embodying the firm's commitment to reclaimed materials. A 118-square-foot balcony, reached by two interior staircases, provides an elevated view across Longwood's celebrated Italian Water Garden below.

**2. The Birdhouse**
A short distance down the path, nestled within another grove of tulip trees, stands the Birdhouse — the smallest of the three structures at 255 square feet but the tallest in elevation, rising more than 20 feet off the ground. Its compact, vertical form evokes its avian namesake, and like the Canopy Cathedral, it was built primarily from salvaged materials.

### Forever Young Treehouses (Burlington, Vermont)

The third structure, the **Lookout Loft**, was designed and constructed by **Forever Young Treehouses**, a Burlington, Vermont–based organization whose central mission is to build fully accessible treehouses — one in every U.S. state — so that people of all physical abilities can experience elevated woodland environments. Their work at Longwood put this mission on full display.

The Lookout Loft is the largest of the three structures at approximately 900 square feet of combined platform space. Located past the Pierce House and overlooking an open meadow, it is a rustic **Adirondack-style** structure featuring three ascending platforms connected by more than 75 feet of graduated ramps meeting ADA (Americans with Disabilities Act) accessibility standards. The platforms reach as high as 14 feet above ground level. Design elements include a white cedar trellis canopy made from substantial hand-selected logs (9-inch upright posts supporting 4-inch top logs), a curved shingled roof, and individual tree openings that allow living trunks to pass through the deck without constraint.

Forever Young's construction philosophy shares the environmental ethic of TreeHouse Workshop but approaches it differently. Their structures **do not use trees for structural support at all** — neither anchoring to trunks nor resting on branches. The Lookout Loft sits on **34 Pin Foundations (Diamond Piers)**: precast concrete heads with angled steel bearing pins driven into the ground, which eliminate the need for conventional concrete footings or any tree contact. Materials are sourced locally to Vermont whenever possible, and the firm prioritizes "recovered, reclaimed, or repurposed wood, to reduce the need to cut living trees." A signature element of their process is walking the forest to find naturally curved or twisted branches that fit specific structural needs — what the firm describes as "one of our favorite parts of the process."

---

## Shared Design Principles

Despite the stylistic differences between the three structures, all of them reflect commitments central to Longwood Gardens' own conservation values. No structure was bolted directly into a living tree for primary support; instead, each used engineered ground-anchoring or the specialized attachment hardware developed specifically for tree-sensitive construction. All three were built predominantly from salvaged or reclaimed materials — the Canopy Cathedral's Toronto factory flooring being the most dramatic example. The overall project cost of approximately $1 million and a four-month construction timeline underscore that these were not decorative garden follies but serious works of architecture, designed to endure as permanent features of the landscape.

---

## Contemporaneous Source

The most detailed contemporaneous account of the exhibition's design and construction comes from a Lancaster Online article titled **"Treehouses get the Longwood Gardens treatment"** (lancasteronline.com), published in 2008 to coincide with the opening. This article names both firms, identifies Peter Nelson and Jake Jacob of TreeHouse Workshop by name, describes the Norwegian stave church inspiration for the Canopy Cathedral, documents the salvaged-material sourcing, and provides key dimensions for all three structures. A Star Stuff Life blog post dated July 14, 2008, provides additional granular detail on the Lookout Loft's construction specifics, including the Diamond Pier foundation system and Forever Young's material philosophy, drawing on Longwood Gardens' own exhibition materials and on-site documentation. A 2008 Seattle Daily Journal of Commerce profile of TreeHouse Workshop (djc.com) provided background on the firm's founding, philosophy, and the "perch don't pin" methodology, though it did not cover the Longwood project specifically.

---

## Summary

The three treehouses of "Nature's Castles: The Treehouse Reimagined" were the work of two firms: **TreeHouse Workshop** of Seattle (co-founders Peter Nelson and Jake Jacob), responsible for the Canopy Cathedral and the Birdhouse; and **Forever Young Treehouses** of Burlington, Vermont, responsible for the accessible Lookout Loft. Multiple contemporaneous 2008 sources document the project's design concepts — particularly the Norwegian stave church inspiration for the Canopy Cathedral and the ADA-compliant ramp system of the Lookout Loft — and the shared commitment across both firms to tree-protective engineering and reclaimed materials that defined this landmark horticultural exhibition.

# Nature's Castles: The Treehouse Reimagined at Longwood Gardens (2008)

## Overview

In spring 2008, Longwood Gardens — the renowned horticultural showplace on US Route 1 in Kennett Square, Pennsylvania — opened "Nature's Castles: The Treehouse Reimagined," a landmark exhibition featuring three architecturally ambitious treehouse structures. The exhibit debuted on April 25, 2008, and ran through November 23, 2008, marking a significant departure from the formal garden displays for which Longwood is celebrated. Patricia Evans, speaking on behalf of the gardens, described the treehouses as offering "a little more rustic and relaxed" experience, presenting "a different kind of wow from nature than you get in the designed gardens."

The three structures — the Canopy Cathedral, the Lookout Loft, and the Birdhouse — were designed by two premier American treehouse-building firms, with overall architectural coordination provided by Matthew Millan Architects. Construction ran from February through April 2008, a four-month build that cost approximately $1 million total, plus additional expenses for their specialized foundations. The project celebrated the ancient trees of Longwood's landscape, acknowledging that it was the magnificent specimens themselves that originally inspired Pierre du Pont to establish the gardens in 1906.

---

## The Designers

### Matthew Millan Architects — Project Coordination

Matthew Millan Architects, based in Chadds Ford, Pennsylvania, served as the coordinating architectural firm, working with Longwood Gardens and the two specialist treehouse companies to ensure that each structure responded uniquely to its site. The firm's involvement brought conventional architectural discipline to the project, integrating each treehouse's design with the historic landscape and providing interpretive coherence across all three structures. [Millan Architects' own project pages for all three treehouses](https://millanarchitects.com/portfolio/institutional/natures-castles-the-treehouses-at-longwood-gardens-the-canopy-cathedral/) confirm this coordinating role.

### Treehouse Workshop (Seattle, Washington) — Canopy Cathedral and Birdhouse

The most prominent design partner was **Treehouse Workshop**, a Seattle-based firm founded in 1997 by **Peter Nelson** and **Jake Jacob** (also referenced in some sources as Jake Jacobs). Treehouse Workshop designed and built two of the three structures: the Canopy Cathedral and the Birdhouse.

Peter Nelson had long dreamed of building a treehouse inspired by traditional Scandinavian ecclesiastical architecture. That vision came to life as the Canopy Cathedral. The design process began on a snowy February day when Nelson and his team walked through the gardens, able to see the tall tulip trees clearly without their leaves. Nelson began sketching immediately on site — a process that translated directly into construction drawings. The structure rises two stories, reaching more than 10 feet, and encompasses 589 square feet of main floor space with an attached 118-square-foot balcony. Structurally, the building rests on 37 steel columns, 34 steel beams, and 8 steel braces, all concealed within a timber frame aesthetic. The glazed gable features diamond-patterned windows, and the whole is wrapped by a large balcony carefully notched with indentations to accommodate existing 100-year-old tulip poplars. Materials were chosen with Longwood's sustainability mandate in mind: reclaimed Douglas-fir timbers sourced from salvaged wildfire logs in Oregon, combined with cedar planking from a dismantled toothpaste factory in Toronto, all given an oiled finish.

The Birdhouse, Treehouse Workshop's second contribution to the exhibit, is the tallest of the three structures at more than 20 feet, though also the smallest at 255 square feet. It is nestled in a grove of tulip trees and uses reclaimed wood from an old dairy barn and a chocolate factory. A barn door fitted with a rhododendron branch handle became one of its signature handcrafted details. Both Treehouse Workshop structures reflected the firm's signature approach: marrying artisan craftsmanship, salvaged and natural materials, and deep responsiveness to the specific trees surrounding each site.

### Forever Young Treehouses (Burlington, Vermont) — Lookout Loft

The third treehouse, the Lookout Loft, was designed and built by **Forever Young Treehouses**, based in Burlington, Vermont. This 900-square-foot Adirondack-style structure comprises two separate viewing platforms connected by a walkway, with platforms rising up to 14 feet high. A defining feature of the Lookout Loft was its full accessibility: in compliance with Americans with Disabilities Act standards, Forever Young Treehouses installed more than 75 feet of ramps, making it possible for visitors of all abilities to experience an elevated woodland vantage point. The structure uses hop-hornbeam wood sourced from Vermont alongside ipe wood from Brazil, with elements shaped to resemble natural tree limbs and accented with wrought iron and metal detailing. It overlooks a meadow past the Pierce House.

---

## Construction Philosophy and Technical Innovation

All three treehouses share a critical engineering principle that was non-negotiable for Longwood Gardens: none of the structures are fastened directly to any tree. The gardens insisted on this condition to protect their historic specimens, some a century old. The solution adopted across all three builds was a **pin foundation** system — elevated steel supports consisting of poles resting on small concrete blocks, positioned with great care to avoid root systems and minimize soil compaction. This approach allowed the treehouses to appear intimately nestled among the trees while exerting no physical stress on them. The strategy of using reclaimed and salvaged materials was similarly non-negotiable, in keeping with Longwood's broader sustainability commitments.

The Canopy Cathedral's construction is particularly illustrative of the broader process. After that initial February site walk and sketching session by Peter Nelson, the design moved rapidly through engineering and fabrication. The steel armature — columns, beams, braces — was fabricated off-site and assembled in the garden to form the structural skeleton, onto which the reclaimed timber frame was built. The result is a building that reads as wholly natural in material and spirit while concealing substantial engineered infrastructure.

---

## Contemporaneous Sources

The Lancaster Online article, published in 2008 in connection with the exhibition's opening, provides contemporaneous reporting on the exhibit's opening date (April 25, 2008), the designers, construction timeline, costs, and materials. It confirms that construction began as early as February 2007 for some planning phases, with primary building running February through April 2008, and that the total project cost approximately $1 million. The Washington Post published a feature article dated October 1, 2008 — "Artisan Treehouses Extend Their Lease at Longwood Gardens" — which provides additional contemporaneous coverage of the exhibit during its run. That article corroborates the design inspiration (Norwegian stave churches for the Canopy Cathedral), the site-design process (Peter Nelson sketching on a February site visit), and the foundation engineering solution. Both pieces represent press coverage published within the exhibition year.

---

## Summary

The design and construction of "Nature's Castles: The Treehouse Reimagined" was primarily the work of two specialist firms — **Treehouse Workshop** (Peter Nelson and Jake Jacob, Seattle) for the Canopy Cathedral and Birdhouse, and **Forever Young Treehouses** (Burlington, Vermont) for the Lookout Loft — with **Matthew Millan Architects** serving as coordinating architectural firm. The project cost approximately $1 million, took four months to build (February–April 2008), and was distinguished by its use of pin foundations to protect host trees, extensive use of reclaimed materials, and a design philosophy rooted in site-responsiveness and artisan craft. The Canopy Cathedral's direct inspiration from Norwegian stave church architecture remains the exhibit's most cited design concept.

# Nature's Castles: The Treehouse Reimagined at Longwood Gardens (2008)

## Overview

In the spring of 2008, Longwood Gardens in Kennett Square, Pennsylvania, opened "Nature's Castles: The Treehouse Reimagined," a landmark exhibit featuring three architecturally distinct treehouse structures nestled among the garden's historic trees. The project was conceived to celebrate the great trees that inspired Pierre du Pont's original 1906 land purchase and to deepen visitor connections to the natural forest environment. After approximately four months of construction — running from roughly February through April 2008 — the treehouses opened to the public and quickly became one of Longwood's most celebrated attractions. Combined, the three structures cost approximately $1 million, comparable in expense to building traditional homes of similar square footage.

## The Architect of Record: Matthew Millan Architects

The presiding architectural firm behind the project was **Matthew Millan Architects**, a Pennsylvania-based practice. Matthew Millan served as architect of record for all three structures, working in coordination with structural engineers to ensure the buildings met applicable building codes. Crucially, Millan did not work alone: Longwood Gardens partnered with what contemporaneous reporting described as "the two premier treehouse companies in the U.S." — **TreeHouse Workshop** of Seattle, Washington (founded in 1997 by Pete Nelson and Jake Jacob), and **Forever Young Treehouses** of Burlington, Vermont (a nonprofit founded in 2000 with a mission to create treehouses accessible to children in wheelchairs). Matthew Millan Architects provided the architectural framework and oversight, while these specialized builders contributed their distinctive craft traditions and technical expertise in arboreal construction.

## The Three Treehouses

### 1. The Lookout Loft

The largest of the three at approximately 900 square feet, the Lookout Loft was designed and built by Forever Young Treehouses and stands along Longwood's shady Forest Walk. Its defining design concept is **universal accessibility**: the structure accommodates visitors of all ages and physical abilities through a deliberate layout of ramps and pavilions — 75 feet of ramps in total — rather than conventional stairs. Architecturally, it evokes rustic **Adirondack-style** construction and incorporates four carefully chosen wood species — northern white cedar, hop hornbeam, spruce, and ipe — selected for durability, workability, and aesthetic appeal. The design guides visitors through a progression "from open ramp to more enclosed pavilions," directing attention upward through the forest canopy and outward toward an adjacent eastern farm field. Educational signage throughout helps interpret local forest ecology and wood characteristics, making the structure as much a learning installation as a viewing platform.

### 2. The Birdhouse

Designed and built by TreeHouse Workshop, the Birdhouse is conceived as "an elevated cabin that offers a birds'-eye view of the tree canopies and surrounding landscape." Its primary architectural inspiration came from **steel forest fire towers** — the utilitarian observation structures that once dotted American forests. A winding staircase ascends around a large, living tulip poplar trunk, creating an intentional tension between the visitor's awareness of height and their intimate contact with the tree's bark. The design juxtaposes a living tree with adjacent elements depicting different stages of the tree lifecycle — a hollow snag and a decaying trunk — making ecological storytelling central to the visitor experience. Organic door pulls and live-edge wooden benches reinforce the tactile, nature-immersive character of the structure.

### 3. The Canopy Cathedral

Also designed and built by TreeHouse Workshop, the Canopy Cathedral is the most ornate of the three and draws its architectural language from **Norwegian stave churches** — the medieval timber-frame ecclesiastical buildings of Scandinavia. Situated at a lakeside location within the gardens, the structure features hand-adzed posts supporting a sheltered porch roof, a soaring interior space accessed via paired staircases leading to a wrap-around balcony, and a prominent gable window providing expansive views over Longwood's Large Lake and the Italian Water Gardens beyond. The interior is built of reclaimed Douglas fir timbers and cedar planking finished with oil, producing "a warm, rich glow." With its 589-square-foot interior, the Cathedral was designated as a venue for small group gatherings and events within the gardens.

## Construction Philosophy and Sustainability

One of the most technically consequential decisions in the project was Longwood Gardens' insistence that no structure be physically fastened to the living trees. Rather than using bolts or lag screws that would penetrate and potentially harm the trees, all three treehouses were built on elevated steel supports resting on small concrete blocks arranged in a "pin foundation" — a system carefully positioned to avoid root systems and minimize soil compaction. The poles effectively transfer structural loads to the ground rather than to the trees themselves, preserving the health of the host specimens while maintaining the visual impression of arboreal habitation. In keeping with Longwood's broader sustainability commitments, the structures were built primarily with **salvaged and reclaimed materials**, including the reclaimed Douglas fir prominently used in the Canopy Cathedral.

## Contemporaneous Source

The most substantive contemporaneous journalistic account of the project is an article published in **The Washington Post** on October 2, 2008, titled "Artisan Treehouses Extend Their Lease at Longwood Gardens" (archived at `washingtonpost.com/archive/lifestyle/2008/10/02/`). The article details the roles of Matthew Millan as architect of record, the specific contributions of TreeHouse Workshop and Forever Young Treehouses, the pin-foundation construction method, the cost figure of approximately $1 million, and design particulars for each of the three structures. The article was written at the time the treehouses were being extended beyond their original planned run, indicating they had attracted significant visitor interest following the spring 2008 opening. Additional contemporaneous coverage appeared in *Main Line Today* magazine ("Lofty Digs"), which provided parallel design and construction detail, though that article is no longer openly accessible online.

## Summary

The "Nature's Castles: The Treehouse Reimagined" exhibit was the product of a genuine design collaboration: **Matthew Millan Architects** served as the architect of record and coordinating firm, while **TreeHouse Workshop** (Seattle) built the Birdhouse and the Canopy Cathedral, and **Forever Young Treehouses** (Burlington, VT) built the universally accessible Lookout Loft. Each structure embodied a distinct design concept — Adirondack accessibility, fire-tower verticality, and Norwegian ecclesiastical timber tradition, respectively — unified by shared commitments to sustainability, salvaged materials, tree preservation through the pin-foundation system, and the experiential goal of drawing visitors into deeper relationship with Longwood's forest canopy.

# "Nature's Castles: The Treehouse Reimagined" at Longwood Gardens (2008)

## Overview

In 2008, Longwood Gardens in Kennett Square, Pennsylvania unveiled "Nature's Castles: The Treehouse Reimagined," an exhibition featuring three architecturally distinct treehouse structures set within the gardens' historic woodland landscape. The project grew out of a multi-year Longwood initiative celebrating the estate's remarkable collection of mature trees — the same trees that had originally inspired Pierre du Pont to purchase the initial parcel of land in 1906. After approximately four months of construction, the treehouses opened to the public at a total cost of roughly $1 million.

## The Design Team: A Three-Way Collaboration

The creative team behind the three structures was not a single firm but a deliberate collaboration between an architect and specialized treehouse builders. **Matthew Millan Architects** served as the primary architectural designer for all three treehouses, working with what the firm describes as "the two premier treehouse companies in the U.S." Those companies were identified in contemporaneous coverage as **Peter (Pete) Nelson and Jake Jacobs of TreeHouse Workshop** in Seattle, Washington, who built two of the structures, and **Forever Young Treehouses** of Burlington, Vermont, who constructed the third. This layered arrangement — an architect of record providing design vision, with expert treehouse builders executing the work — reflects both the structural complexity of building in living trees and Longwood's commitment to design excellence.

Pete Nelson, who would later become widely known through the Animal Planet series "Treehouse Masters," recounted that the design process began with a site walk on a snowy February day when the trees were bare of leaves, allowing the team to read the tall tulip poplars clearly. Nelson began sketching on the spot, responding directly to the site conditions rather than imposing a pre-determined form.

## The Three Structures

### 1. The Lookout Loft

The largest of the three at approximately 900 square feet, the Lookout Loft sits along the shady Forest Walk and is explicitly designed for universal accessibility — a notable departure from the typical treehouse's ladder-and-hatch model. Forever Young Treehouses of Burlington, Vermont constructed the structure, installing 75 feet of ramps built to Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA) standards. The design follows a rustic Adirondack idiom, with two separate viewing platforms connected by an elevated walkway. Matthew Millan Architects conceived the progression architecturally: the sequence moves "from open ramp to more enclosed pavilions," gradually drawing visitors' attention from the forest floor upward into the canopy. Wood species were selected with both durability and aesthetic intent in mind — northern white cedar, hop hornbeam, spruce, and ipe feature throughout.

### 2. The Birdhouse

At 255 square feet, the Birdhouse is the smallest of the three. It was designed and built by Peter Nelson and Jake Jacobs of TreeHouse Workshop. Perched to offer a hawk's-eye view of Peirce's Woods, the structure's design concept was drawn from steel forest fire towers — the elevated observation platforms historically used by rangers to spot wildfires in remote forests. A wide spiral staircase winds around a large tulip poplar trunk, reinforcing the experiential relationship between visitor and tree. The Birdhouse incorporates an educational dimension as well: three tree specimens at the site represent different life stages — a healthy mature tree, a standing hollow snag, and a fallen decaying trunk — threading an ecological narrative through the architectural experience. Custom door pulls and live-edge wooden benches encourage tactile engagement with the materials.

### 3. The Canopy Cathedral

The most ornate of the three, the Canopy Cathedral takes formal inspiration from Norwegian stave churches — the distinctive medieval wooden churches of Scandinavia characterized by vertical stave planks, steep pitched roofs, and elaborate carved ornamentation. The structure is centered on a grove of towering tulip poplars and features a 589-square-foot interior with diamond-shaped windows. The floors are crafted from Douglas fir salvaged from a decommissioned toothpaste factory in Toronto — one of several material choices reflecting Longwood's broader sustainability commitments. The exterior employs hand-adzed timber posts and cedar planking with an oiled finish. A large open deck offers lakefront views, and a wraparound balcony is accessed by dual staircases, making the Canopy Cathedral the most ceremonial and spatially generous of the three structures.

## Design Philosophy and Construction Approach

A consistent thread across all three treehouses is Longwood's insistence on sustainability. The structures were built primarily from salvaged and reclaimed materials, and — crucially — none of the treehouses is actually anchored to the trees themselves. All three are instead supported by pipes driven into the ground, a technique that eliminates the structural damage that bolts and through-fasteners would cause to the host trees over time. The trees pass through or beside the structures, appearing to support them visually while remaining physically uncompromised.

Matthew Millan Architects' design philosophy for the project centered on responding to each individual site rather than applying a uniform aesthetic. Each treehouse is formally distinct — Adirondack vernacular for the Lookout Loft, industrial-historic for the Birdhouse, ecclesiastical-Scandinavian for the Canopy Cathedral — yet all three share the material restraint and woodland sensitivity that Longwood required. The firm describes its commitment to "design excellence, collaboration with talented artisans," and a "passion for nature and well-crafted buildings" as animating principles of the project.

## Source Note

The most detailed contemporaneous account of the project appeared in *Main Line Today* magazine (Lofty Digs, 2008), which provided construction details, builder identities, cost figures, and design descriptions drawn from interviews with the project team. The Millan Architects firm portfolio pages for all three structures corroborate the design concepts and confirm the firm's authorship. Nelson Treehouse (formerly TreeHouse Workshop) has also referenced the project on social media, confirming the 2008 construction date and the firm's participation.

# Nature's Castles: The Treehouse Reimagined at Longwood Gardens (2008)

## Background and Opening

In spring 2008, Longwood Gardens — the celebrated horticultural showplace in Kennett Square, Pennsylvania — unveiled "Nature's Castles: The Treehouse Reimagined," an exhibition of three permanent treehouse structures intended to invite visitors into the forest canopy in an entirely new way. The treehouses officially opened to the public in late April 2008, following roughly four months of construction that had begun with site-scouting in February 2007. The project cost approximately $1 million — comparable to building traditional homes — and was conceived as a way to offer, in the words of Longwood's communications manager Patricia Evans, "a different kind of wow from nature than you get" from conventional garden design.

## The Architects and Builders

The exhibition was the product of a three-way creative collaboration. Longwood Gardens engaged **Matthew Millan Architects** as the coordinating architectural firm, working alongside two premier American treehouse-building firms to design structures that each responded individually to their site and offered distinct educational and experiential opportunities.

The two specialist builders were:

- **TreeHouse Workshop** (Seattle, Washington), founded in 1997 by **Pete Nelson and Jake Jacob**, which designed and built two of the three treehouses: The Canopy Cathedral and The Birdhouse.
- **Forever Young Treehouses** (Burlington, Vermont), which designed and built the third structure, The Lookout Loft.

Matthew Millan Architects' role was to provide overall design guidance and ensure that each treehouse cohered within the broader landscape vision of Longwood Gardens while allowing each specialist builder to bring its own craft vocabulary to its individual structure.

## The Three Treehouses

### The Canopy Cathedral

The largest and most architecturally ambitious of the three, The Canopy Cathedral was designed by TreeHouse Workshop and inspired by Norwegian stave churches. Its main floor encompasses 589 square feet, with an additional 118-square-foot wrap-around balcony reached by paired stairs. The structure rises more than 10 feet and is supported by 37 steel columns, 34 beams, and 8 braces — an extensive armature that allowed a substantial enclosed structure without anchoring directly into tree trunks. The interior features hand-adzed timber posts and salvaged Douglas-fir timbers, the latter sourced from two remarkable origins: logs recovered from a 2002 wildfire in Oregon, and timber dismantled from an old toothpaste factory in Toronto. The cedar planking carries an oiled finish that gives the interior what Millan Architects describes as "a warm, rich glow." Positioned near Longwood's Italian Water Garden, the Cathedral offers a panoramic lakefront view through a gable window on the upper level.

### The Birdhouse

Also by TreeHouse Workshop, The Birdhouse is the smallest of the three structures at 255 square feet but the tallest, rising more than 20 feet into a grove of tulip trees. Entirely enclosed, it incorporates reclaimed wood salvaged from a dairy barn and a chocolate factory, giving it a distinctly rustic character. A barn door with a rhododendron-branch handle serves as its entryway — a detail that typifies the naturalistic craft sensibility TreeHouse Workshop brought to both its Longwood commissions.

### The Lookout Loft

Designed and built by Forever Young Treehouses, The Lookout Loft takes an Adirondack vernacular approach and is distinguished above all by its commitment to universal accessibility. More than 75 feet of ramps lead visitors through a series of open and enclosed pavilions to three platforms rising as much as 14 feet above the forest floor — the ramp system built to comply with Americans with Disabilities Act standards, a rare ambition in treehouse design. The progression is intentional: as architect Millan described it, the journey moves visitors "from the forest floor up to the canopy above," with interpretive signage along the way explaining forest ecology and wood species. Materials include northern white cedar, hop hornbeam, spruce, and Brazilian ipe, chosen for durability, workability, and visual richness. The structure overlooks a meadow beyond the historic Pierce House on the Longwood estate.

## Design Philosophy and Construction Method

A unifying principle across all three treehouses was environmental sensitivity. None of the structures was built by bolting directly into living tree trunks, the conventional treehouse approach. Instead, each used pin foundations set around tree clusters, preserving root systems and allowing the trees to continue growing undisturbed. This approach let the structures nestle within canopy groves without compromising the trees' long-term health — an important constraint given Longwood's mission to showcase and protect its historic specimen trees.

Design scouting for the project began as early as February 2007, when Longwood and its design partners walked the estate grounds during a snowstorm to identify appropriate sites. Construction ran from roughly February through April 2008. The total build time of approximately four months for three complex elevated structures required tight coordination between the Burlington and Seattle firms and Millan Architects' oversight team.

## Contemporaneous Source

The Lancaster Online / Intelligencer Journal published a detailed contemporaneous account of the exhibition timed to the April 2008 opening ("Treehouses get the Longwood Gardens treatment," Lancaster Online, April 24, 2008), which describes the three treehouses, the builders, the construction timeline, the $1 million cost, the pin-foundation technique, and the materials. The Washington Post also ran a piece on October 2, 2008, titled "Artisan Treehouses Extend Their Lease at Longwood Gardens," confirming the structures remained on display after an initial planned season — evidence that the exhibition proved sufficiently popular to warrant extended exhibition. Both articles appeared within the same calendar year as the opening and constitute contemporaneous documentation of the project.

## Summary

"Nature's Castles: The Treehouse Reimagined" was the product of a directed collaboration between **Matthew Millan Architects** (coordinating design firm), **TreeHouse Workshop** of Seattle (Canopy Cathedral and Birdhouse), and **Forever Young Treehouses** of Burlington, Vermont (Lookout Loft). The central design concepts were threefold: each structure would respond to its specific grove and site; salvaged and locally appropriate materials would be prioritized; and environmental impact on the host trees would be minimized through pin rather than bolt foundations. Contemporaneous coverage in Lancaster Online (April 2008) and the Washington Post (October 2008) documented both the opening and the extended run of the exhibition.

## SuperMoney: A Lending Comparison Marketplace for Publishers

SuperMoney (supermoney.com) is a financial-product aggregator often compared to Kayak but for money — it lets consumers compare offers across credit cards, personal loans, auto loans, student loan refinancing, and debt consolidation loans simultaneously. Crucially for affiliates, SuperMoney runs a publisher-facing arm called **Monetize powered by SuperMoney**, which gives content sites turnkey tools to embed comparison widgets and loan pre-qualification flows directly on their own pages.

For a publisher like Mesa Group Consulting, the integration looks roughly like this: a visitor lands on a "Best Personal Loans" page, fills out a soft-pull pre-qualification form embedded on Evert's site, and SuperMoney's engine surfaces real-time offers from its network of hundreds of lenders. Evert earns a commission — structured on a **cost-per-lead (CPL) basis**, with payouts reported up to $25 CPA per qualified lead — without needing to originate, underwrite, or service any loan itself. SuperMoney is listed on affiliate networks including FlexOffers under separate programs for personal loans, credit reporting, and investing, indicating multiple commission tracks.

The five SuperMoney-sourced pages on Evert's board map exactly onto SuperMoney's core product verticals:

- **Credit cards** — card comparison with approval odds
- **Personal loans** — pre-qualification marketplace
- **Auto loan refi** — refinancing comparison for existing auto borrowers
- **Student loan refi** — refinancing marketplace for student debt
- **Debt consolidation loans** — personal loan comparison for debt payoff

Each of these is a high-intent search query category ("best personal loan for bad credit," "refinance student loans," etc.) that generates large organic search volumes and converts well on affiliate commission structures. Evert's play is to create SEO-optimized landing pages for each vertical, embed SuperMoney's comparison tools or deep-link to them via Super Links (their turnkey co-branded offer engine), and earn CPL commissions on every lead that completes a pre-qualification. SuperMoney's partners gain access to "50+ finance verticals" and integration options ranging from simple affiliate links to fully embedded marketplaces.

This is a standard performance-marketing model in personal finance publishing. Evert is essentially replicating the playbook of large personal finance sites (NerdWallet, Bankrate, LendingTree) at a smaller, niche-focused scale, with SuperMoney as the back-end product aggregator doing the lender relationship work.

## Novae: A Fintech Platform for Broader Financial Services

Novae (novaemoney.com), founded in 2014 and headquartered in Conyers, Georgia, is a fintech company focused on credit access and financial services for consumers and small businesses, with an explicit emphasis on underserved communities. Unlike SuperMoney, Novae is not a pure comparison marketplace — it combines **proprietary services, third-party partnerships, and a multi-tier affiliate/partner program**.

- **Business funding** — connections to capital programs for small business owners

Evert's five Novae-sourced pages align tightly with these offerings. The distinction from a simple affiliate link is meaningful: Novae's co-brand arrangement lets Mesa Group Consulting present these services as its own offerings, building brand equity rather than sending visitors offsite. This is a deeper integration than the SuperMoney CPL model.

## The Combined Strategy

The two-vendor architecture reflects a logical product split:

| Need | Vendor | Model |
|---|---|---|

SuperMoney captures high-volume, search-driven lending traffic and converts it via soft-pull pre-qualification — fast, transactional, measurable. For Mesa Group Consulting — which already specializes in credit repair and business funding — these two platforms extend the service menu significantly without Evert needing to hire licensed professionals or build proprietary technology.
